cards.front: Under Fourth Amendment abandonment doctrine, what does voluntarily abandoning property do to a person's ability to challenge its later search?
cards.back: It eliminates any reasonable expectation of privacy in the property, so the person has no standing to challenge the search or seizure.

cards.front: Why is abandonment analyzed as a standing question rather than as a search-warrant question?
cards.back: Because if there is no retained reasonable expectation of privacy, the Fourth Amendment is not implicated at all and the defendant lacks standing to object — the search never had to be justified.

cards.front: In Fourth Amendment abandonment analysis, what is the relationship between holding title to property and retaining a Fourth Amendment interest in it?
cards.back: They are independent: it turns on the Fourth Amendment expectation-of-privacy standard, not state property law — a person can keep title yet abandon any Fourth Amendment interest.

cards.front: What is the controlling question for whether property has been abandoned for Fourth Amendment purposes?
cards.back: Whether the person retained an expectation of privacy "that society accepts as objectively reasonable" — the Greenwood/Katz standard.

cards.front: What four factors do courts commonly weigh, as a synthesis of the case law, in the abandonment totality inquiry?
cards.back: (1) denial of ownership, (2) physical relinquishment or discard, (3) the location where the item was left, and (4) intent inferred from conduct.

cards.front: What is the proper status of the abandonment factors (denial, discard, location, intent) in the analysis?
cards.back: They are not a checklist or independent test that must each be satisfied — they are factors bearing on the single ultimate question of whether a reasonable expectation of privacy was retained under Greenwood.

cards.front: What did the Supreme Court hold in California v. Greenwood about garbage left for collection at the curb?
cards.back: There is no reasonable expectation of privacy in garbage bags left for collection at the curb outside the curtilage, so a warrantless search and seizure of curbside trash does not violate the Fourth Amendment.

cards.front: Which Supreme Court case held that police could search opaque garbage bags a suspect left at the curb for the trash collector without a warrant?
cards.back: California v. Greenwood, 486 U.S. 35 (1988).

cards.front: Greenwood's reason curbside trash fails the test: plastic garbage bags left on or at the side of a public street are "readily accessible to {{animals, children, scavengers, snoops, and other members of the public}}."
cards.back: animals, children, scavengers, snoops, and other members of the public

cards.front: What express location limit did Greenwood place on its curbside-trash holding?
cards.back: The bags had to be left for collection "outside the curtilage of a home"; trash still within the curtilage is a different question not covered by Greenwood on its terms.

cards.front: Common pitfall: why is it wrong to treat all of a suspect's trash as fair game under Greenwood?
cards.back: Greenwood authorizes only the curbside bag left for collection outside the curtilage; trash sitting within the curtilage (e.g., a can beside the back door) is not covered.

cards.front: What did Hester v. United States hold about containers a fleeing suspect dropped while running from officers?
cards.back: By dropping the containers in flight the suspect abandoned any Fourth Amendment interest in them, so examining the contents was no seizure in the legal sense (abandonment by flight).
cards.cite: Hester v. United States, 265 U.S. at 58

cards.front: Hester (abandonment by flight): "there was no {{seizure in the sense of the law}} when the officers examined the contents of each after it had been abandoned."
cards.back: seizure in the sense of the law
cards.cite: Hester v. United States, 265 U.S. at 58

cards.front: In which case did the defendant and an associate drop a jug, a jar, and a bottle while fleeing, and the Court treated the contents as abandoned?
cards.back: Hester v. United States, 265 U.S. 57 (1924).

cards.front: What did Abel v. United States hold about items a guest left in a hotel-room wastebasket?
cards.back: Items left in the wastebasket after the guest had paid his bill and vacated the room were abandoned ("bona vacantia"), so the warrantless search and seizure was lawful.
cards.cite: Abel v. United States, 362 U.S. at 241

cards.front: In Abel, what fact marked the line that made the wastebasket items abandoned — mere absence from the room?
cards.back: No — check-out, not mere absence. The guest had "paid his bill and vacated the room," giving the hotel the exclusive right to possession.
cards.cite: Abel v. United States, 362 U.S. at 241

cards.front: In Abel, what two grounds independently justified the warrantless search after the guest checked out?
cards.back: The guest's abandonment of the articles, and the hotel's consent — once he vacated, the hotel had the exclusive right to possession.
cards.cite: Abel v. United States, 362 U.S. at 241

cards.front: What did Bond v. United States hold about a Border Patrol agent's physical squeezing of a bus passenger's carry-on bag?
cards.back: The passenger retained a reasonable expectation of privacy in the bag, and the agent's exploratory physical manipulation (squeezing) was a Fourth Amendment search.
cards.cite: Bond v. United States, 529 U.S. at 335-39

cards.front: Bond on why squeezing the bag was a search: "Physically invasive inspection is simply {{more intrusive than purely visual inspection}}."
cards.back: more intrusive than purely visual inspection
cards.cite: Bond v. United States, 529 U.S. at 337

cards.front: Which case held that an agent's exploratory squeezing of a bus passenger's overhead carry-on luggage was a search because a bailment is not abandonment?
cards.back: Bond v. United States, 529 U.S. 334 (2000).

cards.front: Bailment (in the abandonment context)
cards.back: A temporary transfer of possession — e.g., handing a bag to a carrier, hotel, or friend — that preserves a privacy interest and does NOT abandon it.

cards.front: Distinguish abandonment from bailment under the Fourth Amendment.
cards.back: Abandonment relinquishes both possession and any reasonable expectation of privacy (no standing); a bailment relinquishes only possession temporarily and keeps the privacy interest intact (Bond).

cards.front: Common pitfall: why is giving up possession not the same as giving up privacy?
cards.back: A bailment surrenders possession temporarily but keeps a privacy interest, so it is not abandonment — e.g., a bag handed to a bus or luggage to a hotel (Bond).

cards.front: What did United States v. Matlock hold about consent by a third party with common authority?
cards.back: A third party with common authority over premises or effects may give valid consent to a search against an absent, nonconsenting co-occupant.
cards.cite: United States v. Matlock, 415 U.S. at 171

cards.front: "Common authority" under United States v. Matlock
cards.back: Authority resting on "mutual use of the property by persons generally having joint access or control for most purposes" — not implied from a mere property interest.
cards.cite: United States v. Matlock, 415 U.S. at 171 n.7

cards.front: Under Matlock, what does common authority NOT rest on, and what does it rest on instead?
cards.back: It does not rest on a property interest — common authority "is, of course, not to be implied from the mere property interest a third party has in the property"; it rests on mutual use and joint access or control.
cards.cite: United States v. Matlock, 415 U.S. at 171 n.7

cards.front: How does the common-authority route of Matlock relate to abandonment?
cards.back: It is a separate third-party consent route, not a sub-test of abandonment: where there is no abandonment, a search may still be valid if someone with common authority consents.

cards.front: Distinguish abandonment from consent as Fourth Amendment doctrines.
cards.back: Abandonment means NO reasonable expectation of privacy exists at all, so the defendant lacks standing to object; consent presupposes a privacy interest exists but has been voluntarily waived. Different doctrines, different proof.

cards.front: On the standing throughline, contrast abandonment, bailment, and common-authority consent as to whether a reasonable expectation of privacy survives.
cards.back: Abandonment: no REP survives (no standing). Bailment: REP survives (Bond). Common-authority consent: REP survives but a third party with joint access/control validly waives it (Matlock).

cards.front: What is the companion seizure-timing rule (California v. Hodari D.) for contraband discarded during flight?
cards.back: Contraband a suspect tosses away while fleeing, before submitting to authority, is abandoned and admissible because no seizure of the person has yet occurred.
cards.cite: California v. Hodari D., 499 U.S. at 623, 629

cards.front: How do Hester and Hodari D. fit together on abandonment by flight?
cards.back: Hester supplies the abandonment rule for items dropped in flight; Hodari D. supplies the companion seizure-of-the-person timing — the suspect who "tossed away" crack before submitting had abandoned it.
cards.cite: California v. Hodari D., 499 U.S. at 629

cards.front: Practical-capture maxim: how should an officer frame the question in the field to establish abandonment?
cards.back: Ask whether the suspect has "anything to do with" the item — not merely whether it is "theirs." A clean disclaimer of any connection supports the inference that no expectation of privacy was retained.

cards.front: Why does abandonment doctrine exist — what is its core analytical payoff for the Fourth Amendment?
cards.back: Because privacy, not property, defines the Amendment's reach: once a person no longer holds an objectively reasonable expectation of privacy in an item, the government's examination of it is not a search or seizure at all.

cards.front: Arrest in the home: absent consent or exigent circumstances, what does the Fourth Amendment require before police cross the threshold to make an arrest?
cards.back: A warrant — the home gets the Amendment's highest protection, so police may not cross the threshold to arrest without a warrant unless consent or exigent circumstances apply.

cards.front: Payton v. New York — what is the holding on warrantless entry into a suspect's OWN home for a routine felony arrest?
cards.back: Warrantless, nonconsensual entry into a suspect's own home to make a routine felony arrest is presumptively unreasonable.

cards.front: Payton v. New York — what does an arrest warrant founded on probable cause authorize as to the suspect's OWN dwelling?
cards.back: It implicitly carries the limited authority to enter a dwelling in which the suspect lives when there is reason to believe the suspect is within.

cards.front: Payton v. New York — what TWO predicates must be satisfied for an arrest warrant to authorize entry into the suspect's own home?
cards.back: (1) The suspect lives there, and (2) there is reason to believe the suspect is presently within.

cards.front: Payton's "firm line": "the Fourth Amendment has drawn a {{firm line at the entrance to the house}}. Absent exigent circumstances, that threshold may not reasonably be crossed without a warrant."
cards.back: firm line at the entrance to the house

cards.front: Which case held that the Fourth Amendment draws a "firm line at the entrance to the house" that absent exigency may not be crossed without a warrant?
cards.back: Payton v. New York, 445 U.S. 573 (1980).

cards.front: Payton's "reason to believe the suspect is within" — why is the QUANTUM of proof an open circuit split?
cards.back: Payton never defined the showing; one camp reads it as probable cause (that the suspect resides there and is presently inside), another reads it as a lesser standard closer to reasonable, articulable suspicion. It is unresolved at the Supreme Court.

cards.front: For a multi-jurisdiction audience, which standard should you DEFAULT to for Payton's "reason to believe the suspect is within," and why?
cards.back: Default to the higher (probable-cause) showing — it satisfies every circuit, and the articulation costs nothing.

cards.front: Steagald v. United States — what warrant is needed to arrest the subject of an arrest warrant inside a THIRD party's home?
cards.back: A search warrant (absent exigency or consent) — an arrest warrant alone is not enough.

cards.front: Steagald v. United States — WHOSE Fourth Amendment interest does the search-warrant requirement for a third-party home protect?
cards.back: The third-party homeowner's interest — a distinct, independently protected interest; the arrest warrant protects the named suspect, not the homeowner.

cards.front: Under Steagald, who can and who CANNOT invoke the third-party-home rule to suppress?
cards.back: The homeowner can invoke it; the suspect (a guest) cannot, because the protected interest is the homeowner's, not the suspect's.

cards.front: Distinguish Payton from Steagald: how does the warrant requirement change between the suspect's OWN home and a THIRD party's home?
cards.back: Suspect's own home (Payton): an arrest warrant + reason to believe the suspect is within authorizes entry. Third party's home (Steagald): a search warrant is required, because the homeowner's privacy is a separate interest.

cards.front: Police have a valid arrest warrant for a guest staying in someone else's home and enter the home to arrest him without more — which case identifies the violation?
cards.back: Steagald v. United States — they needed a search warrant for the third party's home.

cards.front: United States v. Santana — what is the Fourth Amendment status of a suspect standing in her own doorway/threshold?
cards.back: She is in a public place, so she cannot defeat a public-place arrest by retreating inside.

cards.front: United States v. Santana — what justifies the warrantless entry when a suspect arrested in public retreats inside?
cards.back: Hot pursuit — an arrest lawfully begun in a public place may continue across the threshold into the home.

cards.front: Santana: "a suspect may not defeat an arrest which has been set in motion in a {{public place}} ... by the expedient of escaping to a private place."
cards.back: public place

cards.front: United States v. Santana — does a hot pursuit have to be a long chase to justify warrantless entry?
cards.back: No — a pursuit that ended almost as soon as it began was still a "hot pursuit" sufficient to justify the warrantless entry.

cards.front: Welsh v. Wisconsin — how does the gravity of the offense affect warrantless home entry under the exigency analysis?
cards.back: Offense gravity is a key exigency factor; warrantless home entry for a minor offense should rarely be sanctioned.

cards.front: Which case found a nighttime warrantless home arrest for a noncriminal/minor traffic offense (nighttime DUI, civil-forfeiture offense) unconstitutional?
cards.back: Welsh v. Wisconsin, 466 U.S. 740 (1984).

cards.front: Welsh: "application of the exigent-circumstances exception in the context of a home entry should {{rarely be sanctioned}} when there is probable cause to believe that only a minor offense ... has been committed."
cards.back: rarely be sanctioned

cards.front: Kentucky v. King — do police forfeit the exigency exception merely because their conduct "created" the exigency?
cards.back: No — they do not forfeit it unless they created the exigency by engaging or threatening to engage in conduct that violates the Fourth Amendment.

cards.front: Kentucky v. King — state the rule verbatim for when the exigent circumstances rule applies despite police involvement.
cards.back: "The exigent circumstances rule applies when the police do not create the exigency by engaging or threatening to engage in conduct that violates the Fourth Amendment."

cards.front: Kentucky v. King — does a lawful knock-and-announce that prompts occupants to destroy evidence disqualify the resulting warrantless entry?
cards.back: No — lawful police conduct that prompts the exigency does not disqualify the entry; only conduct that itself violates (or threatens to violate) the Fourth Amendment does.

cards.front: Lange v. California — does pursuit of a fleeing MISDEMEANOR suspect categorically justify warrantless home entry?
cards.back: No — misdemeanor pursuit does not categorically justify a warrantless home entry; courts make a case-by-case assessment of exigency.

cards.front: Lange: "pursuit of a fleeing misdemeanor suspect does not always—that is, {{categorically}}—justify a warrantless entry into a home."
cards.back: categorically

cards.front: Lange v. California — what analytical approach replaces a categorical rule for a fleeing misdemeanant's flight into a home?
cards.back: A case-by-case assessment of exigency (e.g., escape, evidence destruction, danger).

cards.front: Distinguish Santana from Lange: how does Lange cabin Santana's fleeing-suspect/hot-pursuit language?
cards.back: Santana's broad fleeing-suspect language survives for felonies and genuine exigencies, but after Lange it no longer automatically carries MISDEMEANOR pursuits across the threshold — those are judged case by case.

cards.front: Arrest in the home: name the three ways the page says the exigency excusing a home-entry warrant is BOUNDED, and the case for each.
cards.back: It is weakest for minor offenses (Welsh); it is not forfeited merely because lawful police conduct prompted it (King); and misdemeanor pursuit is judged case by case, not categorically (Lange).

cards.front: Why does Payton v. New York matter — what doctrine does it anchor for arrests in the home?
cards.back: It sets the spine of the rule: it establishes the "firm line" at the home's threshold and defines what an arrest warrant authorizes for the suspect's own dwelling (entry on reason to believe the suspect is within).

cards.front: Common pitfall: officers use a valid arrest warrant to enter a third party's home to grab a guest. Why is that wrong?
cards.back: It is a Steagald violation — you need a search warrant for the home, even with a valid arrest warrant for the guest inside.

cards.front: Common pitfall: reading Santana as a blank check for hot pursuit. What does Lange require instead for a fleeing misdemeanant?
cards.back: Chasing a fleeing misdemeanant into a home is not automatically lawful after Lange — you must articulate the actual exigency (escape, evidence loss, danger); the categorical rule applies to felony arrests and genuine exigencies.

cards.front: Common pitfall: assuming any arrest supports home exigency. What does Welsh say for a minor offense?
cards.back: The opposite — for a minor offense, warrantless home entry should "rarely be sanctioned."

cards.front: Common pitfall: believing a "police-created exigency" always poisons the entry. What does Kentucky v. King actually require for forfeiture?
cards.back: Only if the police created the exigency by conduct that itself violated or threatened to violate the Fourth Amendment; lawful knocking that triggers evidence destruction is fine.

cards.front: Common pitfall: treating Payton's "reason to believe" as a hunch. What should an officer articulate instead?
cards.back: In several circuits it means probable cause that the suspect both lives there and is presently inside — articulate both halves with facts behind each.

cards.front: Minnesota v. Olson — does an overnight guest have any Fourth Amendment privacy interest in the host's home?
cards.back: Yes — an overnight guest has his own reasonable expectation of privacy in the host's home that society is prepared to recognize as reasonable.

cards.front: Olson: "Olson's status as an {{overnight guest}} is alone enough to show that he had an expectation of privacy in the home that society is prepared to recognize as reasonable."
cards.back: overnight guest

cards.front: Minnesota v. Olson — what does the overnight guest's reasonable expectation of privacy give him procedurally when police make a warrantless entry to arrest him?
cards.back: Standing to object — because he has his own protected privacy interest, a warrantless, nonconsensual entry to seize him implicates the home's full Payton protection and he can move to suppress.

cards.front: Which case holds that an overnight guest's status alone establishes a reasonable expectation of privacy in the host's home, giving him Fourth Amendment standing?
cards.back: Minnesota v. Olson, 495 U.S. 91 (1990).

cards.front: Common pitfall: assuming a guest "has no rights" in the host's home. Why is that wrong?
cards.back: Under Olson an overnight guest has his own reasonable expectation of privacy in the host's home — even where the homeowner's right (Steagald) is the headline, the guest may also have a privacy interest and standing of his own.

cards.front: Map the three rights-holders in the arrest-in-the-home scenario — who is protected, and what does each require/give?
cards.back: Payton protects the ARRESTEE in his own home (arrest warrant + reason to believe he is within suffices); Steagald protects the THIRD-PARTY homeowner (police need a search warrant for that home); Olson gives the overnight GUEST his own reasonable expectation of privacy (and standing).

cards.front: In the third-party-home arrest scenario, why can't the named suspect invoke Steagald, yet he can still move to suppress?
cards.back: Steagald protects the homeowner's interest, not the suspect's — but the suspect can invoke his own Olson/Payton privacy interest in the place he is staying, so standing tracks the interest you actually hold.

cards.front: State the automobile exception in one sentence: when may police search a vehicle without a warrant?
cards.back: Where a vehicle is readily mobile and an officer has probable cause to believe it contains contraband or evidence, the Fourth Amendment permits a warrantless search of every part of the vehicle and every container within it where the object of the search may be found, with no separate showing of exigency required.

cards.front: What is the modern two-element automobile-exception test (as circuits restate it)?
cards.back: A warrantless vehicle search is permitted "if (1) the vehicle is readily mobile and (2) law enforcement has probable cause to search it."
cards.cite: United States v. Morley, 99 F.4th 1328, 1338 (11th Cir. 2024)

cards.front: In the two-element automobile-exception test, what is the FIRST element?
cards.back: The vehicle is readily mobile.

cards.front: In the two-element automobile-exception test, what is the SECOND element?
cards.back: Law enforcement has probable cause to believe the vehicle contains contraband or evidence.

cards.front: What are the TWO rationales that justify the automobile exception (Carney)?
cards.back: (1) The vehicle's ready mobility, and (2) a reduced expectation of privacy stemming from its use as a licensed motor vehicle subject to pervasive police regulation.

cards.front: How did Carney articulate the two rationales for the automobile exception (verbatim)?
cards.back: "First, the vehicle is obviously readily mobile by the turn of an ignition key, if not actually moving. Second, there is a reduced expectation of privacy stemming from its use as a licensed motor vehicle subject to a range of police regulation inapplicable to a fixed dwelling."

cards.front: In Carney's two rationales, what is the FIRST justification for the automobile exception?
cards.back: Ready mobility — the vehicle is readily mobile "by the turn of an ignition key, if not actually moving."

cards.front: In Carney's two rationales, what is the SECOND justification for the automobile exception?
cards.back: A reduced expectation of privacy stemming from the vehicle's use as a licensed motor vehicle subject to pervasive police regulation inapplicable to a fixed dwelling.

cards.front: What kind of vehicle did Carney confirm the automobile exception reaches, beyond an ordinary car?
cards.back: A motor home being used as a vehicle (rather than as a fixed dwelling).

cards.front: Which case is the ORIGIN of the automobile exception, and on what rationale did it rest?
cards.back: Carroll v. United States (1925) — a vehicle may be searched warrantless on probable cause because it can be quickly moved out of the jurisdiction before a warrant can be obtained (ready mobility).

cards.front: How did Carroll describe when a warrantless vehicle search is justified (verbatim)?
cards.back: Where "it is not practicable to secure a warrant, because the vehicle can be quickly moved out of the locality or jurisdiction in which the warrant must be sought."
cards.cite: Carroll v. United States, 267 U.S. at 153

cards.front: How far does the search reach once probable cause justifies an automobile-exception search (Ross)?
cards.back: Every part of the vehicle and every container within it that may conceal the object of the search.

cards.front: What is Ross's verbatim statement of the scope of an automobile-exception search?
cards.back: "If probable cause justifies the search of a lawfully stopped vehicle, it justifies the search of every part of the vehicle and its contents that may conceal the object of the search."
cards.cite: United States v. Ross, 456 U.S. at 825

cards.front: Under Ross/Acevedo, what limits the scope of an automobile-exception search?
cards.back: Probable cause defines the scope — it is object-limited. PC to find a large item (e.g., a stolen rifle) does not justify opening a small container (e.g., a jewelry box or pill bottle), and PC as to one container is not PC to dismantle the whole car.
cards.cite: United States v. Ross, 456 U.S. at 825

cards.front: What is Acevedo's "one unified container rule" for the automobile exception?
cards.back: Police may search a container inside a car without a warrant where they have probable cause to believe it holds contraband or evidence — one rule governs all automobile searches.

cards.front: What is Acevedo's verbatim statement of the unified container rule?
cards.back: "We therefore interpret Carroll as providing one rule to govern all automobile searches. The police may search an automobile and the containers within it where they have probable cause to believe contraband or evidence is contained."

cards.front: Which prior decision did Acevedo overrule when it adopted the unified container rule?
cards.back: Arkansas v. Sanders — Acevedo collapsed the old container/vehicle distinction into a single rule.

cards.front: Under Labron, what additional showing beyond ready mobility and probable cause must police make?
cards.back: None — no separate exigency need be shown. Readily mobile plus probable cause permits a warrantless search "without more."

cards.front: What is Labron's verbatim statement that no separate exigency is required?
cards.back: "If a car is readily mobile and probable cause exists to believe it contains contraband, the Fourth Amendment thus permits police to search the vehicle without more."

cards.front: Under Chambers v. Maroney, must an automobile-exception search occur at the roadside?
cards.back: No — where probable cause and mobility existed at the scene, a station-house search after the car is moved is as reasonable as an immediate roadside one.

cards.front: What is Chambers v. Maroney's verbatim rationale for allowing a later station-house search?
cards.back: "[T]here is little to choose in terms of practical consequences between an immediate search without a warrant and the car's immobilization until a warrant is obtained."

cards.front: Which case is the binding SCOTUS anchor for the delayed-search rule, and what delay did it uphold?
cards.back: United States v. Johns (1985) — it upheld a warrantless search of packages lawfully removed from a vehicle that occurred three days after the seizure; immobilization does not end the justification.

cards.front: What is Johns's verbatim holding that delay did not invalidate the search?
cards.back: The search "was not unreasonable merely because the Customs officers returned to Tucson and placed the packages in a DEA warehouse rather than immediately opening them."
cards.cite: United States v. Johns, 469 U.S. at 487

cards.front: Recognize the case: officers lawfully removed packages from vehicles, then searched them three days later in a DEA warehouse; SCOTUS upheld the delayed warrantless search.
cards.back: United States v. Johns, 469 U.S. 478 (1985) — the binding anchor for the delayed-search rule.

cards.front: As a persuasive 4th Circuit illustration of the delayed-search rule, how long a gap did Gastiaburo treat as permissible?
cards.back: 38 days — the Fourth Circuit (persuasive only) held that "the passage of time between the seizure and the search of Gastiaburo's car is legally irrelevant."
cards.cite: United States v. Gastiaburo, 16 F.3d 582, 587 (4th Cir. 1994)

cards.front: What did the persuasive 4th Circuit decision Gastiaburo say about a "temporal limit" to the automobile exception (verbatim)?
cards.back: "Not a single published federal case speaks of a 'temporal limit' to the automobile exception."
cards.cite: United States v. Gastiaburo, 16 F.3d at 587

cards.front: For the delayed-search rule, distinguish the weight of Johns from Gastiaburo.
cards.back: Johns (469 U.S. 478) is the BINDING SCOTUS anchor (three-day delay upheld); Gastiaburo (4th Cir.) is only PERSUASIVE circuit authority, useful as a vivid illustration pushing the point to 38 days. Anchor the rule to Johns, not Gastiaburo.

cards.front: When is a vehicle's "ready mobility" judged, and does later immobilization defeat the exception?
cards.back: Mobility is judged at the scene. Once mobility plus PC existed there, later immobilization or impoundment does not defeat the exception or suddenly require a warrant (Chambers; Johns).

cards.front: Can the automobile exception reach a car parked within a home's curtilage (e.g., a driveway)?
cards.back: No — Collins holds the exception does not permit warrantless entry of a home or its curtilage to search a vehicle parked there.

cards.front: What is Collins v. Virginia's verbatim statement of the curtilage limit?
cards.back: "The automobile exception does not permit the warrantless entry of a home or its curtilage in order to search a vehicle therein."

cards.front: Why doesn't the automobile exception override the curtilage protection in Collins?
cards.back: The exception is about the vehicle, not the constitutionally protected ground it rests on — it reaches the car, not the home or curtilage in which the car sits.

cards.front: Which case holds that PC to search a vehicle justifies searching every part of it AND every container within it?
cards.back: United States v. Ross, 456 U.S. 798 (1982).

cards.front: Which case holds that a station-house search after the car is moved is as reasonable as an immediate roadside search?
cards.back: Chambers v. Maroney, 399 U.S. 42 (1970).

cards.front: Common pitfall: does the fact that something is an "automobile" by itself authorize a warrantless search?
cards.back: No — "automobile" is not a magic word. You still need probable cause. Carney's reduced-privacy rationale lowers the bar but never eliminates the PC requirement.

cards.front: Common pitfall: how do officers wrongly exceed the scope of an automobile-exception search?
cards.back: By searching beyond the object's likely location. PC defines scope (Ross/Acevedo): PC to find a stolen rifle does not justify opening a small jewelry box, and PC as to one closed container is not PC to tear apart the entire car.

cards.front: Common pitfall: must officers search the vehicle immediately, and does impoundment suddenly require a warrant?
cards.back: Neither. Chambers, Johns, and Labron permit a later search; and a warrant is not suddenly required merely because the car is immobilized or impounded — mobility is judged at the scene and the justification persists.

cards.front: Common pitfall: an officer walks onto a home's driveway to reach a car parked there and searches it under the automobile exception. What is wrong?
cards.back: Collins forbids warrantless entry onto the home's curtilage to get to the vehicle. The exception does not authorize stepping onto constitutionally protected ground to reach the car.

cards.front: PITFALL: officers in the field cite United States v. Anchondo (10th Cir. 1998) as automobile-exception authority. Why is that wrong?
cards.back: Anchondo does NOT hold any automobile-exception rule. It is a brief, conclusory persuasive opinion in which the defendant CONCEDED probable cause to search the vehicle, so the exception was never the disputed or decided issue. The cocaine was found on the defendant's body and the search was actually upheld as a search incident to arrest on canine-alert PC. Do not anchor any automobile-exception rule to it.
cards.cite: United States v. Anchondo, 156 F.3d 1043 (10th Cir. 1998)

cards.front: PITFALL detail: what did the defendant concede in Anchondo, and what did that mean for the automobile exception?
cards.back: He conceded vehicle PC — "The defendant admits that the officers had probable cause to search the vehicle." Because vehicle PC was undisputed, the automobile exception itself was never analyzed or decided; the holding rests on search incident to arrest, not the auto exception.
cards.cite: United States v. Anchondo, 156 F.3d at 1045

cards.front: PITFALL detail: what did Anchondo actually hold, and which doctrine supplied the result?
cards.back: It held that "the discovery of cocaine on the defendant's person was the result of a lawful search incident to arrest" (a canine alert supplied PC), and it expressly declined to reach the Terry question. The result rests on search incident to arrest, not the automobile exception.
cards.cite: United States v. Anchondo, 156 F.3d at 1045-46

cards.front: A warrantless vehicle search is permitted if (1) the vehicle is {{c1::readily mobile}} and (2) law enforcement has {{c2::probable cause}} to search it.
cards.back: (1) readily mobile; (2) probable cause — the modern two-element test (Morley, restating settled SCOTUS doctrine).
cards.cite: United States v. Morley, 99 F.4th at 1338

cards.front: Carney's two rationales for the automobile exception are (1) {{c1::ready mobility}} and (2) a {{c2::reduced expectation of privacy}} from the vehicle's pervasive regulation.
cards.back: (1) ready mobility; (2) reduced expectation of privacy from pervasive regulation as a licensed motor vehicle.

cards.front: Why does the automobile exception exist at all — what is the core justification for not requiring a warrant?
cards.back: Because a vehicle's capacity to be quickly moved out of the jurisdiction makes it often impracticable to secure a warrant, and a vehicle carries a reduced expectation of privacy due to pervasive regulation — so the warrant requirement's usual justification does not apply with full force.

cards.front: Under Wyoming v. Houghton, does PC to search a car reach a passenger's belongings, and is a non-suspect passenger's ownership a shield?
cards.back: Yes — with probable cause to search a car, officers may inspect a passenger's belongings found in the car that are capable of concealing the object of the search; a non-suspect passenger's ownership of the purse or bag is no shield.

cards.front: What is Wyoming v. Houghton's verbatim holding on searching passengers' belongings?
cards.back: "We hold that police officers with probable cause to search a car may inspect passengers' belongings found in the car that are capable of concealing the object of the search."

cards.front: Distinction: what does Houghton reach, and what does it NOT reach, as to a passenger?
cards.back: Houghton reaches a passenger's BELONGINGS in the car (a bag or purse capable of concealing the object). It does NOT reach the passenger's PERSON/body — searching the passenger requires its own probable cause or a search incident to arrest.

cards.front: What did Maryland v. Dyson (per curiam) reaffirm about an exigency requirement for the automobile exception?
cards.back: That there is none — the "automobile exception" "has no separate exigency requirement." If a car is readily mobile and probable cause exists, the Fourth Amendment permits a warrantless search without more.

cards.front: What is the verbatim line from Maryland v. Dyson (per curiam) rejecting a separate exigency requirement?
cards.back: "And under our established precedent, the 'automobile exception' has no separate exigency requirement."
cards.cite: Maryland v. Dyson, 527 U.S. at 466-67 (per curiam)

cards.front: Under the border-search exception, what level of suspicion does a ROUTINE search of persons, baggage, or property at the international border require?
cards.back: None — no warrant, no probable cause, and no individualized suspicion. It is a categorical exception to the warrant requirement because the sovereign's self-protective interest is at its zenith at the border.

cards.front: Under the border-search exception, what level of suspicion does a NON-ROUTINE, highly intrusive border intrusion (prolonged detention, body-cavity, alimentary-canal search) require?
cards.back: Reasonable suspicion.

cards.front: In border-search doctrine, what is the 'master line' that determines whether a search needs reasonable suspicion?
cards.back: Routine vs. non-routine. The trigger for the non-routine (reasonable-suspicion) tier is intrusion on the dignity and bodily integrity of the PERSON — not property damage.

cards.front: Where does the border-search exception apply geographically?
cards.back: At the actual international border and its functional equivalents. It is geographic, not portable — it does not float free into the deep interior.

cards.front: Why are the Fourth Amendment's ordinary expectations relaxed at the international border?
cards.back: Because the sovereign's interest in protecting itself is at its zenith there; this inherent self-protective authority justifies the categorical border-search exception.

cards.front: What did *United States v. Ramsey* (1977) hold about routine border searches (here, incoming international mail)?
cards.back: They require neither a warrant nor probable cause; the border-search exception rests on the sovereign's inherent authority to protect itself.

cards.front: Which SCOTUS case upheld a suspicionless search of incoming international mail at the border, grounding the border-search exception in the sovereign's inherent self-protective authority?
cards.back: *United States v. Ramsey*, 431 U.S. 606 (1977).

cards.front: What did *United States v. Montoya de Hernandez* (1985) hold about prolonged detention of a suspected alimentary-canal smuggler at the border?
cards.back: The prolonged detention is reasonable when customs officers have reasonable suspicion that the traveler is smuggling contraband internally — placing it in the non-routine tier.
cards.cite: United States v. Montoya de Hernandez, 473 U.S. 531 (1985)

cards.front: Fill in the *Montoya de Hernandez* standard: detention beyond a routine customs search is justified at its inception if customs agents, considering all the facts surrounding the traveler and her trip, {{c1::reasonably suspect that the traveler is smuggling contraband in her alimentary canal}}.
cards.back: reasonably suspect that the traveler is smuggling contraband in her alimentary canal
cards.cite: United States v. Montoya de Hernandez, 473 U.S. at 541

cards.front: Which SCOTUS case set the floor for the non-routine border tier by approving prolonged detention of a suspected balloon/alimentary-canal smuggler on reasonable suspicion?
cards.back: *United States v. Montoya de Hernandez*, 473 U.S. 531 (1985).
cards.cite: United States v. Montoya de Hernandez, 473 U.S. 531 (1985)

cards.front: What did *United States v. Flores-Montano* (2004) hold about disassembling and reassembling a vehicle's gas tank at the border?
cards.back: The suspicionless authority over vehicles at the border includes disassembling and reassembling a gas tank; this property search is routine and needs no reasonable suspicion.

cards.front: What is the doctrinal significance of *Flores-Montano* (gas-tank disassembly) for the routine/non-routine line?
cards.back: It confirms that dignity-intrusion on the PERSON — not property damage — drives the reasonable-suspicion tier. A destructive property search (disassembling a gas tank) stayed routine and needed no suspicion.

cards.front: Common pitfall: is a destructive property search at the border (like disassembling a gas tank) 'non-routine' because of the property damage?
cards.back: No. Per *Flores-Montano*, property damage does not make a search non-routine; only dignity-intrusion on the person bumps a search into the reasonable-suspicion tier. The gas-tank disassembly stayed routine.

cards.front: What did *Almeida-Sanchez v. United States* (1973) hold about a roving-patrol vehicle search ~20 miles inside the border, without probable cause or consent?
cards.back: It violated the Fourth Amendment. The border-search exception does not reach the deep interior; such a search is not a 'border search.'

cards.front: Fill in *Almeida-Sanchez*: as to the roving-patrol search 20+ miles north of the border, '{{c1::In the absence of probable cause or consent}}, that search violated the petitioner's Fourth Amendment right to be free of "unreasonable searches and seizures."'
cards.back: In the absence of probable cause or consent
cards.cite: Almeida-Sanchez v. United States, 413 U.S. at 273

cards.front: Common pitfall: does the border-search exception authorize a suspicionless roving-patrol search miles inland from the border?
cards.back: No. *Almeida-Sanchez* invalidated a suspicionless roving-patrol search ~20 miles inside the border; interior searches need probable cause, a warrant, or consent.

cards.front: What did *United States v. Brignoni-Ponce* (1975) hold about a roving Border Patrol stopping a vehicle near the border to question occupants?
cards.back: A roving patrol may make a brief stop to question occupants only on reasonable suspicion, based on specific articulable facts, that they are aliens illegally in the country.

cards.front: Per *Brignoni-Ponce*, is apparent Mexican ancestry alone enough to justify a roving-patrol stop near the border?
cards.back: No. Apparent Mexican ancestry ALONE is not enough; reasonable suspicion must rest on specific articulable facts.

cards.front: Distinguish *Brignoni-Ponce* from *Almeida-Sanchez* for roving patrols near the border.
cards.back: *Brignoni-Ponce* allows a brief STOP on reasonable suspicion (ancestry alone insufficient); *Almeida-Sanchez* bars a suspicionless SEARCH in the interior, which needs probable cause or consent.

cards.front: What did *United States v. Martinez-Fuerte* (1976) hold about brief stops at fixed/permanent interior immigration checkpoints?
cards.back: They are constitutional without any individualized suspicion; routine questioning and referral to secondary inspection are reasonable.

cards.front: Are fixed interior immigration checkpoints (*Martinez-Fuerte*) an exercise of the border-SEARCH power?
cards.back: No. Fixed checkpoints (and roving-patrol stops near the border) are seizure / special-needs doctrines, not the border-search power. *Martinez-Fuerte* upholds suspicionless checkpoint STOPS, not suspicionless searches.

cards.front: Common pitfall: can *Martinez-Fuerte* be cited as authority to SEARCH without suspicion away from the border?
cards.back: No. *Martinez-Fuerte* upholds suspicionless checkpoint stops only; it is not authority to search without suspicion, and it is not a border-search holding.

cards.front: Near the border, what is required for (a) a brief checkpoint stop, (b) a roving-patrol stop, and (c) a roving-patrol search in the interior?
cards.back: (a) Brief checkpoint stops are suspicionless (*Martinez-Fuerte*); (b) a roving-patrol stop needs reasonable suspicion (*Brignoni-Ponce*); (c) a roving-patrol search in the interior needs probable cause or consent (*Almeida-Sanchez*).

cards.front: What did the Ninth Circuit hold in *United States v. Cotterman* (2013) (en banc) about forensic examination of a device seized at the border? (persuasive only)
cards.back: A forensic (comprehensive) examination of the device requires reasonable suspicion; it is the comprehensive/intrusive nature of the forensic search, not its location, that triggers the requirement.
cards.cite: United States v. Cotterman, 709 F.3d 952 (9th Cir. 2013) (en banc)

cards.front: Fill in *Cotterman* (9th Cir., persuasive): 'We therefore hold that the forensic examination of Cotterman's computer required a showing of {{c1::reasonable suspicion}}, a modest requirement in light of the Fourth Amendment.'
cards.back: reasonable suspicion
cards.cite: United States v. Cotterman, 709 F.3d at 968

cards.front: What did the Ninth Circuit hold in *United States v. Cano* (2019) about manual vs. forensic phone searches at the border? (persuasive only)
cards.back: A manual phone search needs no suspicion, but a forensic (Cellebrite-type) device search requires reasonable suspicion that the device itself contains contraband — and the border-search exception covers only searches for digital contraband, not evidence of past or future crimes.
cards.cite: United States v. Cano, 934 F.3d 1002 (9th Cir. 2019)

cards.front: How did *Cano* (9th Cir. 2019) confine *Cotterman*'s reasonable-suspicion rule for device searches?
cards.back: It limited the rule to searches for DIGITAL CONTRABAND — the border-search exception covers searches for contraband, not searches for evidence of past or future crimes.
cards.cite: United States v. Cano, 934 F.3d 1002 (9th Cir. 2019)

cards.front: What did the Eleventh Circuit hold in *United States v. Touset* (2018) about forensic searches of electronic devices at the border? (persuasive only)
cards.back: No suspicion at all — not even reasonable suspicion — is required. Devices are property, and reasonable suspicion is reserved for highly intrusive searches of a person's body.
cards.cite: United States v. Touset, 890 F.3d 1227 (11th Cir. 2018)

cards.front: What is the Eleventh Circuit's rationale in *Touset* for requiring no suspicion to forensically search a device at the border?
cards.back: Devices are PROPERTY, and reasonable suspicion is reserved only for intrusive searches of the person's BODY — so a property search of a device falls in the routine tier.
cards.cite: United States v. Touset, 890 F.3d 1227 (11th Cir. 2018)

cards.front: What is the circuit split on FORENSIC searches of electronic devices at the border, and has SCOTUS resolved it?
cards.back: 9th Cir. (*Cotterman*/*Cano*) requires reasonable suspicion; 11th Cir. (*Touset*) requires no suspicion at all. Both are persuasive only outside their circuits, and SCOTUS has not resolved the split — there is no nationwide device rule.

cards.front: Common pitfall: is it correct to state a nationwide rule that 'forensic device searches always need reasonable suspicion' (or 'never do')?
cards.back: No. 'Always need reasonable suspicion' overstates the 9th Cir. (*Cotterman*/*Cano*); 'never do' overstates the 11th Cir. (*Touset*). Label the circuit and flag the unresolved split — there is no nationwide rule.

cards.front: Distinguish how the 9th and 11th Circuits classify a forensic device search within the routine/non-routine framework.
cards.back: The 9th Cir. (*Cotterman*/*Cano*) treats a forensic device search as intrusive enough to require reasonable suspicion; the 11th Cir. (*Touset*) treats the device as property in the routine tier, reserving reasonable suspicion for intrusive searches of the body.

cards.front: Under Brady v. Maryland, what does due process require of the prosecution?
cards.back: To disclose evidence favorable to the accused; suppressing favorable, material evidence violates due process.

cards.front: Per Brady v. Maryland, does the prosecutor's good or bad faith matter to whether suppression violates due process?
cards.back: No. Suppression of favorable, material evidence violates due process "irrespective of the good faith or bad faith of the prosecution" — it is a no-fault duty.

cards.front: Brady: the suppression by the prosecution of evidence favorable to an accused upon request violates due process where the evidence is material either to guilt or to punishment, {{irrespective of the good faith or bad faith of the prosecution}}.
cards.back: irrespective of the good faith or bad faith of the prosecution

cards.front: Brady's original formula said disclosure was owed "upon request." Does the Brady duty still depend on a defense request?
cards.back: No. Later cases dropped the "upon request" qualifier — the duty exists with or without a defense request.

cards.front: Is the Brady disclosure duty limited to exculpatory evidence, or does it also reach impeachment evidence?
cards.back: It reaches impeachment evidence too: "Impeachment evidence, however, as well as exculpatory evidence, falls within the Brady rule."

cards.front: What did Giglio v. United States hold about impeachment evidence under Brady?
cards.back: Impeachment evidence — such as a promise of leniency to a key government witness — is within the Brady rule and must be disclosed.

cards.front: In Giglio, why is nondisclosure of evidence affecting a witness's credibility a due-process problem?
cards.back: Because where "the reliability of a given witness may well be determinative of guilt or innocence," nondisclosure of evidence affecting that witness's credibility falls within the Brady rule.
cards.cite: Giglio v. United States, 405 U.S. at 154 (quoting Napue)

cards.front: What kinds of impeachment material does the Brady/Giglio rule require disclosing about a witness?
cards.back: Promises or deals with witnesses, prior inconsistent statements, bias, mental condition, relevant convictions, and drug or alcohol issues.

cards.front: State the three components of a true Brady violation as set out in Strickler v. Greene.
cards.back: (1) The evidence is favorable (exculpatory or impeaching); (2) it was suppressed by the State (willfully or inadvertently); and (3) prejudice ensued.

cards.front: What is the FIRST element of a Brady violation under Strickler, and what does it include?
cards.back: The evidence must be favorable to the accused — either because it is exculpatory or because it is impeaching.

cards.front: What is the SECOND element of a Brady violation under Strickler, and what mental states does it cover?
cards.back: The evidence must have been suppressed by the State — "either willfully or inadvertently."

cards.front: What is the THIRD element of a Brady violation under Strickler?
cards.back: Prejudice must have ensued (i.e., the suppressed evidence was material).

cards.front: Does an honest, inadvertent oversight excuse a failure to disclose favorable material evidence under Strickler?
cards.back: No. Strickler counts suppression "willfully or inadvertently" — inadvertent non-disclosure is still a violation if the evidence was favorable and material.

cards.front: What is the Brady materiality standard announced in United States v. Bagley?
cards.back: Evidence is material only if there is a "reasonable probability" that, had it been disclosed, the result of the proceeding would have been different.
cards.cite: United States v. Bagley, 473 U.S. at 682

cards.front: Bagley: "A 'reasonable probability' is a probability sufficient to {{undermine confidence in the outcome}}."
cards.back: undermine confidence in the outcome
cards.cite: United States v. Bagley, 473 U.S. at 682

cards.front: In Brady doctrine, how does the "materiality" inquiry relate to the "prejudice" element?
cards.back: They are the same inquiry, not two — materiality is the same idea as the prejudice element.

cards.front: Under Kyles v. Whitley, is Brady materiality judged item-by-item or collectively?
cards.back: Collectively — materiality is defined "in terms of suppressed evidence considered collectively, not item by item."

cards.front: What affirmative duty does Kyles v. Whitley place on the individual prosecutor regarding others on the government's team?
cards.back: A duty to learn of any favorable evidence known to the others acting on the government's behalf, including the police.

cards.front: Kyles: "the individual prosecutor has a duty to learn of any favorable evidence known to the others acting on the government's behalf in the case, {{including the police}}."
cards.back: including the police

cards.front: In Giglio, to whom is one prosecutor's promise to a witness attributed, and why?
cards.back: To the whole Government — "the prosecutor's office is an entity" and is the spokesman for the Government, so a promise by one attorney is attributed to the Government.
cards.cite: Giglio v. United States, 405 U.S. at 154

cards.front: What does Napue v. Illinois hold about the State's use of false testimony?
cards.back: The State may not knowingly use false testimony to obtain a conviction, and the prosecutor must correct testimony he knows to be false.

cards.front: Per Napue, does the bar on knowingly using false testimony stop applying when the false testimony goes only to a witness's credibility?
cards.back: No — the principle "does not cease to apply merely because the false testimony goes only to the credibility of the witness."

cards.front: Distinguish the Napue duty from the Brady duty.
cards.back: Napue = duty not to knowingly use (and to correct) false testimony; Brady = duty to disclose favorable evidence. Related due-process duties, not identical; Giglio sits at their intersection.

cards.front: How does the Kyles imputation rule turn an officer's own credibility history into disclosable material (the "Giglio cop" hook)?
cards.back: Because police-held favorable evidence is imputed to the prosecution, an officer's sustained dishonesty/untruthfulness findings, false reports, false testimony, and certain convictions become Giglio impeachment material that must be disclosed.

cards.front: What does it mean for an officer to be "Giglio impaired," and why does it matter?
cards.back: An officer carrying disclosable credibility findings can be impeached with them in every case, rendering the officer effectively unusable as a credible witness — for officers, integrity is not just ethics, it is admissibility.

cards.front: Is Brady/Giglio a Fourth Amendment search-and-seizure rule?
cards.back: No — it is a disclosure / trial-fairness doctrine grounded in due process; it is not the Fourth Amendment exclusionary rule and should not be conflated with it.

cards.front: What is the analytical sequence for evaluating a potential Brady violation?
cards.back: Is the evidence favorable (exculpatory or impeaching)? Was it suppressed by the State (willful or inadvertent)? Was it material — a reasonable probability of a different result, judged cumulatively?

cards.front: Which case held that a prosecutor's undisclosed promise of leniency to a key government witness was within the Brady disclosure rule?
cards.back: Giglio v. United States, 405 U.S. 150 (1972).

cards.front: Which case established that Brady materiality is judged cumulatively and that the prosecutor must learn of favorable evidence known to the police?
cards.back: Kyles v. Whitley, 514 U.S. 419 (1995).

cards.front: What did the Ninth Circuit do in Benn v. Lambert (a persuasive, illustrative case), and on what basis?
cards.back: Granted habeas relief because the State suppressed both material exculpatory evidence and material impeachment evidence — each independently sufficient under Brady.

cards.front: What precedential weight does Benn v. Lambert carry in this doctrine, and what is the binding spine?
cards.back: Benn (9th Cir.) is persuasive/illustrative only — do not anchor doctrine to it; the binding spine is the SCOTUS line (Brady, Giglio, Bagley, Kyles, Strickler, Napue).

cards.front: In Benn v. Lambert, what were the two suppressed categories of evidence that each independently warranted relief?
cards.back: An exculpatory failure (fire-cause expert evidence) and an impeachment failure (an informant's ongoing drug use) — each independently material.

cards.front: Common pitfall: an officer thinks "the prosecutor never asked, so I'm clear" about favorable evidence. Why is that wrong?
cards.back: Kyles imputes police-held favorable evidence to the prosecution; officers must affirmatively surface favorable or impeachment material — including their own credibility issues — not assume it stays buried.

cards.front: What did United States v. Agurs hold about the prosecutor's disclosure duty when the defense makes no request?
cards.back: The disclosure duty exists even when the defense makes no request — it does not depend on a defense demand for the evidence.

cards.front: Agurs: if the evidence is so clearly supportive of a claim of innocence that it gives the prosecution notice of a duty to produce, that duty should equally arise {{even if no request is made}}.
cards.back: even if no request is made
cards.cite: United States v. Agurs, 427 U.S. at 107

cards.front: What did Smith v. Cain hold about undisclosed impeachment of the sole eyewitness?
cards.back: Undisclosed impeachment of the sole eyewitness was material under Brady — the conviction was reversed.

cards.front: In Smith v. Cain, why were the witness's undisclosed statements "plainly material"?
cards.back: Because that witness's "testimony was the only evidence linking Smith to the crime," the undisclosed statements were "plainly material."

cards.front: What did Turner v. United States hold, and why is it the Brady "counterweight" case?
cards.back: Materiality is demanding and judged on the whole record; the suppressed evidence here was immaterial — no reasonable probability of a different result — so there was no Brady violation.

cards.front: In Turner v. United States, what did the Court conclude about whether the withheld evidence would have changed the outcome?
cards.back: It found "not a 'reasonable probability' that the withheld evidence would have changed the outcome of petitioners' trial" — so the suppression was immaterial.
cards.cite: Turner v. United States, 582 U.S. at 328-29

cards.front: Distinguish Smith v. Cain from Turner v. United States on materiality.
cards.back: Smith: impeachment of the sole eyewitness — the only evidence linking the defendant — was plainly material, so reversal. Turner: on the whole record there was no reasonable probability of a different result, so the suppression was immaterial and there was no violation.

cards.front: What did Wearry v. Cain (per curiam) reaffirm about how Brady materiality is assessed?
cards.back: It reaffirmed cumulative materiality: the suppressed evidence assessed collectively undermined confidence in the verdict, establishing a due-process violation (reversed).

cards.front: Wearry (per curiam): "He must show only that the new evidence is sufficient to {{undermine confidence}} in the verdict."
cards.back: undermine confidence

cards.front: Does a single Brady violation by a prosecutor establish municipal §1983 failure-to-train (Monell) liability under Connick v. Thompson (a CIVIL holding)?
cards.back: No. A single Brady violation does not establish failure-to-train liability; a pattern of similar constitutional violations is ordinarily necessary to show deliberate indifference. This is the civil boundary, distinct from the criminal due-process duty.

cards.front: In Connick v. Thompson, what does the Court say is "ordinarily necessary" to demonstrate deliberate indifference for failure-to-train §1983 liability?
cards.back: "A pattern of similar constitutional violations by untrained employees is 'ordinarily necessary' to demonstrate deliberate indifference for purposes of failure to train."

cards.front: Why is "no one told me" no defense to a Brady violation when the police, not the prosecutor, held the favorable evidence?
cards.back: The government is treated as one team: Giglio makes the prosecutor's office an entity whose knowledge is imputed across it, and Kyles charges the individual prosecutor with knowledge of favorable evidence held by the police — so the prosecution cannot disclaim what its members know.
cards.cite: Kyles v. Whitley, 514 U.S. at 437; Giglio v. United States, 405 U.S. at 154

cards.front: Does complying with criminal-discovery rules (Fed. R. Crim. P. 16, the Jencks Act) satisfy the Brady duty?
cards.back: No. Brady is a separate constitutional due-process obligation. Rule 16 compliance does not discharge it — favorable evidence may fall outside Rule 16's enumerated categories, and the constitutional duty can run on a different (often earlier) timeline.

cards.front: What does 42 U.S.C. § 1983 create, and against whom?
cards.back: A private civil action for damages or injunctive relief against any person who, under color of state law, deprives someone of a federal constitutional right.

cards.front: Under § 1983, deprivation of WHAT is actionable — rights or mere injuries?
cards.back: Rights. § 1983 protects rights, privileges, or immunities secured by the Constitution and federal law — not mere injuries.

cards.front: What does 42 U.S.C. § 1988(b) authorize in a § 1983 action?
cards.back: The court, in its discretion, may award reasonable attorney's fees to the prevailing party.

cards.front: Under § 1988, who ultimately bears a prevailing civil-rights plaintiff's attorney's fees when the defendant is a government?
cards.back: The public fisc — the fees are taxpayer-funded.

cards.front: What does 18 U.S.C. § 242 make a federal crime?
cards.back: Willfully depriving a person of constitutional or federal rights under color of law — the criminal analog to § 1983.

cards.front: Why are 18 U.S.C. § 242 indictments reserved in practice for the most egregious violations?
cards.back: Because the government must prove the officer acted willfully (specific intent), a steep burden proven beyond a reasonable doubt.

cards.front: DISTINCTION: How does § 1983 (civil) differ from 18 U.S.C. § 242 (criminal) on burden and intent?
cards.back: § 1983 = civil damages, proven by a preponderance, with no willfulness element. § 242 = criminal prosecution, proven beyond a reasonable doubt, requiring a willful (specific-intent) deprivation.

cards.front: Which Supreme Court case construed the "willfully" requirement of 18 U.S.C. § 242?
cards.back: Screws v. United States, 325 U.S. 91 (1945).

cards.front: What did Monroe v. Pape (1961) hold about the reach of "under color of" state law?
cards.back: "Under color of" law reaches an officer's misuse of state-conferred authority — even conduct that state law itself forbids.

cards.front: Monroe v. Pape (quoting Classic): "{{Misuse of power}}, possessed by virtue of state law and made possible only because the wrongdoer is clothed with the authority of state law, is action taken 'under color of' state law."
cards.back: Misuse of power

cards.front: Under Monroe v. Pape, can an officer who acts illegally still be acting "under color of" state law?
cards.back: Yes. The badge — authority possessed by virtue of state law — is what makes the deprivation actionable, even when the conduct violates state law.

cards.front: Which Monroe v. Pape holding was later overruled, and by what case?
cards.back: Monroe's holding that municipalities are not "persons" under § 1983 was overruled by Monell v. Department of Social Services (1978).

cards.front: What did Monell v. Department of Social Services (1978) hold about municipalities under § 1983?
cards.back: Municipalities ARE "persons" suable under § 1983, but only when an official policy or custom causes the constitutional injury.
cards.cite: Monell v. Department of Social Services, 436 U.S. 658 (1978)

cards.front: Can a municipality be held liable under § 1983 on a respondeat superior theory (Monell)?
cards.back: No. "[A] municipality cannot be held liable under § 1983 on a respondeat superior theory" — it is not liable merely for employing a tortfeasor.
cards.cite: Monell v. Dep't of Soc. Servs., 436 U.S. at 691

cards.front: Under Monell, what must a plaintiff identify to reach the municipality itself under § 1983?
cards.back: A policy, custom, or final-policymaker decision whose execution inflicts the injury — not merely a single bad-acting employee.
cards.cite: Monell v. Dep't of Soc. Servs., 436 U.S. at 694

cards.front: PITFALL: Why does suing the city "because it employs the wrongdoer" fail under § 1983?
cards.back: Monell forecloses respondeat superior — you must point to a policy or custom that caused the injury, not just a bad employee.
cards.cite: Monell v. Department of Social Services, 436 U.S. 658 (1978)

cards.front: How did Harlow v. Fitzgerald (1982) reformulate qualified immunity — objective or subjective?
cards.back: As a purely objective test: officials are shielded unless their conduct violated clearly established law; it abandoned the old subjective good-faith/malice prong.

cards.front: Harlow: officials "are shielded from liability for civil damages insofar as their conduct does not violate {{clearly established}} statutory or constitutional rights of which a reasonable person would have known."
cards.back: clearly established

cards.front: Under Harlow's objective QI test, what is the real question — whether the officer meant well, or something else?
cards.back: Not whether the officer meant well; whether the law was clearly established at the time the officer acted.

cards.front: Why did Harlow drop qualified immunity's subjective good-faith prong?
cards.back: Because the subjective component was incompatible with resolving insubstantial claims on summary judgment; an objective standard permits early disposition.

cards.front: What two-step qualified-immunity sequence did Saucier v. Katz (2001) establish?
cards.back: (1) Do the facts, viewed most favorably to the plaintiff, show a constitutional violation? and (2) was that right clearly established at the time, judged at a specific level?

cards.front: What is the FIRST prong of the Saucier qualified-immunity analysis?
cards.back: Taken in the light most favorable to the plaintiff, do the facts alleged show the officer's conduct violated a constitutional right?

cards.front: What is the SECOND prong of the Saucier qualified-immunity analysis, and at what level is it judged?
cards.back: Whether the right was clearly established at the time — and it must be considered at a specific (not general) level.

cards.front: What did Pearson v. Callahan (2009) change about the Saucier two-step sequence?
cards.back: It made the sequence discretionary — courts may decide either prong first; the Saucier order "should no longer be regarded as mandatory."

cards.front: Pearson v. Callahan: "while the sequence set forth there is often appropriate, it should no longer be regarded as {{mandatory}}."
cards.back: mandatory

cards.front: After Pearson, what can happen to the underlying constitutional question when a court grants QI on "not clearly established"?
cards.back: The court can grant immunity without ever deciding whether a right was violated, leaving the underlying constitutional question unresolved.

cards.front: DISTINCTION: How do Saucier and Pearson differ on the ORDER of the QI prongs?
cards.back: Saucier made the two-step order mandatory (violation first); Pearson made the sequence discretionary, letting courts decide either prong first.

cards.front: DISTINCTION: How does qualified immunity differ from the Leon good-faith exception?
cards.back: Qualified immunity is a civil defense to § 1983 damages. The Leon good-faith exception is a separate criminal-side doctrine about whether evidence is suppressed under the exclusionary rule.

cards.front: PITFALL: The maxim "no good-faith exception to a warrantless search — it's objective reasonableness" refers to which doctrine, and which doctrine should it NOT be merged with?
cards.back: It refers to Harlow's objective qualified-immunity standard; do not merge it with Leon's good-faith exception to the exclusionary rule.

cards.front: PITFALL: Does qualified immunity affect whether evidence is suppressed in a criminal trial?
cards.back: No. QI never determines whether evidence comes in or out of a criminal trial — it only limits civil damages.

cards.front: Under Graham v. Connor (1989), by what standard are excessive-force § 1983 seizure claims judged?
cards.back: The Fourth Amendment's "objective reasonableness" standard — subjective "malice" and "sadism" have no proper place in the inquiry.

cards.front: How do the QI side (Harlow) and the merits side (Graham) relate on the role of subjective intent?
cards.back: They march in lockstep: objective reasonableness governs both, and the officer's subjective good or bad faith is irrelevant on each side.

cards.front: PITFALL: Post-Harlow/Graham, does a pure good-faith motive save objectively unreasonable conduct (or a bad motive defeat objectively reasonable conduct)?
cards.back: No. A good-faith motive does not save objectively unreasonable conduct, and a bad motive does not defeat objectively reasonable conduct.

cards.front: At what level of generality must "clearly established" law be defined for the QI second prong, and why does it matter?
cards.back: At a specific, particularized level. Defining the right too generally (e.g., "the right to be free from excessive force") rarely overcomes QI; concrete, articulated facts do.

cards.front: What weight does Wright v. City of Euclid (6th Cir. 2020) carry on the QI standard?
cards.back: Persuasive only — one circuit, illustrative. The binding spine is Harlow/Saucier/Pearson (SCOTUS); do not anchor the QI standard to Wright.
cards.cite: Wright v. City of Euclid, 962 F.3d 852 (6th Cir. 2020)

cards.front: What did the Sixth Circuit do with qualified immunity in Wright v. City of Euclid (persuasive)?
cards.back: It reversed the grant of QI on Fourth Amendment claims — excessive force, false arrest, extended detention, and malicious prosecution — finding a jury could find clearly-established violations.
cards.cite: Wright v. City of Euclid, 962 F.3d 852 (6th Cir. 2020)

cards.front: What concrete, date-anchored conduct did Wright v. City of Euclid (persuasive) hold was clearly established as excessive force?
cards.back: Drawing a weapon on a suspect who was not fleeing or posing a safety risk, and tasering a suspect who was not actively resisting arrest (clearly established as of Nov. 4, 2016).
cards.cite: Wright v. City of Euclid, 962 F.3d at 868

cards.front: What does Wright v. City of Euclid (persuasive) illustrate about whether QI is an absolute shield?
cards.back: QI is a meaningful but not absolute shield — where a reasonable jury could find a clearly-established Fourth Amendment violation, immunity is denied.
cards.cite: Wright v. City of Euclid, 962 F.3d at 868, 874

cards.front: Which case made § 1983 operative against individual officers, holding that misuse of state-conferred authority is action "under color of" law and requiring no exhaustion of state remedies?
cards.back: Monroe v. Pape, 365 U.S. 167 (1961).

cards.front: Which case holds that a municipality is liable under § 1983 only for injuries caused by an official policy or custom, with no respondeat superior?
cards.back: Monell v. Department of Social Services, 436 U.S. 658 (1978).
cards.cite: Monell v. Department of Social Services, 436 U.S. 658 (1978)

cards.front: DISTINCTION: What is the principal § 1983 hurdle for suing an individual officer versus suing the agency?
cards.back: Suing the individual officer: overcome qualified immunity (clearly-established law). Suing the agency: prove a Monell policy or custom (no respondeat superior).

cards.front: Under Ashcroft v. al-Kidd (2011), when is a right "clearly established" for qualified immunity?
cards.back: Only when existing precedent placed the question "beyond debate" — clearly established law is not defined at a high level of generality.

cards.front: al-Kidd: "We do not require a case directly on point, but existing precedent must have placed the statutory or constitutional question {{beyond debate}}."
cards.back: beyond debate

cards.front: What is the dispositive "clearly established" question under Mullenix v. Luna (2015) (per curiam)?
cards.back: Whether the violative nature of the particular conduct is clearly established — the inquiry must be particularized to the specific context, not framed generally.

cards.front: Mullenix v. Luna (per curiam): "The dispositive question is 'whether the {{violative nature of particular conduct}} is clearly established.'"
cards.back: violative nature of particular conduct

cards.front: For the "clearly established" standard, what does District of Columbia v. Wesby (2018) require of the legal principle?
cards.back: It must be "settled law" — a sufficiently clear foundation in then-existing precedent that places the question "beyond debate." (Cite Wesby for the clearly-established standard, not its probable-cause holding.)
cards.cite: District of Columbia v. Wesby, 583 U.S. 48, 63 (2018)

cards.front: Wesby (clearly-established standard): "To be clearly established, a legal principle must have a sufficiently clear foundation in then-existing precedent. The rule must be '{{settled law}}.'"
cards.back: settled law
cards.cite: District of Columbia v. Wesby, 583 U.S. at 63

cards.front: Under Rivas-Villegas v. Cortesluna (2021) (per curiam), what must a § 1983 plaintiff identify to show a clearly-established violation?
cards.back: A case that put the officer on notice that his specific conduct was unlawful — the inquiry is undertaken in light of the specific context, not as a broad general proposition.

cards.front: Rivas-Villegas v. Cortesluna (per curiam): the plaintiff "must identify a case that put [the officer] on notice that his {{specific conduct}} was unlawful."
cards.back: specific conduct

cards.front: What does City of Tahlequah v. Bond (2021) (per curiam) warn courts not to do, and whom does QI protect?
cards.back: Courts must not define clearly established law "at too high a level of generality"; qualified immunity protects "all but the plainly incompetent or those who knowingly violate the law."
cards.cite: City of Tahlequah v. Bond, 595 U.S. 9, 11-12 (2021) (per curiam)

cards.front: Tahlequah v. Bond (per curiam): qualified immunity protects "'all but the {{plainly incompetent}} or those who knowingly violate the law.'"
cards.back: plainly incompetent
cards.cite: City of Tahlequah v. Bond, 595 U.S. at 11-12

cards.front: What does Hope v. Pelzer (2002) establish as the counterweight to the QI specificity requirement?
cards.back: A right can be clearly established without a factually identical case — in an "obvious case," officials have fair warning that their conduct is unlawful even in novel factual circumstances.

cards.front: Hope v. Pelzer: "officials can still be on notice that their conduct violates established law even in {{novel factual circumstances}}."
cards.back: novel factual circumstances

cards.front: DISTINCTION: Read together, how do al-Kidd and Hope v. Pelzer define what defeats qualified immunity?
cards.back: Either particularized precedent placing the question "beyond debate" (al-Kidd) OR an "obvious case" giving fair warning without a case on point (Hope) will defeat QI.

cards.front: What did Connick v. Thompson (2011) hold about Monell failure-to-train liability and a single Brady violation?
cards.back: A single Brady violation cannot support failure-to-train liability; a pattern of similar constitutional violations is "ordinarily necessary" to show deliberate indifference.

cards.front: Connick v. Thompson: "A pattern of similar constitutional violations by untrained employees is '{{ordinarily necessary}}' to demonstrate deliberate indifference for purposes of failure to train."
cards.back: ordinarily necessary

cards.front: PITFALL: Why does treating a single bad incident as Monell failure-to-train fail under Connick v. Thompson?
cards.back: Connick requires a pattern of similar violations to show deliberate indifference; one incident almost never suffices to reach the municipality on a failure-to-train theory.

cards.front: What did Bivens v. Six Unknown Named Agents (1971) recognize, and how does it relate to § 1983?
cards.back: An implied damages remedy against federal officers for Fourth Amendment violations — the federal-officer analog to § 1983 (which reaches only action under color of state law).
cards.cite: Bivens v. Six Unknown Named Agents, 403 U.S. 388 (1971)

cards.front: How did Egbert v. Boule (2022) cabin the Bivens remedy?
cards.back: If there is even a single reason to pause before applying Bivens in a new context, a court may not recognize a Bivens remedy — the question often resolves to whether Congress might be better equipped to create the remedy.

cards.front: Egbert v. Boule: "If there is even a single 'reason to pause before applying Bivens in a new context,' a court may {{not}} recognize a Bivens remedy."
cards.back: not

cards.front: DISTINCTION: § 1983 versus Bivens — which actors does each reach?
cards.back: § 1983 reaches state and local actors (action under color of state law). Bivens is the analog for federal officers — but it is now sharply cabined (Egbert) and nearly foreclosed outside its original Fourth Amendment context.

cards.front: PITFALL: Can you sue a federal officer (DEA, FBI, CBP) under § 1983?
cards.back: No — § 1983 reaches only action under color of state law. A federal officer is reached, if at all, via Bivens, now nearly foreclosed outside its original Fourth Amendment context (Egbert).

cards.front: When does Heck v. Humphrey (1994) bar a § 1983 damages action?
cards.back: When a judgment for the plaintiff would necessarily imply the invalidity of an outstanding conviction or sentence — the suit is not cognizable unless that conviction has already been invalidated (the favorable-termination bar).

cards.front: Under Heck v. Humphrey, how must the underlying conviction be invalidated before the § 1983 action can proceed?
cards.back: The plaintiff must prove the conviction or sentence was reversed on direct appeal, expunged by executive order, declared invalid by an authorized state tribunal, or called into question by a federal writ of habeas corpus.

cards.front: PITFALL: May a defendant convicted on challenged evidence file a parallel § 1983 suit attacking that same search while the conviction stands?
cards.back: Generally no. Heck bars the suit if success would necessarily imply the invalidity of the still-standing conviction.

cards.topic: Collective Knowledge and the Fellow-Officer Rule
cards.front: Under the collective-knowledge (fellow-officer) doctrine, what may be imputed from one officer to another?
cards.back: The probable cause or reasonable suspicion held by one officer can be imputed to another who acts at his direction; the acting officer need not personally possess all the underlying facts.

cards.topic: Collective Knowledge and the Fellow-Officer Rule
cards.front: Collective-knowledge doctrine: what is presumed about an officer who acts in objective reliance on a bulletin, flyer, or radio dispatch?
cards.back: He is presumed to be acting on the requisite quantum of probable cause or reasonable suspicion, and need not personally know all the facts underlying the directive.

cards.topic: Collective Knowledge and the Fellow-Officer Rule
cards.front: Does the collective-knowledge doctrine ever create probable cause or reasonable suspicion?
