cards.back: A knock and talk is a lawful approach, not a lawful search. By itself it authorizes no search; any entry or search still needs valid consent or an independent warrant exception.

cards.front: When officers use the knock-and-talk to ask for consent to search, what body of law governs what they obtain?
cards.back: Ordinary consent-search law governs. The knock and talk just gets officers lawfully to the door; voluntariness of any consent is judged under standard consent doctrine.

cards.front: The knock-and-talk approach becomes a Fourth Amendment search the moment officers exceed the implied license in {{c1::scope}}, {{c2::time}}, or {{c3::manner}}.
cards.back: Scope, time, or manner. Examples: straying off the normal route, lingering after being told to go, or bringing investigative tools onto the curtilage.

cards.front: Under Florida v. Jardines, what two dimensions limit the implied license to approach a home?
cards.back: Physical area (the customary path to the front door) and purpose (a brief, ordinary visitor's errand).

cards.front: What did the Supreme Court hold in Florida v. Jardines (2013)?
cards.back: Walking a drug dog onto the home's front porch to investigate exceeded the implied license to approach and knock — a trespassory search of the curtilage.

cards.front: Which case held that bringing a drug-detection dog onto a home's front porch to investigate was a trespassory search exceeding the implied license?
cards.back: Florida v. Jardines, 569 U.S. 1 (2013).

cards.front: On what Fourth Amendment theory did Jardines find a search — privacy or property?
cards.back: The Jones property-trespass theory: officers physically intruded on the curtilage beyond what the implied license authorized, so it was a search without needing a privacy analysis.

cards.front: Under Kentucky v. King (2011), may police lawfully knock and announce their presence at a door?
cards.back: Yes. Police may lawfully knock and announce their presence; doing so does not create an unusable (manufactured) exigency.

cards.front: Under Kentucky v. King, when is a police-created exigency unusable to justify a warrantless entry?
cards.back: Only when police create the exigency by engaging or threatening to engage in conduct that itself violates the Fourth Amendment.

cards.front: Quote the Kentucky v. King formulation of when the exigent-circumstances rule applies despite police involvement.
cards.back: "The exigent circumstances rule applies when the police do not create the exigency by engaging or threatening to engage in conduct that violates the Fourth Amendment."

cards.front: Which case establishes that merely knocking and announcing is fine, and that a police-created exigency is unusable only when the police's conduct itself violates the Fourth Amendment?
cards.back: Kentucky v. King, 563 U.S. 452 (2011).

cards.front: Under Florida v. Bostick, what is the test for whether an encounter stays consensual?
cards.back: Whether a reasonable person would feel free to decline the officers' requests or otherwise terminate the encounter.

cards.front: Which case supplies the "free to decline the requests or terminate the encounter" standard for consensual encounters?
cards.back: Florida v. Bostick, 501 U.S. 429 (1991).

cards.front: What did United States v. Drayton (2002) hold about advising a person of the right to refuse consent?
cards.back: Consent can be voluntary even though officers never advised the person of the right to refuse; there is no per se warning requirement.

cards.front: Which case holds there is no per se requirement to warn a person of the right to refuse before consent can be voluntary?
cards.back: United States v. Drayton, 536 U.S. 194 (2002).

cards.front: What is the combined Bostick/Drayton rule for keeping a knock-and-talk consensual?
cards.back: The encounter stays consensual if a reasonable person would feel free to decline the requests or terminate the encounter (Bostick), and officers need not advise the resident of the right to refuse (Drayton).

cards.front: What is the precedential weight of French v. Merrill (1st Cir. 2021) on knock-and-talk limits, and how should it be labeled?
cards.back: Persuasive only — a First Circuit marker, not a nationwide rule. Lower courts vary on repeated knocks, back-door approaches, and pre-dawn visits.

cards.front: In French v. Merrill (1st Cir. 2021), what officer conduct exceeded the implied license to enter the curtilage?
cards.back: Repeated pre-dawn visits, knocking on a bedroom window, and peering in through a window covering with a flashlight — intrusive, contentious conduct no reasonable visitor would understand as authorized.

cards.front: How did the First Circuit in French v. Merrill describe the officers' conduct relative to the curtilage license?
cards.back: Their conduct "plainly exceeded the scope of the implied license to enter the curtilage of French's home."
cards.cite: French v. Merrill, 15 F.4th 116 (1st Cir. 2021) (slip op. at 43)

cards.front: How did French v. Merrill tie the officers' conduct to Jardines for qualified-immunity purposes?
cards.back: The court held the officers, like those in Jardines, physically intruded on the property without license in a way no reasonable visitor could understand as authorized — making the violation clearly established and defeating qualified immunity.
cards.cite: French v. Merrill, 15 F.4th 116 (1st Cir. 2021) (slip op. at 40)

cards.front: Distinguish: what is within the implied license at the front door vs. what breaks it (per Jardines)?
cards.back: Within: walking the customary front-door path, knocking, and waiting briefly for an ordinary errand. Breaks it: deploying a drug dog on the porch, cutting to a back door or yard, or lingering for an investigative purpose.

cards.front: Distinguish lawful knocking from manufacturing exigency under Kentucky v. King.
cards.back: Lawfully knocking and announcing is permitted and does not taint a resulting emergency. Banging while threatening to break in — i.e., threatening conduct that itself violates the Fourth Amendment — manufactures an exigency that cannot justify a warrantless entry.

cards.front: Common pitfall: officers treat the knock and talk as its own warrant exception. Why is that wrong?
cards.back: It is not an exception. Without consent or another independent exception, nothing inside the home becomes fair game; the doctrine only authorizes a lawful approach.

cards.front: Common pitfall: ignoring curtilage / implied-license limits during a knock and talk. What breaks the license?
cards.back: Cutting to a back door, lingering in the yard, or bringing a dog onto the porch — anything beyond the front-door path for a brief errand (Jardines).

cards.front: Common pitfall: what happens when officers overstay a resident's refusal during a knock and talk?
cards.back: Once a resident declines or asks officers to leave, continued or repeated intrusion converts the encounter into a search — as the First Circuit illustrated in French v. Merrill.

cards.front: Common pitfall: using the knock to manufacture exigency. What conduct taints any resulting "emergency"?
cards.back: Banging while shouting threats to break in, or otherwise threatening a Fourth Amendment violation, taints the emergency and cannot justify a warrantless entry (King).

cards.front: Why does the knock-and-talk doctrine exist — what legal fact makes the approach lawful without a warrant?
cards.back: Because any visitor has an implied license to approach the front door, knock, wait briefly, and leave; officers may use that same customary license to seek a consensual conversation or ask for consent.

cards.front: Reading a standard Bluebook case cite left to right (use the neutral example 392 U.S. 1, 30 (1968)), what six components appear and in what order?
cards.back: Case name (parties) - volume number - reporter abbreviation - first page - pinpoint (pin cite) - court & year parenthetical.

cards.front: In the neutral example cite '392 U.S. 1, 30 (1968),' what is the difference between the '1' and the '30'?
cards.back: The '1' is the first page where the opinion starts; the '30' is the pinpoint (pin cite) — the specific page the quoted or relied-on language is on.

cards.front: When must you include a pinpoint (pin cite), and why does a cite without one matter?
cards.back: Always pin when you quote or attribute a specific holding. A cite without a pin is hard to check and weak in a courtroom.

cards.front: Why does a Supreme Court cite's parenthetical carry only the year (e.g., '(1968)') while a lower-court cite names the court (e.g., '(9th Cir. 2021)')?
cards.back: For SCOTUS the reporter 'U.S.' already identifies the court, so the parenthetical is year only; for lower courts the parenthetical must also name the deciding court.

cards.front: Which three reporter abbreviations identify a U.S. Supreme Court opinion?
cards.back: 'U.S.' (official United States Reports), 'S. Ct.' (West), and 'L. Ed. 2d' (Lawyers' Edition) — the official plus its two parallels.

cards.front: Which reporters signal a U.S. Court of Appeals case versus a U.S. District Court case?
cards.back: Courts of Appeals -> 'F.4th'/'F.3d'/'F.2d'/'F.' (with the circuit in the parenthetical); District Courts -> 'F. Supp. 3d'/'F. Supp. 2d'/'F. Supp.'

cards.front: What court and what kind of disposition does the reporter 'F. App'x' (Federal Appendix) signal?
cards.back: Unpublished circuit (Court of Appeals) dispositions — typically non-precedential, persuasive at best.

cards.front: What is the relationship between the reporter series 'F.', 'F.2d', 'F.3d', and 'F.4th'?
cards.back: They are successive editions of the same Federal Reporter that started over as volumes filled up over the decades — 'F.4th' is simply the current one; all are Courts of Appeals reporters.

cards.front: What is a parallel citation, and what should you NOT mistake it for?
cards.back: The same opinion reported in more than one reporter at once (e.g., a SCOTUS case in U.S., S. Ct., and L. Ed. 2d). Don't mistake the three parallels for three different cases.

cards.front: In Bluebook practice, what do the introductory signals '(no signal),' 'see,' 'cf.,' 'e.g.,' and 'but see' each tell the reader?
cards.back: (no signal) = the source directly states the proposition; see = supports by clear inference; cf. = supports an analogous point; e.g. = one example among many that say the same thing; but see = authority that cuts the other way.

cards.front: Short form: '{{c1::Id.}}' refers to the immediately preceding authority, so 'Id. at 30' means {{c2::same source, new page}}.
cards.back: Id. = the immediately preceding authority; 'Id. at 30' = same source, new page. Use only when the cite right before it is the same source.

cards.front: What does 'supra' point to, and why is it generally NOT used for cases under the Bluebook?
cards.back: Supra points back to a source cited earlier but not immediately above; it is used mainly for books, articles, and the like — generally not for cases under Bluebook.

cards.front: What is the practical weight difference between a published and an unpublished federal opinion?
cards.back: Published opinions are precedential — binding on that court and the courts below it within the jurisdiction; unpublished dispositions (often in F. App'x) are typically non-precedential and persuasive at best.

cards.front: Federal Rule of Appellate Procedure 32.1 permits citing federal unpublished opinions issued on or after Jan. 1, 2007 — why is that permission not the whole story?
cards.back: Permission to cite is not the same as binding force; an unpublished opinion remains persuasive at best, so confirm a case is published (or otherwise binding in your jurisdiction) before leaning on it.

cards.front: What does 'per curiam' mean, and why can't you judge an opinion's weight from that label alone?
cards.back: Per curiam = 'by the court,' issued in the name of the whole court rather than a single authoring judge. They can be fully precedential (many SCOTUS per curiams are) or summary and non-precedential — judge the weight by the court and whether it's published, not by the 'per curiam' label.

cards.front: Name the free federal case-law research tools an instructor can rely on instead of Westlaw or Lexis.
cards.back: CourtListener (+ RECAP), Google Scholar (case law mode), Justia, Caselaw Access Project (searched via CourtListener), OpenJurist, govinfo, and official court/SCOTUS sites.

cards.front: What is CourtListener's RECAP Archive, and why check it before paying PACER?
cards.back: RECAP is the largest open collection of federal docket filings (briefs, motions, orders) pulled from PACER; the RECAP browser extension captures PACER documents users pull and shares them free, so a filing you need may already be there at no cost.

cards.front: The three ways to search depend on what you already have — what are they?
cards.back: By citation (drop '392 U.S. 1' into a citation lookup for the exact opinion); by party name (search the case name, watching for same-named different cases); by proposition / full text (search the idea, then read down to the controlling case).

cards.front: With no free Shepard's or KeyCite, how do you verify a case is still good law, and what are you scanning for?
cards.back: Do it by hand with free citator substitutes — CourtListener's 'Cited By'/'Authorities' and Google Scholar's 'How cited' — scanning whether later courts followed, criticized, distinguished, or overruled it. A narrowed or reversed case is a trap.

cards.front: Why must you read the actual opinion rather than cite from a headnote or summary?
cards.back: Headnotes are editorial summaries, not the court's words, and can mislead. Pull the text, find the court's actual language, and pin to its page.

cards.front: What is the 'unit of trustworthy legal research' — the triple this wiki saves for every verified case?
cards.back: A consistent Bluebook cite + a CourtListener permalink + a pinpoint (plus a one-line note on the holding and its weight), so the case can be re-found and re-verified instantly.

cards.front: What does the circuit named in a Court of Appeals parenthetical (e.g., '(5th Cir.)') tell you about the case's authority, and what's the pitfall?
cards.back: It tells you whose persuasive authority it is — a Fifth Circuit case binds the Fifth Circuit but is only persuasive elsewhere. Never anchor a multi-jurisdiction point to one circuit.

cards.front: What two elements must BOTH be present to trigger the requirement of Miranda warnings (the instructor's 'two C's')?
cards.back: Custody AND interrogation. Custody = objective, arrest-equivalent restraint of freedom of action; interrogation = express questioning or its functional equivalent. Warnings are not required unless both coincide.

cards.front: Statements obtained during custodial interrogation without warnings or a valid waiver are inadmissible in the {{c1::prosecution's case-in-chief}}.
cards.back: the prosecution's case-in-chief — the Miranda exclusionary remedy bars unwarned custodial-interrogation statements from the government's case-in-chief.

cards.front: What did Miranda v. Arizona hold regarding statements from custodial interrogation?
cards.back: Such statements are inadmissible unless police first gave the warnings and the suspect knowingly and voluntarily waived them.

cards.front: How did Miranda v. Arizona originally describe 'custody'?
cards.back: A suspect 'taken into custody or otherwise deprived of his freedom of action in any significant way.'

cards.front: What is the benchmark for Miranda 'custody' under the objective test?
cards.back: Whether a reasonable person's freedom of action is restrained to the degree associated with formal arrest, judged by the objective circumstances — not the officer's private view.

cards.front: How did Rhode Island v. Innis define 'interrogation' under Miranda?
cards.back: Interrogation is not limited to express questioning; it also includes the 'functional equivalent' — any words or actions by police (other than those normally attendant to arrest and custody) that police should know are reasonably likely to elicit an incriminating response from the suspect.

cards.front: Innis: the functional-equivalent test reaches words or actions by police that they {{c1::should know are reasonably likely to elicit an incriminating response}} from the suspect.
cards.back: should know are reasonably likely to elicit an incriminating response — Innis, 446 U.S. at 300-01.

cards.front: Under Innis, whose perceptions does the 'functional equivalent of interrogation' test focus on?
cards.back: The perceptions of the suspect — the question is whether police should know their words or actions are reasonably likely to elicit an incriminating response, not their actual intent.

cards.front: Which case held that officers' in-car conversation about a missing shotgun and nearby handicapped children was NOT the functional equivalent of interrogation?
cards.back: Rhode Island v. Innis — the officers should not have known the exchange was reasonably likely to elicit an incriminating response, so it was not interrogation.

cards.front: What did Dickerson v. United States hold about Miranda and 18 U.S.C. § 3501?
cards.back: Miranda is a constitutional rule that Congress cannot override by statute, so § 3501 cannot supersede it; Miranda governs the admissibility of confessions in federal court. This remains current law.

cards.front: Why does Dickerson v. United States matter to the status of Miranda?
cards.back: It settled that Miranda is a constitutional decision (not a mere judicially created prophylactic rule), so Congress's attempt to replace it with a voluntariness statute (§ 3501) was unconstitutional.

cards.front: What two distinct things did Berkemer v. McCarty hold?
cards.back: (1) Miranda applies to ALL custodial interrogation regardless of offense severity, misdemeanors included; and (2) the temporary, public detention of an ordinary traffic stop is NOT Miranda custody.

cards.front: Is an ordinary traffic stop Miranda custody?
cards.back: No. Under Berkemer v. McCarty a temporary, public roadside detention is not custody, so on-scene questioning needs no warnings — even though Miranda otherwise reaches all offenses.

cards.front: Does the severity of the offense (misdemeanor vs. felony) affect whether Miranda applies?
cards.back: No. Berkemer v. McCarty holds Miranda reaches all custodial interrogation regardless of whether the charge is a misdemeanor or a felony.

cards.front: In Oregon v. Mathiason, was a suspect who voluntarily came to the station and was told he was free to leave 'in custody' for Miranda?
cards.back: No. A voluntary station-house interview where the suspect is told he is not under arrest and free to leave is not custody — even inside a police building and even when confronted with a false claim of evidence.

cards.front: What did Orozco v. Texas establish about the location of Miranda custody?
cards.back: Custody is not limited to the stationhouse — four officers questioning an arrested suspect in his own bedroom in the early morning was custody requiring warnings.

cards.front: In Orozco v. Texas, what fact made the bedroom questioning custodial?
cards.back: By the officer's own testimony the suspect 'was under arrest and not free to leave when he was questioned in his bedroom in the early hours of the morning.'

cards.front: Contrast Mathiason and Orozco: why was the station visit non-custodial but the bedroom questioning custodial?
cards.back: Setting is not dispositive — restraint is. In Mathiason the suspect came voluntarily and was told he was free to leave (no custody); in Orozco he was under arrest, surrounded by four officers, and not free to leave (custody), even though it was his own home.

cards.front: What did J.D.B. v. North Carolina add to the Miranda custody analysis?
cards.back: A child's age is a relevant factor in the objective custody analysis when it was known to or objectively apparent to the officer, because a reasonable child may feel restrained where an adult would not.

cards.front: Did J.D.B. v. North Carolina turn the custody test into a subjective inquiry?
cards.back: No. Custody stays objective; a child's age is folded into that objective calculus only when known or apparent to the officer — it does not become a subjective test about the particular suspect's actual state of mind.

cards.front: Does imprisonment by itself put an inmate 'in custody' for Miranda when questioned about an outside offense?
cards.back: No. Howes v. Fields holds imprisonment alone is not Miranda custody; whether an inmate is in custody depends on the totality — here, told he could leave and return to his cell, he was not in custody.

cards.front: What did Stansbury v. California hold about how custody is determined?
cards.back: Custody is determined by the objective circumstances of the interrogation, not by the subjective, undisclosed views of the officer or suspect; an officer's uncommunicated suspicion is irrelevant.

cards.front: Which case holds that an officer's hidden belief that a person is a suspect is irrelevant to whether that person is in Miranda custody?
cards.back: Stansbury v. California — custody turns on objective circumstances, not the officer's uncommunicated suspicion.

cards.front: What is the public-safety exception recognized in New York v. Quarles?
cards.back: When officers ask questions reasonably prompted by an immediate threat to public safety (e.g., the location of a hidden loaded gun), the unwarned answers and the resulting evidence (the gun) are admissible.

cards.front: How narrow is the Quarles public-safety exception?
cards.back: Narrow — it is limited to questions reasonably prompted by an immediate danger; it is not a license for routine investigative questioning or general fact-gathering.

cards.front: Why did Illinois v. Perkins hold that no Miranda warnings are required for undercover or jailhouse-informant questioning?
cards.back: Because there is no police-dominated 'coercive atmosphere' — a suspect who does not know he is speaking to law enforcement faces no compelling pressure, so the Miranda predicate is absent.

cards.front: Which case removes undercover agents posing as fellow inmates from Miranda's reach entirely?
cards.back: Illinois v. Perkins — incarceration plus questioning by an undisclosed government agent does not create the coercive atmosphere Miranda guards against.

cards.front: Do volunteered, spontaneous statements require Miranda warnings?
cards.back: No. Volunteered statements are not the product of interrogation, so they need no warnings — even after arrest, words 'normally attendant to arrest and custody' do not count as interrogation (Innis).

cards.front: Common pitfall: officers treat Miranda as covering all police contact. When do warnings actually attach?
cards.back: Only when custody AND interrogation coincide. On-scene, consensual, or otherwise non-custodial questioning requires no warnings.

cards.front: Distinction pitfall: is a Terry stop or traffic stop a Fourth Amendment seizure the same as Miranda custody?
cards.back: No. A brief, public investigative detention is a Fourth Amendment seizure of the person, but it is NOT Miranda custody (Berkemer). Miranda custody requires arrest-equivalent restraint.

cards.front: Common pitfall: reading the Quarles public-safety exception too broadly. What is the limit?
cards.back: It is confined to questions neutralizing an immediate danger; it does not authorize general investigative fact-gathering.

cards.front: What is the analytical sequence for deciding whether Miranda warnings were required for a statement?
cards.back: (1) Was the suspect in custody (objective, arrest-equivalent restraint)? If no, no warnings. (2) Was there interrogation (express or functional equivalent under Innis)? If no, no warnings. (3) Did a public-safety danger justify unwarned questions (Quarles)? If yes, no warnings; otherwise warnings were required.

cards.front: Contrast Perkins and Orozco: both involve a suspect already in police hands, so why does Orozco require warnings but Perkins does not?
cards.back: Miranda guards against police-dominated coercion. In Orozco the suspect knew he was under arrest and facing officers (coercive custody → warnings). In Perkins the suspect did not know he was talking to law enforcement, so there was no coercive atmosphere and Miranda did not apply.

cards.front: After Miranda warnings are given, what two responses can a suspect make, and what is the standard for a valid waiver?
cards.back: He may waive his rights (the waiver must be knowing, intelligent, and voluntary, and may be express or inferred from conduct) or he may invoke them.

cards.front: Invocation under Miranda runs on two distinct tracks with different rules. What are the two standards depending on which right is invoked?
cards.back: Invoking COUNSEL triggers the Edwards bright-line bar on police-initiated re-questioning; invoking SILENCE only requires that police "scrupulously honor" the invocation.

cards.front: What did the Supreme Court hold about Miranda waiver in North Carolina v. Butler (1979)?
cards.back: A waiver need not be express; it may be inferred from the suspect's words and conduct. But silence alone is never enough, and the burden of proving waiver stays on the government.

cards.front: Under North Carolina v. Butler, a valid Miranda waiver may be {{c1::inferred from the suspect's words and conduct}}, but {{c2::silence alone}} is never enough.
cards.back: inferred from the suspect's words and conduct / silence alone

cards.front: Under North Carolina v. Butler, who bears the burden of proving a Miranda waiver?
cards.back: The government bears the burden of proving the suspect waived his rights; it cannot be presumed from silence.

cards.front: What bright-line rule does Edwards v. Arizona (1981) establish once a suspect invokes the right to counsel?
cards.back: Police may not re-initiate interrogation until counsel has been made available, unless the accused himself initiates further communication.

cards.front: Quote the Edwards v. Arizona rule on re-interrogation after an accused has invoked counsel.
cards.back: "[A]n accused ... having expressed his desire to deal with the police only through counsel, is not subject to further interrogation by the authorities until counsel has been made available to him, unless the accused himself initiates further communication, exchanges, or conversations with the police."

cards.front: Under Edwards v. Arizona, what is the one circumstance in which interrogation may resume after counsel has been invoked but before counsel is made available?
cards.back: When the accused himself initiates further communication, exchanges, or conversations with the police.

cards.front: What standard governs the admissibility of later statements after a suspect invokes the right to SILENCE, under Michigan v. Mosley (1975)?
cards.back: Later statements are admissible if the police "scrupulously honored" the invocation of silence.

cards.front: In Michigan v. Mosley, what facts showed the police had "scrupulously honored" the suspect's invocation of silence?
cards.back: Questioning ceased, time passed, fresh Miranda warnings were given, and the second interrogation concerned a different crime.

cards.front: What did Berghuis v. Thompkins (2010) hold about how the right to silence must be invoked?
cards.back: Silence alone does not invoke the right; the right to remain silent must be invoked unambiguously. A suspect who answers a question after a long silence has impliedly waived the right.

cards.front: Under Berghuis v. Thompkins, what is the effect of a suspect staying largely silent through a long interrogation and then answering a question?
cards.back: He has impliedly waived his right to remain silent; staying quiet neither invokes the right nor blocks a finding of waiver.

cards.front: What did Arizona v. Roberson (1988) hold about the scope of the Edwards bar across different offenses?
cards.back: The Edwards bar is NOT offense-specific — once counsel is invoked, police may not interrogate about any offense, including an unrelated one, and a second officer's ignorance of the invocation is no excuse.

cards.front: Under Arizona v. Roberson, can a second, unaware officer bypass the Edwards bar by questioning a suspect who already invoked counsel?
cards.back: No — a second officer's ignorance of the prior invocation is no excuse; the Edwards bar still applies.

cards.front: How does the Fifth Amendment Edwards bar (Arizona v. Roberson) differ from the Sixth Amendment right to counsel on offense-specificity?
cards.back: The Edwards/Fifth Amendment bar is NOT offense-specific — it blocks questioning on any offense. The Sixth Amendment right to counsel IS offense-specific.

cards.front: What did Minnick v. Mississippi (1990) hold about whether prior consultation with a lawyer satisfies Edwards?
cards.back: Edwards is not satisfied merely because the suspect already consulted a lawyer; counsel must be PRESENT for police-initiated re-questioning.

cards.front: Under Minnick v. Mississippi, the Edwards bar is not lifted merely because the suspect consulted a lawyer; counsel must be {{c1::present}} for police-initiated re-questioning.
cards.back: present

cards.front: What did Maryland v. Shatzer (2010) hold ends Edwards protection?
cards.back: A 14-day break in Miranda custody ends Edwards protection; after that, police may re-approach and seek a fresh waiver.

cards.front: Under Maryland v. Shatzer, does releasing a suspect back into the general prison population count as a break in Miranda custody?
cards.back: Yes — release back into the general prison population is itself a break in custody, and a 14-day break ends Edwards protection.

cards.front: Under Maryland v. Shatzer, a {{c1::14-day}} break in Miranda custody ends Edwards protection.
cards.back: 14-day

cards.front: What did Davis v. United States (1994) hold about an ambiguous reference to counsel?
cards.back: Invocation of counsel must be unambiguous; an equivocal reference ("maybe I should talk to a lawyer") does not require police to stop questioning, and they need not even ask clarifying questions.

cards.front: Quote the Davis v. United States rule on what an officer must do when a suspect makes an ambiguous reference to an attorney.
cards.back: "[I]f a suspect makes a reference to an attorney that is ambiguous or equivocal in that a reasonable officer in light of the circumstances would have understood only that the suspect might be invoking the right to counsel, our precedents do not require the cessation of questioning."

cards.front: Under Davis v. United States, are officers required to ask clarifying questions after an ambiguous reference to a lawyer?
cards.back: No — officers are not required to ask clarifying questions (though doing so is good practice); they may keep questioning.

cards.front: What did Oregon v. Elstad (1985) hold about a confession that follows an earlier un-warned statement?
cards.back: An earlier un-warned but voluntary statement does not automatically taint a later confession; if the suspect is then properly Mirandized and waives, the second, warned statement is admissible.

cards.front: Oregon v. Elstad's safe harbor depends on what quality of the earlier un-warned statement?
cards.back: The earlier statement must have been voluntary in the due-process sense; actual coercion is a separate problem that the Elstad safe harbor does not cover.

cards.front: What did United States v. Patane (2004) hold about physical evidence derived from an un-warned statement?
cards.back: Physical fruits of an un-warned but voluntary statement are admissible.

cards.front: Which Supreme Court case holds that the physical fruits of an un-warned but voluntary statement are admissible?
cards.back: United States v. Patane (2004).

cards.front: What did Missouri v. Seibert (2004) hold about the deliberate "question-first, warn-later" interrogation technique?
cards.back: A deliberate "question-first, warn-later" two-step interrogation, designed to circumvent Miranda, is invalid; Elstad's safe harbor does not cover such bad-faith end-runs.

cards.front: Distinguish Oregon v. Elstad from Missouri v. Seibert on the two-step interrogation.
cards.back: Elstad gives a safe harbor where an earlier un-warned but voluntary statement does not taint a later warned confession; Seibert removes that safe harbor when the two-step was a DELIBERATE strategy to circumvent Miranda.

cards.front: What did Moran v. Burbine (1986) hold about a waiver when police concealed that an attorney was trying to reach the suspect?
cards.back: The waiver is valid; events outside the suspect's knowledge do not bear on whether HE made a knowing, intelligent, and voluntary choice to waive.

cards.front: What principle about Miranda waiver does Moran v. Burbine illustrate — what does waiver test?
cards.back: Waiver tests the suspect's own knowledge, not the police's candor; events outside his awareness do not undermine his knowing, intelligent, and voluntary choice.

cards.front: Common pitfall: an officer treats a suspect's mere silence as an invocation of the right to remain silent. Why is this wrong (per Berghuis v. Thompkins)?
cards.back: Merely staying quiet neither invokes the right to silence nor blocks waiver; to stop questioning, the suspect must invoke unambiguously.

cards.front: Common pitfall: an officer treats an ambiguous lawyer reference as an invocation and stops questioning. Why is that not required (per Davis v. United States)?
cards.back: An ambiguous or equivocal reference does not invoke counsel; invocation must be unambiguous, so officers may keep questioning and need not stop or even clarify.

cards.front: Common pitfall: officers treat invoking silence and invoking counsel identically. What is the consequence?
cards.back: They either over- or under-protect the suspect — invoking silence allows re-questioning if scrupulously honored (Mosley), while invoking counsel triggers the rigid, offense-blind Edwards bar.

cards.front: Where does Fourth Amendment fruit-of-the-poisonous-tree analysis live, as distinguished from Miranda-statement fruits (Elstad/Patane/Seibert)?
cards.back: Fourth Amendment fruit-of-the-poisonous-tree analysis is governed separately on The Exclusionary Rule; this page covers Miranda-statement fruits.

cards.front: What does Maryland v. Shatzer's clock actually require — and what is the most common misreading of it?
cards.back: It requires a break in Miranda custody PLUS 14 days — not merely 14 days of waiting while the suspect stays in custody. The common misreading treats Shatzer as a 14-day waiting period that lets police re-approach a still-jailed, counsel-invoking suspect.

cards.front: Under Maryland v. Shatzer, does continuous pretrial detention give a suspect a break in Miranda custody?
cards.back: No — continuous pretrial detention is NOT a Shatzer break, because the coercive Miranda-custody pressure never lets up; Edwards still bars police-initiated re-questioning no matter how much time passes.

cards.front: Why did Maryland v. Shatzer fix the break-in-custody period at 14 days?
cards.back: Because that span gives the suspect time "to get reacclimated to his normal life, to consult with friends and counsel, and to shake off any residual coercive effects of his prior custody."

cards.front: Common pitfall: officers re-approach a jailed suspect who invoked counsel just because 14 days have passed. What does Maryland v. Shatzer actually require to lift the Edwards bar in custody?
cards.back: A genuine break in Miranda custody — e.g., release to the general prison population — and only then the 14 days. A suspect in continuous jail custody never gets that break, so the bar is not lifted.

cards.front: How did the New Jersey Supreme Court in State v. Wint (2018) — a non-binding, illustrative state decision — apply the Shatzer break-in-custody rule to continuous pretrial detention?
cards.back: It held that continuous pre-indictment pretrial detention is NOT a Shatzer break in custody, so a suspect held ~6 months after invoking counsel could not be reinterrogated; the result illustrates (it does not bind) the federal Shatzer/Edwards rule.

cards.front: Which non-binding state decision held that a defendant kept in ~6 months of continuous pre-indictment detention after invoking counsel got no Shatzer break, so out-of-state detectives' interrogation on an unrelated murder violated Edwards despite repeated fresh warnings?
cards.back: State v. Wint (N.J. 2018) — a New Jersey Supreme Court decision, persuasive/illustrative only, not binding federal authority.

cards.front: In State v. Wint (N.J. 2018, non-binding illustration), did repeated fresh Miranda warnings cure the Edwards violation where the suspect remained in continuous detention after invoking counsel?
cards.back: No — repeated fresh warnings did not cure the Edwards violation, because continuous detention provided no Shatzer break in custody to lift the bar.

cards.front: Does the Davis "reasonable officer" unambiguity requirement apply only to invocations of counsel, or to invocations of silence too?
cards.back: It polices entry to BOTH tracks: Davis (512 U.S. at 459) requires an unambiguous invocation of counsel, and Berghuis v. Thompkins imported the same standard into the silence track — neither track is triggered by an ambiguous invocation.

cards.front: The same unambiguity gate guards both invocation tracks: {{c1::Davis}} requires an unambiguous invocation of counsel, and {{c2::Thompkins}} imported that standard into the silence track.
cards.back: Davis / Thompkins

cards.front: Common pitfall (illustrated by the non-binding "lawyer dog" anecdote, State v. Demesme, La. 2017): how literally can courts apply Davis's ambiguity rule to a suspect's reference to counsel?
cards.back: Very literally — a Louisiana writ-denial concurrence treated "why don't you just give me a lawyer dog cause this is not what's up" as too ambiguous to invoke counsel under Davis. It is a one-justice, state, non-binding anecdote, not a merits holding.

cards.front: Which non-binding state case is the source of the "lawyer dog" anecdote, where a concurrence to a writ denial treated an equivocal request for counsel as too ambiguous to invoke under Davis?
cards.back: State v. Demesme (La. 2017) — a Louisiana Supreme Court writ-denial concurrence, illustrative and non-binding, not a merits holding.

cards.front: Sheriff's deputies stand by while a trailer-park owner tears a mobile home from its foundation and tows it away — no search, no rummaging, no invasion of privacy, just the carting off of the family's home. Which case held this was still a Fourth Amendment seizure?
cards.back:
**Soldal v. Cook County (1992)**
Holding: A Fourth Amendment seizure of property occurs whenever there is "meaningful interference with an individual's possessory interests in that property" — and it happens even when there is NO search and NO invasion of privacy or liberty (here, deputies aiding the tow of a mobile home).
Signature: "A 'seizure' of property occurs when there is some meaningful interference with an individual's possessory interests in that property" (quoting Jacobsen); the Amendment protects property as such, not just privacy.
Why it matters: separates the two thresholds — a SEARCH is measured by privacy (Katz), a SEIZURE of property by possessory interest; you can have one without the other.
⚠ Don't confuse the SEARCH threshold (Katz — reasonable expectation of privacy) with the SEIZURE-of-property threshold (Soldal/Jacobsen — possessory interference); Soldal is the seizure side, no privacy invasion required.

cards.front: Police with probable cause to believe a man has drugs in his trailer refuse to let him re-enter unaccompanied while an officer leaves to get a warrant — barring him from his own home for about two hours. Which case upheld this temporary seizure of the premises?
cards.back:
**Illinois v. McArthur (2001)**
Holding: Police with probable cause may, for a reasonable time, temporarily prevent a resident from entering his home (a roughly two-hour seizure of the premises) to keep him from destroying evidence while they diligently obtain a warrant.
Signature: a brief, narrowly tailored seizure of the home is reasonable where supported by probable cause, exigency (risk of evidence destruction), and diligent pursuit of a warrant.
Why it matters: balancing test — preserving evidence by restraining access to the home is far less intrusive than a warrantless entry, search, or arrest.
⚠ Pair with the exigent-circumstances exception (the lawful warrantless-entry alternative) and **Segura v. United States** (securing premises from within pending a warrant); McArthur keeps officers OUTSIDE while the warrant is sought.

cards.front: Suspicious first-class packages (fictitious return address, foreign plates on the mailer's car) are held back from the mail for 29 hours while agents investigate and obtain a warrant. Which case held this detention reasonable?
cards.back:
**United States v. Van Leeuwen (1970)**
Holding: Detaining mailed first-class packages on reasonable suspicion — here for 29 hours — pending an investigation and a warrant is a reasonable seizure; the delay alone did not violate the Fourth Amendment.
Signature: "detention of mail could at some point become an unreasonable seizure" — but a limited detention to confirm suspicion and secure a warrant is the prudent, reasonable course.
Why it matters: temporary seizure of property/effects on reasonable suspicion is permissible; the touchstone is reasonableness in time and diligence, not a fixed clock.
⚠ Don't confuse with **United States v. Place** (1983) — a 90-minute detention of luggage was UNreasonable there given the intrusion on the traveler and the agents' lack of diligence; Van Leeuwen's 29-hour mail hold was reasonable because of the unavoidable delay and minimal intrusion.
cards.cite: United States v. Van Leeuwen, 397 U.S. 249, 252-253 (1970)

cards.front: Officers arrest two men based on a statewide radio bulletin — but the sheriff who issued the bulletin had only an uncorroborated informant's tip and no probable cause. The arresting officers acted in good faith on the BOLO. Valid arrest? Which case?
cards.back:
**Whiteley v. Warden, Wyoming State Penitentiary (1971)**
Holding: Officers may act on a police bulletin/BOLO, but the arrest is valid only if the ORIGINATING officer actually had probable cause; if the source lacked PC, relaying the request through a bulletin cannot manufacture it, and the arrest is unconstitutional.
Signature: "an otherwise illegal arrest cannot be insulated from challenge by the decision of the instigating officer to rely on fellow officers to make the arrest."
Why it matters: the collective-knowledge / fellow-officer rule AND its limit — the acting officer may rely on the bulletin, but the constitutionality is judged by the knowledge of the agency that issued it.
⚠ Don't confuse with **United States v. Hensley** (1985) — relying on a flyer/bulletin is proper when the issuing agency HAD the requisite suspicion (RAS for a Terry stop there); Whiteley invalidates the arrest because the source had no probable cause to begin with.

cards.front: The Supreme Court strikes down New York's permissive eavesdropping statute as too broad and, in doing so, lays out the constitutional requirements for lawful wiretaps — particularity, probable cause, limited duration, and a return — that Congress then codified in Title III. Which case?
cards.back:
**Berger v. New York (1967)**
Holding: New York's eavesdrop statute was unconstitutional because it authorized broad, indiscriminate electronic surveillance without adequate safeguards; the Court set out the requirements electronic-surveillance authorizations must meet — probable cause, particular description of the crime/place/conversations to be seized, strict limits on duration, and prompt notice/return.
Signature: the constitutional "blueprint" for wiretap statutes; its requirements were codified by Congress in Title III of the Omnibus Crime Control Act (18 U.S.C. §§ 2510-2522).
Why it matters: the statutory-drafting side of electronic surveillance — Berger defines what a valid wiretap order must contain.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Katz v. United States** (decided the same year, 1967) — Katz supplies the privacy THEORY (a bug is a search), while Berger supplies the procedural blueprint a wiretap order must satisfy.

cards.front: FBI agents secretly break into a suspect's office to install a bug under a Title III surveillance order that never expressly mentioned a covert entry. Was a separate, explicit authorization for the break-in required? Which case?
cards.back:
**Dalia v. United States (1979)**
Holding: A Title III electronic-surveillance order implicitly authorizes a covert/surreptitious entry to install the listening device; no separate or explicit judicial authorization for the entry is required, and such an entry does not per se violate the Fourth Amendment.
Signature: "The Fourth Amendment does not prohibit per se a covert entry performed for the purpose of installing otherwise legal electronic bugging equipment."
Why it matters: makes Title III wiretap orders workable — most bugging is impossible without a break-in, so the power to authorize surveillance carries the power to authorize the entry needed to accomplish it.
⚠ Pair with **Berger v. New York** — Berger sets the requirements a wiretap order must satisfy; Dalia holds that a valid order silently carries the authority to enter and plant the device.

cards.front: Federal agents arrest suspects, skip taking them before a U.S. Commissioner, and instead hold them in a barren cell for 14 hours of continuous questioning until they confess. Invoking its supervisory power (not the Constitution), which case excluded those confessions from federal court?
cards.back:
**McNabb v. United States (1943)**
Holding: Confessions obtained from a federal arrestee during prolonged detention before presentment to a magistrate are inadmissible in federal court — an exclusionary rule grounded in the Supreme Court's supervisory power over federal criminal justice, not the Constitution.
Signature: to admit such evidence "would stultify the policy which Congress has enacted into law"; the Court expressly declined to reach the Fifth Amendment issue.
Why it matters: origin of the federal prompt-presentment (McNabb-Mallory) rule — a FEDERAL-only confession-suppression doctrine separate from voluntariness and from Miranda.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Miranda** (warnings) or the **voluntariness** due-process line — McNabb-Mallory turns on UNNECESSARY PRE-PRESENTMENT DELAY, and is a federal supervisory rule.

cards.front: A federal arrestee is held and interrogated for hours instead of being taken "without unnecessary delay" before a committing magistrate as Fed. R. Crim. P. 5(a) requires; he then confesses. Which case held the confession must be suppressed because of the delay?
cards.back:
**Mallory v. United States (1957)**
Holding: A confession obtained during "unnecessary delay" in bringing a federal arrestee before a magistrate (Fed. R. Crim. P. 5(a)) must be excluded; delay to extract a confession is forbidden, though a brief delay for quick verification may be permissible.
Signature: arraignment "without unnecessary delay" — "the delay must not be of a nature to give opportunity for the extraction of a confession."
Why it matters: the second half of the "McNabb-Mallory" rule — ties the federal prompt-presentment exclusion to Rule 5(a)'s text.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Miranda** (warnings) or **due-process voluntariness** — this is the federal DELAY rule; a confession can be voluntary yet still suppressed for presentment delay.

cards.front: Which 2009 case held that 18 U.S.C. § 3501 modified but did NOT abolish the federal prompt-presentment rule — a voluntary confession within 6 hours of arrest is admissible, but beyond 6 hours the unnecessary-delay exclusionary rule still applies?
cards.back:
**Corley v. United States (2009)**
Holding: "§ 3501 modified McNabb-Mallory without supplanting it" — under § 3501(c), a voluntary confession made within 6 hours of arrest is admissible despite presentment delay; a confession made beyond 6 hours is still subject to suppression if the delay was unnecessary or unreasonable.
Signature: the 6-hour safe harbor — reading § 3501(a) to abolish the rule would render § 3501(c) "nonsensical and superfluous."
Why it matters: the modern, controlling statement of the federal prompt-presentment rule; resolves the circuit split over § 3501's reach.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Miranda** (§ 3501 tried and failed to override Miranda; Dickerson handles that) — Corley is about the DELAY/presentment rule, not warnings or voluntariness.

cards.front: At trial a defendant tells an exculpatory "frame-up" story for the first time; the prosecutor cross-examines him on why he stayed silent after getting his Miranda warnings at arrest. Which case held this violates due process?
cards.back:
**Doyle v. Ohio (1976)**
Holding: Using a defendant's silence AFTER receiving Miranda warnings to impeach his trial testimony violates the Due Process Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment.
Signature: Miranda warnings carry an "implicit" assurance that silence will carry no penalty, so post-warning silence is "insolubly ambiguous" and unfair to use against him.
Why it matters: the cornerstone limit on impeachment use of post-Miranda silence.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Harris v. New York** (un-Mirandized STATEMENTS may impeach a testifying defendant) or **Salinas v. Texas** (PRE-custody, pre-warning silence is usable) — Doyle is specifically about POST-warning SILENCE.

cards.front: A defendant, with appointed counsel, invokes his Sixth Amendment right to counsel at a bail hearing on an armed-robbery charge; police later question him (after Miranda waivers) about an unrelated murder. Which case held that invoking 6A counsel did NOT invoke the 5A Miranda right for the uncharged crime?
cards.back:
**McNeil v. Wisconsin (1991)**
Holding: The Sixth Amendment right to counsel is "offense-specific," and invoking it at a judicial proceeding (a bail hearing) does NOT invoke the non-offense-specific Fifth Amendment Miranda-Edwards right to counsel for other, uncharged offenses.
Signature: "offense-specific"; "Requesting the assistance of an attorney at a bail hearing" is not a request for counsel as to custodial interrogation.
Why it matters: the hinge separating the 5A and 6A counsel rights.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Arizona v. Roberson** (5A-Edwards invocation bars questioning even on UNRELATED crimes) or **Texas v. Cobb** (6A right is offense-specific by Blockburger) — McNeil keeps the two rights in separate lanes.

cards.front: A jailhouse informant offers a frightened inmate protection from other prisoners in exchange for the truth, eliciting a murder confession later found coerced. Which case held that admitting a coerced confession is subject to harmless-error review, not automatic reversal?
cards.back:
**Arizona v. Fulminante (1991)**
Holding: The admission of an involuntary/coerced confession is a "trial error" subject to harmless-error analysis under Chapman, not automatic reversal (though on these facts the error was NOT harmless and the conviction was reversed).
Signature: a coerced confession is a "trial error," "similar in both degree and kind to the erroneous admission of other types of evidence" — distinguished from "structural" defects.
Why it matters: overturned the old rule that a coerced confession always required reversal.
⚠ Don't confuse with the voluntariness/admissibility line (**Brown v. Mississippi, Ashcraft, Spano, Colorado v. Connelly**) — those decide WHETHER a confession comes in; Fulminante decides the consequence when a coerced one was wrongly admitted.

cards.front: Police give Miranda warnings and get a waiver, then — without telling the suspect in advance — question him about a different, more serious crime (a homicide) than the one he expected. Which case held the waiver was still "knowing"?
cards.back:
**Colorado v. Spring (1987)**
Holding: A Miranda waiver is "knowing and intelligent" even though police did not warn the suspect of all the crimes about which he would be questioned; awareness of every subject of interrogation is not a prerequisite to a valid waiver.
Signature: "The Constitution does not require that a criminal suspect know and understand every possible consequence of a waiver" of the Fifth Amendment privilege.
Why it matters: defines the "knowing" prong narrowly — police silence about the topic of questioning doesn't defeat a waiver.
⚠ Pair with **Moran v. Burbine** (waiver judged on the suspect's OWN awareness; police failure to tell him an attorney called does not vitiate it) — both reject outside-information theories of an invalid waiver.

cards.front: During custodial interrogation a juvenile asks, "Can I have my probation officer here?" Which case held that request is NOT a per se invocation of the right to counsel/silence, so admissibility turns on the totality of the circumstances?
cards.back:
**Fare v. Michael C. (1979)**
Holding: A juvenile's request to see his probation officer is NOT a per se invocation of his Fifth Amendment/Miranda rights (a probation officer is not the equivalent of an attorney); whether he validly waived his rights is judged by the totality of the circumstances.
Signature: admissibility is "resolved on the totality of the circumstances surrounding the interrogation," considering the juvenile's age, experience, and background.
Why it matters: limits what counts as an invocation and applies the totality test to juvenile waivers.
⚠ Don't confuse with **J.D.B. v. North Carolina** (a child's AGE is relevant to the CUSTODY analysis) — Fare is about INVOCATION/WAIVER, a different question; both involve juveniles but at different steps.

cards.front: An officer asks a man if he can search his car; the man says yes. The officer never tells him he has the right to refuse. The government concedes nothing about whether he knew he could say no. Is the consent valid — and which case sets the test?
cards.back:
**Schneckloth v. Bustamonte (1973)**
Holding: Consent is valid if voluntary under the totality of the circumstances; the government need NOT prove the person knew of, or was warned of, the right to refuse.
Signature: "a question of fact to be determined from the totality of all the circumstances"; knowledge of the right to refuse is "one factor," not the "sine qua non."
Why it matters: The voluntariness spine of consent-search doctrine — there is no Miranda-style consent warning.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Bumper** — there a false claim of a warrant turned "yes" into mere acquiescence (invalid).

cards.front: Officers tell a grandmother they have a warrant to search her house; relying on that, she lets them in. There was no valid warrant. The State then tries to justify the entry as a consent search. Which case?
cards.back:
**Bumper v. North Carolina (1968)**
Holding: Consent that is mere acquiescence to a claim of lawful authority (e.g., an asserted warrant) is NOT voluntary and cannot satisfy the government's burden.
Signature: a claimed warrant "announces in effect that the occupant has no right to resist"; "[w]here there is coercion there cannot be consent."
Why it matters: The floor under prong one — submission to claimed authority is not consent.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Schneckloth** (no warning required) — Bumper is the opposite pole: a false assertion of authority voids consent.

cards.front: While a suspect sits in a squad car outside, a woman who shares the bedroom with him lets police in and consents to a search of that room. The suspect never objected because he was not asked and not present. Which case validates the search?
cards.back:
**United States v. Matlock (1974)**
Holding: A co-occupant with common authority (mutual use / joint access, not property title) may consent to a search valid against an ABSENT, non-consenting co-occupant who assumed the risk.
Signature: "common authority" rests on "mutual use of the property by persons generally having joint access or control."
Why it matters: Anchors third-party (actual-authority) consent.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Randolph** (a physically PRESENT objector defeats the other's consent) or **Rodriguez** (apparent — even mistaken — authority).
cards.cite: United States v. Matlock, 415 U.S. 164, 170-71 & n.7 (1974)

cards.front: A woman calls herself the suspect's girlfriend, says "our apartment," and uses her key to let officers in to consent to a search. It later turns out she had moved out and no longer had authority — but the officers reasonably believed she did. Which case?
cards.back:
**Illinois v. Rodriguez (1990)**
Holding: Apparent authority — a warrantless entry on third-party consent is valid if officers REASONABLY (even if mistakenly) believed the consenter had common authority, judged by an objective standard.
Signature: would the facts "warrant a man of reasonable caution in the belief" that the consenter had authority?
Why it matters: Extends valid consent to reasonable mistakes of fact; ambiguity triggers a duty to inquire further.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Matlock** (ACTUAL common authority) — Rodriguez covers the mistaken-but-reasonable belief.

cards.front: A wife invites police in and consents to search the home, but her husband is standing right there at the door and expressly tells them "no." Officers search anyway. Which case makes that search invalid as to the husband?
cards.back:
**Georgia v. Randolph (2006)**
Holding: A physically present co-occupant's express refusal to consent prevails over another occupant's consent — the search is unreasonable and invalid as to the objector.
Signature: "a physically present co-occupant's stated refusal to permit entry prevails."
Why it matters: Carves a present-objector exception out of Matlock's co-tenant rule.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Matlock** (absent co-tenant — consent good) or **Fernandez** (objector lawfully REMOVED — remaining tenant may then consent).

cards.front: A man objects to police entering his apartment, so they arrest him for an unrelated assault and haul him away. An hour later, with him gone, his girlfriend consents to the search. Which case upholds it?
cards.back:
**Fernandez v. California (2014)**
Holding: Randolph applies only while the objector is physically present; once he is lawfully and objectively-reasonably removed (e.g., by arrest), the remaining occupant may validly consent.
Signature: Randolph "was limited to situations in which the objecting occupant is physically present"; removal must not be objectively unreasonable.
Why it matters: Narrows Randolph — a present "no" doesn't survive the objector's lawful departure.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Randolph** (objector PRESENT and objecting — search blocked); a STAGED removal to manufacture consent is not allowed.

cards.front: A driver gives general consent to search his car for drugs. An officer opens a folded paper bag on the floorboard and finds cocaine inside. The driver argues he never specifically authorized opening the bag. Which case rejects that argument?
cards.back:
**Florida v. Jimeno (1991)**
Holding: The scope of consent is measured by objective reasonableness — general consent to search a car for drugs reasonably reaches closed containers inside that might hold drugs; police need not separately ask for each.
Signature: "what would the typical reasonable person have understood"; a suspect "may of course delimit as he chooses the scope."
Why it matters: The scope prong of consent — and the suspect's power to limit it.
⚠ Note: authority is still person-/area-specific — a driver's consent to "the car" does not necessarily reach a passenger's personal bag.

cards.front: Police, without a warrant, get the night clerk of a hotel to unlock a guest's room with a passkey — "Be my guest" — and they search it while the guest is out. Which case holds that consent invalid?
cards.back:
**Stoner v. California (1964)**
Holding: A hotel clerk has no authority to consent to a police search of a guest's room; the guest's Fourth Amendment right is his alone to waive.
Signature: the Fourth Amendment's protections "are not to be eroded by strained applications of the law of agency."
Why it matters: Third-party consent requires real common authority — innkeepers, landlords, and clerks can't surrender a tenant/guest's privacy.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Matlock/Rodriguez** (co-occupant with actual or apparent authority) — a clerk has neither over the guest's room.

cards.front: Cab drivers radio that an armed robber just fled into a particular house. Police arrive minutes later and, without a warrant, enter and search the house (and find weapons and clothing) while looking for the robber and weapons. Which case upholds the entry?
cards.back:
**Warden v. Hayden (1967)**
Holding: Hot pursuit of a fleeing armed robber into a house is a valid warrantless entry and search where "the exigencies of the situation made that course imperative"; the case also abolished the old "mere evidence" rule.
Signature: "hot pursuit"; "the exigencies of the situation made that course imperative."
Why it matters: A foundational hot-pursuit / exigent-circumstances entry case.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Lange** (fleeing MISDEMEANANT — no categorical exigency) or **Welsh** (minor, nonjailable offense — no exigent home entry).

cards.front: Police smell marijuana at an apartment door, knock loudly and announce "Police!", then hear sounds of people moving — "like things were being moved inside" — and kick the door in to stop evidence destruction. The defendant says the police created their own exigency. Which case?
cards.back:
**Kentucky v. King (2011)**
Holding: Police do not impermissibly "create" an exigency so long as they did not gain entry by, or threaten to engage in, conduct that violates the Fourth Amendment; lawful knocking-and-announcing that prompts evidence destruction is fine.
Signature: "police-created exigency" doctrine; OK "[w]here... the police did not create the exigency by engaging or threatening to engage in conduct that violates the Fourth Amendment."
Why it matters: Sets the test for the police-created-exigency limit on the destruction-of-evidence exception.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Lange** (misdemeanant flight — case-by-case, no categorical entry).

cards.front: After a car accident, an officer with probable cause for DUI directs hospital staff to draw the arrestee's blood without a warrant, fearing the alcohol level will fall while a warrant is sought. On these facts, which 1966 case upholds the warrantless blood draw?
cards.back:
**Schmerber v. California (1966)**
Holding: A warrantless DUI blood draw was reasonable ON THESE FACTS — the officer could reasonably believe an emergency, where delay to get a warrant (after an accident) threatened "the destruction of evidence" as alcohol dissipated.
Signature: warrantless blood test upheld given "the destruction of evidence" risk and the accident's delay.
Why it matters: The original DUI blood-draw exigency case — but decided on its facts, not as a blanket rule.
⚠ Don't confuse with **McNeely** — alcohol's natural dissipation is NOT a per se exigency; exigency is case-by-case.

cards.front: A routine 2 a.m. DUI stop — no accident, no special delay. The officer takes the driver straight to a lab for a warrantless blood draw, arguing that because alcohol always dissipates, exigency is automatic. Which case rejects that per se rule?
cards.back:
**Missouri v. McNeely (2013)**
Holding: The natural metabolization of alcohol is NOT a per se exigency justifying a warrantless DUI blood draw in every case; exigency must be decided case-by-case on the totality of the circumstances.
Signature: "exigency in this context must be determined case by case based on the totality of the circumstances."
Why it matters: Confines Schmerber to its facts — dissipation alone doesn't excuse the warrant.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Schmerber** (accident + delay = exigency on the facts) or **Mitchell** (unconscious driver — exigency almost always present).

cards.front: A drunk driver passes out before any breath test can be given and is rushed to the hospital; with probable cause, police order a warrantless blood draw while he's unconscious. Which 2019 case (a plurality) upholds it?
cards.back:
**Mitchell v. Wisconsin (2019) (plurality)**
Holding: When police have probable cause for DUI and the driver's unconsciousness/stupor forces hospitalization before a breath test can be given, they may almost always order a warrantless blood draw under the exigency exception.
Signature: unconsciousness creates a second exigency factor on top of BAC dissipation; "Schmerber controls."
Why it matters: Applies the exigency framework to the unconscious-driver scenario; note it is a plurality (Alito), so its reach is narrower than a majority.
⚠ Don't confuse with **McNeely** (ordinary DUI — no per se exigency) — Mitchell turns on the added emergency of an unconscious driver.

cards.front: After a homicide, police conduct a sweeping four-day warrantless search of the apartment, justifying it as a "murder scene" exception. Which case holds there is no such exception?
cards.back:
**Mincey v. Arizona (1978)**
Holding: There is no general "murder-scene" exception — the seriousness of the offense does not by itself create exigency; but police may make a warrantless entry to render immediate aid and a prompt sweep for other victims or a killer, with scope limited to the emergency.
Signature: "the seriousness of the offense under investigation" does not "itself create[] exigent circumstances."
Why it matters: Caps emergency-scene authority — aid/sweep yes, general crime-scene search no.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Brigham City** (objective emergency-aid entry is allowed) — Mincey forbids the open-ended crime-scene search that follows.

cards.front: Through a window, officers see a fight inside a house — a juvenile punches an adult who spits blood. They enter without a warrant to break it up. The homeowner says the officers really wanted to investigate, not help. Which case says their subjective motive doesn't matter?
cards.back:
**Brigham City v. Stuart (2006)**
Holding: Police may enter a home without a warrant on an objectively reasonable basis to believe an occupant is seriously injured or imminently threatened; the officer's subjective motivation is irrelevant.
Signature: "objectively reasonable basis for believing that an occupant is seriously injured or imminently threatened"; "[t]he officer's subjective motivation is irrelevant."
Why it matters: The controlling emergency-aid (in-home) standard — purely objective.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Mincey** (no murder-scene exception; aid yes, general search no) or **Caniglia** (no freestanding caretaking entry into a home).

cards.front: Officers find a man screaming and throwing things inside his house, with blood on his hand and a smashed truck outside. He won't let them in. They enter without a warrant. Which per curiam case applies the emergency-aid rule to uphold the entry?
cards.back:
**Michigan v. Fisher (2009) (per curiam)**
Holding: Applies Brigham City — emergency-aid entry is lawful where it is objectively reasonable to believe someone is injured or in danger; officers need no ironclad proof of serious injury and need not turn out to be right.
Signature: the test "is not what [the officer] believed, but whether there was an objectively reasonable basis for believing" aid was needed.
Why it matters: A concrete, officer-facing application of the objective emergency-aid standard.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Brigham City** (the source rule) — Fisher is its applied per curiam progeny.

cards.front: A man drives off the road and walks home; police, without a warrant, enter his house at night and arrest him for a nonjailable, civil-forfeiture DUI offense, partly to preserve his blood-alcohol evidence. Which case holds this warrantless home entry unlawful?
cards.back:
**Welsh v. Wisconsin (1984)**
Holding: A warrantless nighttime home entry to arrest for a minor, nonjailable offense is not justified by exigent circumstances; the gravity of the underlying offense is an important factor, and evidence dissipation alone won't justify entry for a trivial crime.
Signature: "an important factor to be considered" is "the gravity of the underlying offense"; hesitation is greatest when the offense is "relatively minor."
Why it matters: Offense-seriousness limits exigent home entries.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Hayden** (armed-robbery hot pursuit — entry OK) or **Lange** (misdemeanant flight — case-by-case).

cards.front: A man playing loud music and honking is followed by an officer who flips on his lights; the man keeps driving into his own garage. The officer sticks a foot under the closing door and enters to arrest him for a misdemeanor. Which 2021 case rejects a categorical pursuit rule here?
cards.back:
**Lange v. California (2021)**
Holding: Pursuit of a fleeing misdemeanant does not categorically justify a warrantless home entry; courts must assess exigency case-by-case.
Signature: "[t]he flight of a suspected misdemeanant does not always justify a warrantless entry into a home."
Why it matters: Refuses a flat hot-pursuit rule for misdemeanors — flight plus a real exigency (harm, escape, evidence loss) is needed.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Hayden** (felony hot pursuit — entry OK) or **King** (police-created exigency for evidence destruction).

cards.front: After a domestic argument, officers do a welfare check, then enter a man's home without a warrant and seize his guns, invoking a "community caretaking" rationale. Which 2021 case holds there is no such standalone exception for the home?
cards.back:
**Caniglia v. Strom (2021)**
Holding: There is no freestanding "community caretaking" exception authorizing warrantless entry into a home; Cady's caretaking rationale was vehicle-specific and does not transplant to the house.
Signature: caretaking tasks are "not an open-ended license to perform them anywhere"; the lower court's rule "goes beyond anything this Court has recognized."
Why it matters: Polices the boundary — home welfare entries must run through consent, a warrant, or genuine exigency/emergency aid (which survive).
⚠ Don't confuse with **Cady** (community caretaking for VEHICLES — valid) or **Brigham City/Fisher** (emergency-aid home entry, still allowed).

cards.front: Police impound a drunk-driving officer's wrecked car and, without a warrant, search the trunk for his service revolver to keep it out of the wrong hands. Which case coins the "community caretaking" idea?
cards.back:
**Cady v. Dombrowski (1973)**
Holding: A warrantless caretaking search of an impounded vehicle (here, for a firearm) can be reasonable; police perform many vehicle functions divorced from criminal investigation.
Signature: "community caretaking functions, totally divorced from the detection, investigation, or acquisition of evidence relating to the violation of a criminal statute."
Why it matters: Origin of the community-caretaking doctrine — and it is vehicle-specific, resting on the car/home distinction.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Caniglia** — there is no community-caretaking exception for the HOME; Cady is for cars at the roadside.

cards.front: After a man is INDICTED and released on bail, federal agents wire a radio transmitter in his codefendant's car and get the codefendant to draw him into incriminating talk — no interrogation room, no questioning by police. Which case?
cards.back:
**Massiah v. United States (1964)**
Holding: Once adversary proceedings have begun (here, post-indictment), the government violates the Sixth Amendment when it deliberately elicits incriminating statements from the accused in the absence of counsel — even surreptitiously, outside any custodial setting.
Signature: the "deliberately elicited" test; founds the Sixth Amendment right-to-counsel line of interrogation cases.
Why it matters: the cornerstone of the Massiah doctrine — a counsel-based, charge-triggered limit on getting statements, distinct from Miranda.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Miranda** (5A, custodial interrogation, triggered by custody+questioning) — Massiah is 6A, triggered by the filing of formal charges, and reaches secret elicitation with no custody at all.

cards.front: A murder suspect is interrogated at the station BEFORE any indictment; he repeatedly asks to see his lawyer, his lawyer is in the building asking to see him, and police refuse to let them meet. Decided two years before Miranda. Which case?
cards.back:
**Escobedo v. Illinois (1964)**
Holding: Where a police investigation has focused on a suspect in custody who has requested and been denied counsel, and police have not warned him of his right to remain silent, statements elicited violate the Sixth Amendment right to counsel.
Signature: the immediate forerunner of Miranda — counsel's role at the accusatory, pre-indictment interrogation.
Why it matters: bridged Massiah and Miranda; its reasoning was largely absorbed into Miranda's Fifth Amendment framework two years later.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Miranda** (1966), which recast the protection as a Fifth Amendment / self-incrimination warnings rule; Escobedo today is read narrowly, mostly of historical importance.

cards.front: A murder suspect has been arraigned and has counsel in two cities; on a long car ride, a detective tells him the little girl's parents deserve to give her a proper burial before the snow falls — and the suspect leads them to the body. Which case?
cards.back:
**Brewer v. Williams (1977)**
Holding: The detective's "Christian burial speech" was the functional equivalent of interrogation and deliberately elicited incriminating statements after the Sixth Amendment right had attached (post-arraignment), with no valid waiver — a Massiah violation.
Signature: the "Christian burial speech"; deliberate elicitation need not be formal questioning.
Why it matters: extended Massiah to a clever, indirect appeal; the statements were suppressed (the body itself later came in under inevitable discovery in Nix v. Williams).
⚠ Don't confuse with **Rhode Island v. Innis** (5A Miranda "functional equivalent of interrogation" — different amendment, custody-triggered) — Brewer is 6A, triggered by the arraignment.

cards.front: The government pays a jailhouse informant — placed in the indicted defendant's cellblock and told to keep his ears open — and he engages the defendant in conversation that draws out incriminating remarks. ACTIVE informant. Which case?
cards.back:
**United States v. Henry (1980)**
Holding: By intentionally creating a situation likely to induce the indicted defendant to make incriminating statements, the government (through a paid informant who was ostensibly just a fellow inmate) "deliberately elicited" them in violation of the Sixth Amendment.
Signature: three factors — paid government informant, posing as a mere fellow inmate, while the accused was in custody under indictment.
Why it matters: applies Massiah to active jailhouse informants — you cannot do indirectly (via a planted agent) what you cannot do directly.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Kuhlmann v. Wilson** — Henry's informant actively drew the defendant out (violation); Kuhlmann's informant merely listened (no violation).

cards.front: A jailhouse informant is planted in the cell of an indicted defendant but is told only to listen — he asks no questions about the crime and makes no effort to draw the defendant out — and the defendant spontaneously confesses. PASSIVE informant. Which case?
cards.back:
**Kuhlmann v. Wilson (1986)**
Holding: A defendant does not make out a Sixth Amendment violation merely by showing an informant reported his statements; he must show the informant took some action, beyond merely listening, designed deliberately to elicit them. A pure "listening post" is permissible.
Signature: the "listening post" rule — passive listening is not deliberate elicitation.
Why it matters: marks the boundary of Massiah/Henry — the line is active elicitation vs. passive overhearing.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Henry** — same jailhouse-informant setup, opposite result: Henry's agent actively prompted the defendant (violation); Kuhlmann's only listened (no violation).

cards.front: A charged codefendant turns informant and wears a wire to a meeting the DEFENDANT himself requested to plan trial strategy; police knowingly use that meeting to record incriminating statements. Which case?
cards.back:
**Maine v. Moulton (1985)**
Holding: The Sixth Amendment is violated when the State knowingly exploits an opportunity to confront the accused without counsel — it makes no difference that the meeting was set up by the defendant, not the police, once the right has attached.
Signature: bars the "knowing exploitation by the State" of any opportunity to confront the accused without counsel after charges.
Why it matters: extends Massiah to government exploitation of a chance encounter; the affirmative-vs.-passive distinction does not save knowing exploitation.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Kuhlmann** (a passive listening post is fine) — in Moulton the informant actively engaged and police knowingly used him.

cards.front: Two robbery suspects are arrested and shown to the victim at a stationhouse showup — but they have NOT yet been indicted or arraigned, and no counsel is present. The Court asks: had any formal charge been filed? Which case?
cards.back:
**Kirby v. Illinois (1972)**
Holding: The Sixth Amendment right to counsel attaches only at or after the initiation of adversary judicial criminal proceedings (formal charge, preliminary hearing, indictment, information, or arraignment). A pre-charge identification is not a critical stage requiring counsel.
Signature: the right-to-counsel "attachment" rule — no counsel at a pre-indictment ID.
Why it matters: cabins Wade/Gilbert — the lineup-counsel right exists only after charges; it is the timing anchor for the whole 6A interrogation/ID line.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Wade/Gilbert** (counsel required at a POST-charge lineup) — Kirby says no counsel at a PRE-charge ID; and with **Rothgery** on exactly when the right attaches.

cards.front: A man is brought before a magistrate after a warrantless arrest, told of the accusation, and committed to jail (a "magistrate's hearing"); no prosecutor is involved yet. The Court fixes the precise moment the 6A right to counsel attaches. Which case?
cards.back:
**Rothgery v. Gillespie County (2008)**
Holding: A criminal defendant's initial appearance before a magistrate, where he learns the charge against him and his liberty is restricted, marks the attachment of the Sixth Amendment right to counsel — even if no prosecutor is aware of or involved in it.
Signature: attachment = the first appearance / initial arraignment before a judicial officer.
Why it matters: pins down Kirby's "initiation of adversary proceedings" to a concrete event; attachment does not require prosecutorial involvement.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Kirby** — Kirby establishes THAT the right attaches at formal proceedings; Rothgery specifies WHEN (the first appearance before a magistrate).

cards.front: A man is charged with BURGLARY and has a lawyer for it; while free on bond he is questioned about the still-uncharged MURDERS connected to that same burglary, and confesses. Is the burglary lawyer's right to counsel a shield for the murders? Which case?
cards.back:
**Texas v. Cobb (2001)**
