Holding: The Sixth Amendment right to counsel is offense-specific; it attaches only to the charged offense and does not extend to other, uncharged offenses — even factually related ones (using the Blockburger same-elements test to define "offense").
Signature: "offense-specific" right to counsel.
Why it matters: police may question a represented defendant about different, uncharged crimes without violating the 6A; narrows the protection's reach.
⚠ Don't confuse with **McNeil v. Wisconsin** (6A is offense-specific in the waiver context) — Cobb defines the offense via Blockburger, refusing a broad "closely related" reading.

cards.front: After counsel is appointed at a preliminary hearing, police read the defendant his Miranda rights and he agrees to go along on a trip to find the murder weapon, writing an inculpatory letter en route. The Court overrules Michigan v. Jackson. Which case?
cards.back:
**Montejo v. Louisiana (2009)**
Holding: A defendant may validly waive his Sixth Amendment right to counsel during police-initiated interrogation even after counsel has been appointed; the Court overruled Michigan v. Jackson's rule that such a waiver was presumptively invalid.
Signature: overruled Michigan v. Jackson; Miranda/Edwards-style waiver rules now do the protective work.
Why it matters: a represented, charged defendant can waive 6A counsel after warnings — the protection runs through voluntary-waiver doctrine, not an automatic bar.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Edwards v. Arizona** (5A: once a suspect invokes counsel, police may not re-initiate) — Montejo is the 6A side and rejects an automatic post-appointment bar.

cards.front: After indictment, a bank-robbery defendant is placed in a lineup — wearing strips of tape on his face and made to say the robber's words — with no notice to his lawyer. Was that lineup a "critical stage"? Which case?
cards.back:
**United States v. Wade (1967)**
Holding: A post-indictment lineup is a critical stage of the prosecution at which the accused has a Sixth Amendment right to counsel; counsel's absence may taint a later in-court identification unless the in-court ID has an independent source.
Signature: the post-charge lineup as a "critical stage" requiring counsel.
Why it matters: anchors the right-to-counsel branch of eyewitness-ID law; remedy is an independent-source inquiry for the in-court ID.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Gilbert** (companion case — applies a PER SE exclusion to the lineup-identification testimony itself) and **Kirby** (no counsel right at a PRE-charge ID).

cards.front: Wade's companion case: at trial, witnesses testify that they identified the defendant at an uncounseled post-indictment lineup. The Court applies a strict, automatic exclusion to that lineup-ID testimony. Which case?
cards.back:
**Gilbert v. California (1967)**
Holding: Testimony that a witness identified the accused at an uncounseled post-indictment lineup must be excluded per se — a strict rule (no harmless-error / reliability cure) to deter Sixth Amendment violations at lineups.
Signature: the per se exclusionary rule for testimony about the uncounseled lineup itself.
Why it matters: the enforcement teeth of Wade — the lineup-ID evidence is automatically out, while a later in-court ID survives only with an independent source.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Wade** — Wade allows an in-court ID if independently sourced (taint test); Gilbert flatly bars testimony about the uncounseled lineup (per se).

cards.front: A stabbing victim, hospitalized and possibly dying, is shown a single handcuffed suspect at her bedside — a one-on-one "showup," not a lineup — and identifies him. The Court judges it not by a counsel rule but by due process. Which case?
cards.back:
**Stovall v. Denno (1967)**
Holding: A confrontation that is unnecessarily suggestive and conducive to irreparable mistaken identification can violate due process; admissibility turns on the totality of the circumstances (here the bedside showup was justified by the witness's critical condition).
Signature: the due-process "unnecessarily suggestive / totality of the circumstances" test for IDs.
Why it matters: opens the due-process branch of ID law (distinct from Wade's counsel branch); Wade/Gilbert were also held non-retroactive here.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Wade/Gilbert** (6A right-to-counsel branch) — Stovall is the DUE PROCESS branch, applying to showups and pre-charge IDs where no counsel right exists.

cards.front: A rape victim identifies her attacker at a suggestive one-man showup months later — yet she had seen him up close for a long time during the crime and gave a detailed description. The Court lists FIVE factors to decide if the ID is still reliable. Which case?
cards.back:
**Neil v. Biggers (1972)**
Holding: Even an unnecessarily suggestive identification is admissible if, under the totality of the circumstances, it is nonetheless reliable; reliability is judged by five factors.
Signature: the five reliability factors — (1) the witness's opportunity to view the criminal, (2) degree of attention, (3) accuracy of the prior description, (4) level of certainty, and (5) the time between the crime and the confrontation.
Why it matters: suggestiveness alone does not exclude an ID — reliability can save it; supplies the operative test.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Stovall** (whether the procedure was unnecessarily suggestive) — Biggers supplies the next step: the five-factor reliability check that can admit a suggestive ID anyway.

cards.front: An undercover officer is shown a SINGLE photo of the suspected drug dealer and later identifies him from it. The Court refuses a per se exclusion of suggestive IDs and declares reliability the "linchpin." Which case?
cards.back:
**Manson v. Brathwaite (1977)**
Holding: There is no per se rule excluding identifications from unnecessarily suggestive procedures; reliability is the linchpin, assessed under the Biggers five-factor totality test and weighed against the corrupting effect of the suggestive procedure.
Signature: "reliability is the linchpin" — adopts the totality/reliability approach over a per se bar.
Why it matters: the controlling modern framework for due-process challenges to suggestive eyewitness IDs (built on Biggers).
⚠ Don't confuse with **Gilbert's** per se rule — that is the 6A counsel branch (uncounseled lineup); Manson REJECTS a per se rule for the due-process suggestiveness branch, favoring reliability.

cards.front: A witness, looking out her window, spontaneously points to a man standing next to a police officer in a parking lot as the thief — the suggestiveness came from the circumstances, not from anything the police arranged. Does due process require screening this ID? Which case?
cards.back:
**Perry v. New Hampshire (2012)**
Holding: The Due Process Clause requires a preliminary judicial screening of eyewitness-identification reliability ONLY when the suggestive circumstances were arranged by law enforcement; absent improper police conduct, reliability is left to the ordinary safeguards of trial (cross-examination, instructions, the jury).
Signature: due-process ID screening is triggered by POLICE-arranged suggestiveness, not by happenstance.
Why it matters: caps the Stovall/Biggers/Manson due-process inquiry — no special screening for accidentally suggestive IDs.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Biggers/Manson** (reliability factors that apply once a suggestive police procedure is in play) — Perry says you never reach those factors unless the police created the suggestive circumstances.

cards.front: During Prohibition, a federal agent posing as a fellow World War veteran works on the defendant's wartime camaraderie and, after repeated and persistent solicitation, finally talks the otherwise law-abiding man into getting him a half-gallon of whiskey. Which case first recognized the entrapment defense?
cards.back:
**Sorrells v. United States (1932)**
Holding: Entrapment is a valid defense; it arises when government officials implant the criminal design in the mind of a person who had no previous disposition to commit the offense and then lure that "otherwise innocent" person into committing it.
Signature: the founding case of the entrapment defense; the controlling question is whether the defendant "is a person otherwise innocent whom the Government is seeking to punish for an alleged offense which is the product of the creative activity of its own officials."
Why it matters: origin of the FEDERAL SUBJECTIVE test — the inquiry turns on the defendant's predisposition, not on the propriety of police conduct. The majority's predisposition focus over Roberts' concurrence (which urged an objective, government-conduct test) launches the subjective-vs-objective split.
⚠ Don't confuse with the OBJECTIVE test (Roberts' Sorrells concurrence / Model Penal Code) — that minority approach, adopted by some STATES, asks whether police methods would induce a hypothetical law-abiding person; it is NOT the federal rule.

cards.front: A government informant meets the defendant in a doctor's office where both are being treated for drug addiction, plays on their shared struggle to get clean, and after repeated pleas finally persuades the recovering addict to obtain narcotics for him. Which case reaffirmed the subjective entrapment test?
cards.back:
**Sherman v. United States (1958)**
Holding: Entrapment is established as a matter of law when the government, through its informant, implants the criminal design in an otherwise-unwilling person and induces the crime; the defendant's lack of predisposition was clear (he was a recovering addict pressured by a fellow patient).
Signature: the line is between "the unwary innocent and the unwary criminal" — "a different question is presented when the criminal design originates with the officials of the Government, and they implant in the mind of an innocent person the disposition to commit the alleged offense and induce its commission."
Why it matters: unanimously reaffirms the FEDERAL SUBJECTIVE/predisposition test 26 years after Sorrells; the controlling articulation later courts quote.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Sorrells** (the originating case) — Sherman is the reaffirmation; like Sorrells, a four-Justice concurrence (Frankfurter) again pressed the objective test, which the majority again declined.

cards.front: An undercover agent supplies a defendant with a hard-to-obtain but perfectly legal chemical (phenyl-2-propanone) needed to manufacture methamphetamine, where the defendant was already in the drug business and predisposed. In a 5-4 ruling, which case kept the subjective test and rejected the objective approach?
cards.back:
**United States v. Russell (1973)**
Holding: There is no entrapment where the defendant was predisposed, even though a government agent supplied a difficult-to-obtain but legal ingredient for the crime; the Court (5-4) declined to overturn the predisposition test and rejected the proposed objective test.
Signature: the entrapment defense was "not intended to give the federal judiciary a 'chancellor's foot' veto over law enforcement practices of which it did not approve"; the Court was "content to leave the matter where it was left by the Court in Sherman."
Why it matters: cements the FEDERAL SUBJECTIVE/predisposition test as settled law; Stewart's dissent (joined by 3) lays out the OBJECTIVE/government-conduct test, sharpening the subjective-vs-objective divide.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Jacobson** (no predisposition INDEPENDENT of the government's own inducement → entrapment) — in Russell the predisposition pre-existed, so the defense failed. Pair with **Hampton** (agent supplied contraband, not just a legal ingredient — still no defense).

cards.front: Over 26 months, postal inspectors and fictitious organizations repeatedly mail a Nebraska farmer solicitations and pseudo-political appeals about sexual freedom until he finally orders child pornography — conduct that had been legal when he earlier bought similar magazines. Which case found entrapment as a matter of law?
cards.back:
**Jacobson v. United States (1992)**
Holding: Where the government induces the crime, it must prove the defendant was predisposed to commit it INDEPENDENT of, and PRIOR TO, the government's inducement; 26 months of government solicitation that itself created the predisposition means the prosecution failed as a matter of law, so entrapment is established.
Signature: the government "overstepped the line between setting a trap for the 'unwary innocent' and the 'unwary criminal'" — predisposition must exist before the government "came on the scene," not be the product of its own campaign.
Why it matters: the modern showpiece of the subjective test — sharpens Sorrells/Sherman by demanding predisposition be independent and pre-existing, and reverses a conviction the jury had upheld.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Russell/Hampton** (predisposition pre-existed → defense FAILS). The discriminator: in Jacobson the government's own long campaign CREATED the predisposition, so it was not independent.

cards.front: A government informant both supplies the heroin AND arranges its sale to undercover agents, but the defendant conceded he was predisposed to deal drugs. He argues that the government supplying the very contraband should bar conviction on due-process grounds. Which case rejected that argument?
cards.back:
**Hampton v. United States (1976)**
Holding: Neither the entrapment defense nor the Due Process Clause bars conviction of a PREDISPOSED defendant even where a government agent supplied the contraband (heroin) the defendant then sold; his conceded predisposition defeated the entrapment defense.
Signature: a predisposed defendant's "remedy . . . lies solely in the defense of entrapment" — and that defense is unavailable once predisposition is conceded (Rehnquist plurality).
Why it matters: pushes Russell's logic to its limit — even government supply of the actual contraband does not raise an outrageous-government-conduct due-process bar for a predisposed defendant; predisposition controls.
⚠ Note: a PLURALITY (Rehnquist, 3 Justices) would foreclose any due-process bar; Powell/Blackmun concurred in the judgment but left open that truly outrageous police conduct might someday bar conviction even of a predisposed defendant. Don't confuse with **Russell** (legal ingredient supplied) — Hampton goes further: actual contraband supplied, still no defense.

cards.front: Officers arrest a man in his home for burglary, then search his entire three-bedroom house — including drawers in other rooms — without a warrant. The Court says the search was too broad: incident to arrest, police may search only the arrestee and the area within his reach. Which case?
cards.back:
**Chimel v. California (1969)**
Holding: A search incident to arrest is limited to the arrestee's person and the area "within his immediate control" — the "wingspan" from which he might grab a weapon or destroy evidence.
Signature: the "wingspan"/"area of immediate control" rule; twin rationales = officer safety + evidence preservation.
Why it matters: the foundational scope rule for SITA; the engine *Gant* and *Robinson* are built on.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Robinson** (full search of the *person* on any custodial arrest, no extra showing) or **Gant** (the *vehicle* version of the reach/officer-safety rule).

cards.front: An officer arrests a driver for operating after revocation, then does a full search of his person and finds heroin in a crumpled cigarette package. No reason to think he was armed — but the Court upholds it: a lawful custodial arrest by itself authorizes a full search of the person. Which case?
cards.back:
**United States v. Robinson (1973)**
Holding: A lawful custodial arrest categorically permits a *full search of the arrestee's person* — no case-by-case showing that weapons or evidence are present.
Signature: "It is the fact of the lawful arrest which establishes the authority to search." 414 U.S. at 235.
Why it matters: the bright-line search-of-the-person rule; unlike a *Terry* frisk, the arrest alone is enough.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Chimel** (wingspan/area of control), **Knowles** (a mere citation gives NO search authority), or **Riley** (phone *data* still needs a warrant).

cards.front: An officer stops a driver for speeding and writes a citation instead of arresting him — then conducts a full search of the car anyway, citing a state "search incident to citation" statute. The Court rejects it. Which case?
cards.back:
**Knowles v. Iowa (1998)**
Holding: There is no "search incident to *citation*" — where the officer issues a ticket rather than making a custodial arrest, the search-incident authority never attaches.
Signature: declined to extend *Robinson*'s bright line where neither officer-safety nor evidence-preservation concerns are present.
Why it matters: the custodial-arrest predicate has teeth — no custody, no SITA.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Robinson** (a *custodial* arrest = full search) — the difference is whether the person was actually arrested or just cited.

cards.front: After a lawful arrest of a car's occupant, the Court announces a bright-line rule that police may search the whole passenger compartment and any containers in it — setting the SCOPE of a vehicle search incident to arrest (later narrowed by Gant). Which case?
cards.back:
**New York v. Belton (1981)**
Holding: On a lawful custodial arrest of a vehicle occupant, police may search the passenger compartment and any containers found within it.
Signature: the old bright-line vehicle-compartment search — Belton sets the *where* (the room).
Why it matters: defines SITA *scope* for vehicles, but its trigger was cabined by *Gant* — "Gant is the gate, Belton is the room."
⚠ Don't confuse with **Gant** (the *trigger/when*: only if the arrestee is unsecured & within reach, OR evidence of the crime of arrest may be in the car). After *Gant*, *Belton* no longer authorizes the search by itself.

cards.front: A man parks and gets out of his car before the officer reaches him; he is then arrested. The Court holds the vehicle-search-incident rule still applies because he was a "recent occupant." Scalia's concurrence floats the evidence-of-the-offense idea that a later case adopts. Which case?
cards.back:
**Thornton v. United States (2004)**
Holding: The *Belton* vehicle-search-incident rule reaches "recent occupants," not just people seized inside the car.
Signature: "recent occupant"; Scalia's concurrence proposed limiting these searches to where evidence of the crime of arrest might be found.
Why it matters: the bridge case — *Belton* (scope/occupants) → *Thornton* (recent occupants + the evidentiary idea) → *Gant* (the two-prong gate).
⚠ Don't confuse with **Gant** — *Gant* adopted Scalia's *Thornton* concurrence as binding law (the second prong).

cards.front: Police arrest a driver for a suspended license, handcuff him, lock him in the back of the patrol car, then search the passenger compartment of his car. The Court suppresses what they found. Which case?
cards.back:
**Arizona v. Gant (2009)**
Holding: Police may search a vehicle incident to a recent occupant's arrest only if (1) the arrestee is unsecured and within reaching distance of the compartment, OR (2) it is reasonable to believe the vehicle contains evidence of the offense of arrest.
Signature: the two-prong gate that narrowed the broad reading of *Belton*.
Why it matters: the controlling rule for vehicle SITA — cuffing-and-securing usually kills prong 1, and a suspended-license arrest rarely supplies prong 2.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Belton** (old bright-line: search the compartment on any occupant's arrest) or **Riley** (phone data needs a warrant).

cards.front: Which case held that police must get a warrant before searching the digital contents of a cell phone seized incident to arrest — answering the question "what must police do?" with "get a warrant"?
cards.back:
**Riley v. California (2014)**
Holding: The search-incident-to-arrest bright line does NOT extend to the digital data on a cell phone — officers must generally get a warrant.
Signature: "Our answer ... is accordingly simple — get a warrant." 573 U.S. at 403.
Why it matters: digital is different; the *Chimel*/*Robinson* rationales (weapon, evidence destruction) don't transfer to data. Police may still seize the phone and inspect the physical device.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Robinson** (full search of the *physical* person/effects is fine) — *Riley* carves out the phone's *data*. Pairs with **Birchfield** (also limits SITA's reach to a new context).

cards.front: On a DUI arrest, the Court splits chemical testing in two: a warrantless BREATH test is OK incident to arrest, but a warrantless BLOOD draw is not — it needs a warrant. Which case?
cards.back:
**Birchfield v. North Dakota (2016)**
Holding: A warrantless breath test may be administered as a search incident to a DUI arrest; a warrantless blood test may not (it needs a warrant or another exception).
Signature: breath = yes, blood = no — because a breath test is far less intrusive.
Why it matters: applies the SITA reasonableness balance to DUI chemical testing and limits it for blood.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Riley** (both limit SITA's reach — *Riley* to phone data, *Birchfield* to blood draws) or **Schmerber**-style exigency theories (blood can still be drawn under a warrant or another exception, just not on the arrest alone).

cards.front: An arrestee booked into custody on a serious felony has his cheek swabbed for DNA, which is run through a database and links him to an unsolved crime. The Court upholds the swab as a legitimate booking procedure — like fingerprinting. Which case?
cards.back:
**Maryland v. King (2013)**
Holding: Taking a buccal (cheek) DNA swab from a person arrested for a serious offense and held in custody is a reasonable booking procedure under the Fourth Amendment.
Signature: DNA swab "like fingerprinting and photographing" — a *booking* rationale, not search-incident-of-the-person.
Why it matters: extends routine booking-identification practices to DNA for serious offenses.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Robinson** (search of the person justified by the *arrest*) — *King* rests on the booking/identification rationale, sitting beside **Lafayette** (booking inventory), not the wingspan search.

cards.front: Prohibition-era agents stop a car on the highway, tear into the upholstery, and find bootleg liquor — without a warrant. The Court upholds it, founding the rule that a readily mobile vehicle may be searched on probable cause because it can be driven away before a warrant issues. Which case?
cards.back:
**Carroll v. United States (1925)**
Holding: A readily mobile vehicle may be searched without a warrant on probable cause, because it can be quickly moved out of the jurisdiction before a warrant can be obtained.
Signature: the *origin* of the automobile exception; ready mobility as the founding rationale.
Why it matters: the first and foundational automobile-exception case — every later vehicle case traces back to it.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Chambers** (you don't have to search at the roadside — you can search later at the station) or **Ross** (how far the search reaches once you have PC).

cards.front: Police have probable cause and mobility at the scene but tow the car to the station, then search it without a warrant there. The Court holds the later station-house search is just as reasonable as a roadside one. Which case?
cards.back:
**Chambers v. Maroney (1970)**
Holding: Where probable cause and mobility existed at the scene, officers may search the vehicle without a warrant later at the station house — immobilizing the car until a warrant issues is no better.
Signature: "little to choose ... between an immediate search without a warrant and the car's immobilization until a warrant is obtained." 399 U.S. at 52.
Why it matters: the delayed-search rule — mobility is judged at the scene, and the justification persists.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Carroll** (the origin/roadside search) or **Collins** (no entry of a home's curtilage to reach a car). And don't assume the converse — a warrant is NOT suddenly required just because the car is impounded.

cards.front: With probable cause to search a car for drugs, officers open the trunk and a brown paper bag inside it. The Court holds the search may reach every part of the vehicle and every container in it where the object might be found. Which case?
cards.back:
**United States v. Ross (1982)**
Holding: If probable cause justifies searching a lawfully stopped vehicle, it justifies searching every part of the vehicle and every container within it that may conceal the object of the search.
Signature: scope is as broad as a magistrate could authorize by warrant — scope tracks PC.
Why it matters: defines the *reach* of the automobile exception; PC defines scope (PC for a stolen TV won't justify opening a pill bottle).
⚠ Don't confuse with **Acevedo** (PC focused on a *specific container* placed in the car — search that container) — *Ross* is PC as to the *whole car*. Pairs with **Houghton** (reach extends to a passenger's belongings).

cards.front: Officers watch a man place a paper bag they have probable cause to believe holds marijuana into his car trunk, then stop the car and open just that bag. The Court adopts "one rule to govern all automobile searches" and overrules the old footlocker/luggage distinction. Which case?
cards.back:
**California v. Acevedo (1991)**
Holding: Police may search a container in a car without a warrant whenever they have probable cause to believe it holds contraband or evidence — one unified rule for all automobile searches.
Signature: "one rule to govern all automobile searches"; overruled *Arkansas v. Sanders*.
Why it matters: erased the anomaly under which PC as to a *specific container* in a car had required a warrant while PC as to the *whole car* did not.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Ross** (PC as to the *whole vehicle* → search everything). And note: *Acevedo* swept away the overruled **Chadwick/Sanders** footlocker-and-luggage rule that once demanded a warrant for a container police had PC to search before it entered the car.

cards.front: Police have probable cause to search a car for drugs and search a female passenger's purse — even though she is not a suspect. The Court upholds it: with PC to search the car, officers may search a passenger's belongings capable of concealing the object. Which case?
cards.back:
**Wyoming v. Houghton (1999)**
Holding: With probable cause to search a vehicle, officers may search a passenger's belongings found in the car that are capable of concealing the object of the search.
Signature: a non-suspect passenger's ownership of a bag is no shield.
Why it matters: extends the automobile-exception's container reach to passengers' property.
⚠ Don't confuse the reach — *Houghton* covers a passenger's *belongings*, NOT the passenger's *body/person*, which needs its own PC or a search incident to arrest. Pairs with **Ross** (scope) and **Acevedo** (containers).

cards.front: Agents search a Dodge Mini Motor Home parked in a downtown lot, used by a man trading sex for marijuana. The Court holds the automobile exception applies because it was being used as a vehicle — and names the doctrine's two rationales. Which case?
cards.back:
**California v. Carney (1985)**
Holding: The automobile exception applies to a motor home being used as a vehicle; the exception rests on two rationales — (1) ready mobility and (2) a reduced expectation of privacy from pervasive regulation of motor vehicles.
Signature: "readily mobile by the turn of an ignition key" + "reduced expectation of privacy." 471 U.S. at 392-93.
Why it matters: the canonical statement of the exception's *two* justifications; covers motor homes in use as vehicles.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Collins** (a vehicle parked within the home's curtilage is off-limits) — *Carney*'s motor home was in a public lot, mobile and regulated as a vehicle.

cards.front: Customs agents seize packages from a pickup, store them in a DEA warehouse, and open them three days later without a warrant. The Court upholds the delayed search — immobilization didn't end the justification. Which case?
cards.back:
**United States v. Johns (1985)**
Holding: A warrantless search of packages lawfully removed from a vehicle on probable cause is valid even when delayed (here, three days later); immobilizing the contents does not end the automobile-exception justification.
Signature: the "three days later" delayed-search case.
Why it matters: the binding SCOTUS anchor for delay — confirms *Chambers* that a later search is fine.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Chambers** (delayed search at the *station*) — *Johns* extends the same logic to *packages* opened days after seizure. (4th Cir.'s *Gastiaburo* pushes it to 38 days as persuasive illustration.)

cards.front: A state court demanded proof of EXTRA exigent circumstances beyond mobility before allowing a warrantless car search. The Supreme Court reversed per curiam: readily mobile + probable cause permits a search "without more." Which case?
cards.back:
**Pennsylvania v. Labron (1996)** (per curiam)
Holding: If a car is readily mobile and probable cause exists to believe it contains contraband, the Fourth Amendment permits a warrantless search "without more" — there is no separate exigency requirement.
Signature: "the Fourth Amendment thus permits police to search the vehicle without more." 518 U.S. at 940.
Why it matters: confirms the automobile exception has no separate exigency element beyond mobility + PC (reaffirmed in *Maryland v. Dyson*).
⚠ Don't confuse with **Carroll** (origin) — *Labron* answers a different question: whether you must show *extra* exigency (you don't).

cards.front: An officer walks up a residential driveway, lifts a tarp covering a motorcycle parked near the house, and confirms it was stolen — no warrant. The Court suppresses it: the automobile exception does not let police enter the home's curtilage to reach a vehicle. Which case?
cards.back:
**Collins v. Virginia (2018)**
Holding: The automobile exception does not permit the warrantless entry of a home or its curtilage in order to search a vehicle parked there.
Signature: the motorcycle-under-a-tarp-in-the-driveway case.
Why it matters: the exception reaches the *vehicle*, not the constitutionally protected ground it sits on — a car in the curtilage needs a warrant or another exception.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Carney** (motor home in a *public* lot — exception applies) — the deciding fact is location: curtilage vs. a public/roadside spot.

cards.front: A car is impounded for parking violations; police inventory its contents under standard procedure and find marijuana in the unlocked glove box. The Court upholds the routine impound inventory as reasonable caretaking, not investigation. Which case?
cards.back:
**South Dakota v. Opperman (1976)**
Holding: An inventory search of a lawfully impounded vehicle, conducted under standard police procedures and not as a pretext for investigation, is reasonable under the Fourth Amendment.
Signature: the foundational *routine impound inventory* case.
Why it matters: first leg of the inventory trilogy — establishes inventory as an administrative caretaking exception, distinct from SITA and the automobile exception.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Wells** (an inventory can't be a *ruse* for rummaging) or **Bertine** (inventory may open *containers* under standardized criteria) — *Opperman* is the baseline "standard procedure = reasonable" case.

cards.front: After a DUI arrest, police inventory the van and open a closed backpack inside, finding drugs and cash. The Court upholds opening the container — inventory discretion is fine so long as it follows standardized criteria, not a hunch about evidence. Which case?
cards.back:
**Colorado v. Bertine (1987)**
Holding: An inventory may include opening closed containers, so long as police discretion is exercised according to standardized criteria and not on suspicion of evidence of crime.
Signature: inventories "conducted according to standardized criteria"; containers may be opened.
Why it matters: second leg of the inventory trilogy — extends the inventory to containers while cabining discretion.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Wells** (no *standardized policy* on opening containers → suppression) or **Opperman** (the impound-baseline). *Bertine* allows container-opening *because* a standardized criterion governed it.

cards.front: Police open a locked suitcase in an impounded car and find marijuana — but the department had NO policy on opening closed containers during inventories. The Court suppresses: an inventory must follow standardized criteria and can't be a ruse for rummaging. Which case?
cards.back:
**Florida v. Wells (1990)**
Holding: An inventory search must not be a ruse for general rummaging; the policy governing it must be designed to produce an inventory, and absent any standardized criteria on opening containers, the suitcase search was unlawful.
Signature: "an inventory search must not be a ruse for a general rummaging in order to discover incriminating evidence." 495 U.S. at 4.
Why it matters: third leg of the inventory trilogy — the crispest statement of the "standardized criteria, not a ruse" guardrail.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Bertine** (container-opening upheld *because* standardized criteria existed) — *Wells* suppressed *because* none did. Same doctrine, opposite facts.

cards.front: At the stationhouse during booking, police search the contents of an arrestee's shoulder bag per routine procedure and find drugs. The Court upholds it as part of the routine incident to incarceration — a booking inventory, not a search incident to arrest. Which case?
cards.back:
**Illinois v. Lafayette (1983)**
Holding: As part of the routine procedure incident to incarcerating an arrested person, police may search any container or article in his possession in accordance with established inventory procedures.
Signature: the *booking/stationhouse inventory* case.
Why it matters: extends the inventory rationale to an arrestee's effects at booking — administratively distinct from SITA.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Robinson** (a search of the person justified by the *arrest*) — *Lafayette* rests on the *booking-inventory* rationale, alongside **King** (booking DNA swab), not the wingspan/SITA theory.

cards.front: Officers searching a home under a warrant for stolen rings fully expect to also find the robbery weapons — and do, in plain view. The Court holds the seizure valid even though the discovery was not inadvertent: inadvertence is NOT a requirement for plain view. Which case?
cards.back:
**Horton v. California (1990)**
Holding: A plain-view seizure is lawful when (1) the officer is lawfully positioned to view the item, (2) its incriminating character is "immediately apparent" (= probable cause, no manipulation), and (3) the officer has a lawful right of access — and *inadvertence is NOT required*.
Signature: killed the inadvertent-discovery requirement; the modern three-prong plain-view test.
Why it matters: the controlling plain-view *seizure* test; an officer who expects to find the item may still seize it.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Coolidge** (the *origin*, which had *required* inadvertence — abandoned here) or **Hicks** ("immediately apparent" = PC, and *moving* an item to develop PC is a separate search).

cards.front: Lawfully inside an apartment after a shooting, an officer suspects expensive stereo equipment is stolen, so he moves it a few inches to read the serial numbers. The Court holds that moving it was a separate search needing probable cause. Which case?
cards.back:
**Arizona v. Hicks (1987)**
Holding: Moving stereo equipment to read its serial number was a "search" separate from the lawful entry, and plain view's "immediately apparent" requirement means probable cause — mere suspicion is not enough.
Signature: "a truly cursory inspection — one that involves merely looking at what is already exposed to view, without disturbing it — is not a 'search.'" 480 U.S. at 328.
Why it matters: defines "immediately apparent" (= PC) and the manipulation line — develop PC by moving an item and you have searched.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Horton** (the three-prong seizure test) — *Hicks* polices prong 2: you must have PC *before* moving the item, with no manipulation.

cards.front: Which case is the ORIGIN of the modern plain-view doctrine — a plurality that required a lawful vantage, an "immediately apparent" incriminating nature, and (originally) inadvertent discovery, and warned plain view "may not be used to extend a general exploratory search"?
cards.back:
**Coolidge v. New Hampshire (1971)**
Holding (plurality): Plain view justifies a warrantless seizure only where the incriminating nature is immediately apparent and the officer is lawfully positioned; it "may not be used to extend a general exploratory search from one object to another." Originally also required *inadvertent* discovery.
Signature: the doctrinal origin + the anti-general-search limiting principle.
Why it matters: founds plain view and the no-general-exploratory-search rule (the spine of the digital-warrant debate).
⚠ Don't confuse with **Horton** — *Coolidge*'s inadvertence condition was *abandoned* by *Horton* (1990). State the modern test from *Horton*, not *Coolidge*'s superseded inadvertence prong.

cards.front: Federal marshals enter a man's house without a warrant, seize his private letters and papers, and the government uses them to convict him of running a lottery by mail. The Court holds those papers can't be used against him in FEDERAL court. Which case?
cards.back:
**Weeks v. United States (1914)**
Holding: Evidence obtained by federal officers in violation of the Fourth Amendment is inadmissible against the defendant in federal court — the birth of the federal exclusionary rule.
Signature: without exclusion, the Fourth Amendment "might as well be stricken from the Constitution." 232 U.S. at 393.
Why it matters: the origin of the exclusionary rule; for decades it bound only FEDERAL officers (the "silver platter" gap let states hand evidence up).
⚠ Don't confuse with **Mapp** (extends the rule to the STATES, 1961) or **Wolf** (the 1949 way-station that applied 4A to states but withheld the exclusionary remedy).

cards.front: The Court holds the Fourth Amendment's core — security against arbitrary intrusion by police — applies to the STATES through the Fourteenth Amendment, but refuses (for now) to force the states to use the exclusionary remedy. Which case?
cards.back:
**Wolf v. Colorado (1949)**
Holding: The Fourth Amendment's protection against unreasonable searches binds the states via the Fourteenth Amendment, but the exclusionary rule is NOT (yet) required of state courts — states may choose other remedies.
Signature: the incorporation-without-the-remedy half-step.
Why it matters: the bridge between Weeks and Mapp — recognized the right against the states but left the remedy optional, a gap Mapp would close.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Mapp** (1961), which OVERRULED Wolf's remedy holding and made exclusion mandatory in state courts, or **Weeks** (federal-only origin).

cards.front: Cleveland officers force their way into a woman's home without a valid warrant looking for a bombing suspect, find obscene materials instead, and prosecute her for those. The Court uses the case to make the exclusionary rule binding on the STATES. Which case?
cards.back:
**Mapp v. Ohio (1961)**
Holding: "All evidence obtained by searches and seizures in violation of the Constitution is, by that same authority, inadmissible in a state court." 367 U.S. at 655.
Signature: extended the exclusionary rule to the states; overruled Wolf's remedy holding.
Why it matters: completes the timeline Weeks (federal) → Wolf (right, no remedy) → Mapp (remedy applies to states).
⚠ Don't confuse with **Wolf** (applied 4A to states but WITHOUT the remedy) or **Weeks** (federal officers only).

cards.front: Federal agents illegally seize a lumber company's books, photograph them, then return the originals and try to subpoena the same documents based on what they learned. Justice Holmes says the government can't use illegally obtained knowledge that way. Which case — the origin of the "fruit" principle?
cards.back:
**Silverthorne Lumber Co. v. United States (1920)**
Holding: The government may not use information gained from an unconstitutional search — not even indirectly, by issuing a subpoena for documents it learned of through the illegal seizure.
Signature: Holmes — the knowledge gained illegally cannot be used "at all"; the seed of fruit-of-the-poisonous-tree.
Why it matters: the doctrinal origin later named in Wong Sun; bars derivative use of tainted evidence.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Wong Sun** (1963), which coined the "fruit of the poisonous tree" label and set the exploitation/attenuation test.
cards.cite: Silverthorne Lumber Co. v. United States, 251 U.S. 385 (1920)

cards.front: After an unlawful arrest, one statement is tossed but a later voluntary statement — made days after the suspect was released and came back on his own — is allowed. The Court asks not "but for" the illegality, but whether the evidence was come at by EXPLOITING it. Which case?
cards.back:
**Wong Sun v. United States (1963)**
Holding: Derivative evidence is suppressed only if obtained by exploiting the primary illegality, not merely because it would not have surfaced "but for" the illegality; intervening acts of free will can purge the taint.
Signature: coined "fruit of the poisonous tree"; the test is "exploitation" vs. "means sufficiently distinguishable to be purged of the primary taint." 371 U.S. at 487-488.
Why it matters: the framework for all FOTPT analysis and its exceptions.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Silverthorne** (the older origin of the principle) — Wong Sun is the one that named it and set the exploitation/attenuation test.
cards.cite: Wong Sun v. United States, 371 U.S. 471, 487-88 (1963)

cards.front: A defendant's statements lead police to a murder victim's body — but a volunteer search team was already closing in on the same area and would have found it anyway. The Court lets the body in. Which case?
cards.back:
**Nix v. Williams (1984)**
Holding: Inevitable-discovery exception — unlawfully obtained evidence is admissible if the prosecution proves by a preponderance that it would inevitably have been discovered by lawful means.
Signature: "ultimately or inevitably would have been discovered by lawful means." 467 U.S. at 444.
Why it matters: the "would-have-found-it-anyway" escape hatch from the fruit doctrine.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Murray/Segura** (INDEPENDENT SOURCE — evidence was actually obtained a second, lawful way) — inevitable discovery is hypothetical (it WOULD have been found), independent source is actual.

cards.front: Agents make an illegal entry into a warehouse and see bales of marijuana, then leave, get a warrant based ONLY on prior untainted information, and seize the marijuana on the lawful re-entry. The Court asks whether the warrant was genuinely independent of what they saw. Which case?
cards.back:
**Murray v. United States (1988)**
Holding: Independent-source exception — evidence first seen during an unlawful entry is admissible if later acquired through a genuinely independent lawful source, e.g. a warrant NOT prompted by what was seen and not based on tainted information.
Signature: "genuinely independent source"; invalid if the decision to seek the warrant was prompted by the illegal observation. 487 U.S. at 542.
Why it matters: the actual-second-source twin of inevitable discovery.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Nix** (INEVITABLE discovery — hypothetical lawful discovery) or **Segura** (independent source where the illegal entry just secured the premises while a valid warrant was independently obtained).

cards.front: Agents illegally enter an apartment and stay to secure it for ~19 hours while others get a search warrant — and the warrant rests entirely on information known BEFORE the illegal entry. The Court admits the evidence seized under the warrant. Which case?
cards.back:
**Segura v. United States (1984)**
Holding: Evidence seized under a valid warrant is admissible even after an earlier illegal entry, where the warrant was supported wholly by information known to police independent of (and before) the unlawful entry.
Signature: an illegal entry to secure premises does not taint evidence later seized under a genuinely independent warrant.
Why it matters: an independent-source companion to Murray (decided four years earlier); the illegal entry was harmless because the warrant stood on its own.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Murray** (saw the evidence during the illegal entry, then got an independent warrant) or **Nix** (inevitable discovery, no second lawful acquisition).

cards.front: After an illegal arrest, police give Miranda warnings and the suspect confesses. The State argues the warnings alone purge the taint. The Court rejects that and sets out three factors for whether a confession is purged. Which case?
cards.back:
**Brown v. Illinois (1975)**
Holding: Miranda warnings alone do not automatically purge the taint of an illegal arrest; courts weigh (1) temporal proximity, (2) intervening circumstances, and (3) "particularly, the purpose and flagrancy of the official misconduct." 422 U.S. at 603-604.
Signature: the "Brown factors" for attenuation.
Why it matters: supplies the attenuation test that Strieff later applies.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Strieff**, which APPLIES these very factors and finds attenuation; **Wong Sun** is the parent doctrine. Brown = the factors; Strieff = the application.

cards.front: An officer stops a man with no reasonable suspicion (an unlawful stop), runs his name, finds a valid PRE-EXISTING arrest warrant, arrests him on it, and finds drugs in the search incident to arrest. The Court admits the drugs. Which case?
cards.back:
**Utah v. Strieff (2016)**
Holding: The discovery of a valid, pre-existing arrest warrant during an unlawful stop is an intervening circumstance that attenuates the taint, so evidence found in the ensuing search-incident-to-arrest is admissible.
Signature: the outstanding warrant is "a critical intervening circumstance ... wholly independent of the illegal stop." 579 U.S. at 241 (applying the Brown factors).
Why it matters: the modern attenuation case; an intervening warrant breaks the causal chain.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Brown** (the SOURCE of the factors) or with **Wong Sun** (parent doctrine). Strieff = warrant-attenuates-the-stop.

cards.front: Officers get a search warrant from a neutral magistrate and reasonably rely on it, but a court later decides the affidavit didn't establish probable cause. The Court refuses to suppress — punishing reasonable reliance wouldn't deter anyone. Which case launched the good-faith exception?
cards.back:
**United States v. Leon (1984)**
Holding: Good-faith exception — evidence seized in objectively reasonable reliance on a search warrant later found unsupported by probable cause is admissible; exclusion would not serve the rule's deterrent purpose.
Signature: "objectively reasonable reliance on a subsequently invalidated search warrant." 468 U.S. at 922. Good faith FAILS in four situations (468 U.S. at 923): false-affidavit (Franks), magistrate abandoned neutral role (Lo-Ji Sales), affidavit so lacking in indicia of PC that belief is unreasonable, or a warrant so facially deficient officers can't presume it valid.
Why it matters: the foundation of the entire good-faith family.
⚠ Don't confuse the FLAVORS: Leon (defective WARRANT) vs. Sheppard (wrong warrant FORM) vs. Krull (unconstitutional STATUTE) vs. Evans (COURT clerk's error) vs. Herring (POLICE negligence) vs. Davis (binding PRECEDENT).

cards.front: An officer fills out a warrant on the only form available — a pre-printed "controlled substance" form for a murder investigation — and the judge assures him he'll fix it but doesn't. The warrant is technically defective in FORM, yet the Court admits the evidence. Which case?
cards.back:
**Massachusetts v. Sheppard (1984)**
Holding: Leon's companion — where a warrant is defective only because of a clerical/form error and officers reasonably relied on the issuing judge's assurances, the good-faith exception applies; the mistake was the judge's, not the police's.
Signature: "it was the judge, not the police officers, who made the critical mistake." 468 U.S. at 990.
Why it matters: good faith covers a magistrate's paperwork error on an otherwise reasonable search.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Leon** (warrant lacked probable cause) — Sheppard is the wrong-FORM twin decided the same day. Both turn on officers reasonably relying on the magistrate.

cards.front: An officer conducts a warrantless administrative search authorized by a state STATUTE that a court later strikes down as unconstitutional. The Court admits the evidence — the officer can't be expected to second-guess the legislature. Which case?
cards.back:
**Illinois v. Krull (1987)**
Holding: Good-faith reliance on a STATUTE later held unconstitutional does not trigger exclusion; suppression would not deter an officer who reasonably relied on a facially valid law.
Signature: "Unless a statute is clearly unconstitutional, an officer cannot be expected to question the judgment of the legislature." 480 U.S. at 349-350.
Why it matters: extends Leon's good-faith logic from warrants to statutes.
⚠ Don't confuse the good-faith flavors: Krull = unconstitutional STATUTE; Leon = defective WARRANT; Evans = COURT-clerk error; Herring = POLICE negligence; Davis = binding PRECEDENT.

cards.front: An officer arrests a driver because the patrol-car computer shows an outstanding warrant — but the warrant had been quashed weeks earlier and a COURT clerk simply never deleted it. The Court admits the drugs found in the search. Which case?
cards.back:
**Arizona v. Evans (1995)**
Holding: The good-faith exception covers evidence seized on a mistaken arrest record caused by clerical errors of COURT employees; the arresting officer relied objectively reasonably on the police computer.
Signature: "a categorical exception to the exclusionary rule for clerical errors of court employees." 514 U.S. at 16.
Why it matters: extends good faith to a court clerk's recordkeeping mistake (contrast Herring, where the error was the POLICE's own).
⚠ Don't confuse with **Herring** — Evans = COURT clerk's error; Herring = POLICE's own negligent recordkeeping. Both still admit the evidence, but for different actors.

cards.front: Officers arrest a man relying on a neighboring county's warrant database showing an active warrant — but the warrant had been recalled and the POLICE in that county failed to update their own records. The Court admits the evidence. Which case?
cards.back:
**Herring v. United States (2009)**
Holding: Isolated, attenuated POLICE negligence (a recordkeeping error) does not trigger exclusion; suppression is warranted only where deterrence benefits outweigh costs.
Signature: to trigger the rule, conduct must be "sufficiently deliberate ... and sufficiently culpable" — deliberate, reckless, grossly negligent, or systemic. 555 U.S. at 144.
Why it matters: pushed the rule toward a pure cost-benefit, culpability-focused inquiry; mere negligence isn't enough.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Evans** (the error was a COURT clerk's, not the police's). Herring = the police's OWN negligence; both still admit, but Herring narrows exclusion to culpable police conduct.

cards.front: Police search a car incident to arrest in a way that was lawful under binding appellate precedent at the time — but while the case is on appeal, the Supreme Court changes the rule (Gant). The Court refuses to suppress. Which case?
cards.back:
**Davis v. United States (2011)**
Holding: Searches conducted in objectively reasonable reliance on binding appellate precedent are not subject to the exclusionary rule, even if that precedent is later overruled.
Signature: good-faith reliance on then-binding PRECEDENT; no deterrence value in punishing officers who followed the law as it stood.
Why it matters: the precedent-reliance branch of the good-faith family (here, a pre-Gant Belton-style vehicle search).
⚠ Don't confuse the flavors: Davis = binding PRECEDENT; Leon = defective WARRANT; Krull = unconstitutional STATUTE; Evans = COURT-clerk error; Herring = POLICE negligence.

cards.front: Police have a valid search warrant but burst in after waiting only 3-5 seconds, violating the knock-and-announce rule. The defendant wants the drugs suppressed. The Court says no — suppression isn't the remedy for that violation. Which case?
cards.back:
**Hudson v. Michigan (2006)**
Holding: A knock-and-announce violation does NOT require suppression of the evidence found inside; the interests protected by knock-and-announce have nothing to do with the seizure of evidence, and the deterrence benefits don't justify the cost.
Signature: "whether violation of the 'knock-and-announce' rule requires the suppression of all evidence found in the search" — answer: no.
Why it matters: a major limit on the exclusionary remedy — not every Fourth Amendment violation yields suppression.
⚠ Don't confuse with the good-faith line — Hudson is a CAUSATION/remedy-scope holding (the violation didn't cause the discovery), not a good-faith case.

cards.front: Police search a car and find a rifle and shells under the seat and in the glove box. The PASSENGERS — who don't own the car and claim no interest in the rifle or those areas — try to suppress. The Court says they can't; Fourth Amendment rights are personal. Which case?
cards.back:
**Rakas v. Illinois (1978)**
Holding: Fourth Amendment rights are personal and may not be vicariously asserted; a defendant must show that HIS OWN legitimate expectation of privacy was infringed. Passengers with no possessory or privacy interest in the searched areas lack standing.
Signature: "Fourth Amendment rights are personal rights which ... may not be vicariously asserted." 439 U.S. at 133-134; reframed "standing" as part of the merits (REP in the place).
Why it matters: the controlling standing case — folds standing into the reasonable-expectation-of-privacy inquiry.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Rawlings** (owning the ITEMS isn't enough), **Olson** (overnight guest HAS REP), **Carter** (short-term commercial visitor does NOT), or **Byrd** (rental driver not on the agreement CAN have REP).

cards.front: A man dumps his drugs into a female companion's purse minutes before police arrive, then admits the drugs are his. He owns the drugs — but the Court says he had no reasonable expectation of privacy in HER purse, so he can't suppress the search of it. Which case?
cards.back:
**Rawlings v. Kentucky (1980)**
Holding: Owning the items seized is not enough to challenge a search; a defendant must have a legitimate expectation of privacy in the PLACE searched. No REP in a companion's purse = no standing.
Signature: ownership of the contraband ≠ privacy interest in the container/place; applies Rakas.
Why it matters: separates ownership of the thing from privacy in the place — the place is what matters.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Rakas** (the parent: passengers, no interest in the car) — Rawlings sharpens it: even OWNING the seized drugs doesn't give you standing to contest the search of someone else's purse.

cards.front: Police make a warrantless, nonconsensual entry into a home to arrest a man who was staying there as an OVERNIGHT GUEST. The Court holds he had a reasonable expectation of privacy in the home and could challenge the entry. Which case?
cards.back:
**Minnesota v. Olson (1990)**
Holding: An overnight guest has a reasonable expectation of privacy in his host's home and therefore standing to challenge a warrantless entry to arrest him.
Signature: "staying overnight in another's home is a longstanding social custom" that society recognizes as reasonable; an overnight guest HAS REP.
Why it matters: the standing case on the YES side — overnight guests are protected.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Carter** — Olson (overnight guest) HAS standing; Carter (short-term, commercial visitor present a couple hours to bag drugs) does NOT.

cards.front: Two men come to a stranger's apartment purely to bag cocaine — there for a couple of hours, never met the lessee before, no overnight stay. An officer peers through a gap in the blinds. The Court holds these visitors had no Fourth Amendment standing. Which case?
cards.back:
**Minnesota v. Carter (1998)**
Holding: A short-term visitor present in another's home for a purely commercial purpose (bagging drugs), with no prior relationship and no overnight stay, has no legitimate expectation of privacy there and cannot challenge the police observation.
Signature: commercial, transient presence ≠ the social-guest protection of Olson.
Why it matters: the standing case on the NO side; marks the line between an overnight/social guest and a brief business visitor.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Olson** (overnight guest HAS REP). Carter = short-term COMMERCIAL visitor, no standing. The dividing facts: duration, purpose, and prior connection to the home.

cards.front: A man drives a rental car with the renter's permission, but he is NOT listed as an authorized driver on the rental agreement. Police search the trunk. The Court holds he can still have a reasonable expectation of privacy in the car. Which case?
cards.back:
**Byrd v. United States (2018)**
Holding: A driver in lawful possession and control of a rental car generally has a reasonable expectation of privacy in it, even though he is not listed as an authorized driver on the rental agreement.
Signature: lawful possession, not the paperwork, drives the privacy inquiry; the unlisted driver can have standing.
Why it matters: a modern standing case rejecting a bright-line "not on the contract = no REP" rule.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Rakas** (passenger, no possessory/privacy interest) — Byrd had lawful possession and control. Possession matters; mere passenger status does not.

cards.front: A grand-jury witness refuses to answer questions based on evidence from an allegedly illegal search, claiming the exclusionary rule protects him. The Court says no — the rule is a deterrent remedy, not a personal right, and it doesn't reach grand-jury questioning. Which case?
cards.back:
**United States v. Calandra (1974)**
Holding: The exclusionary rule is a judicially created deterrent remedy, not a personal constitutional right of the aggrieved party, and it does not apply to grand-jury questions based on illegally obtained evidence.
Signature: "a judicially created remedy designed to safeguard Fourth Amendment rights generally through its deterrent effect, rather than a personal constitutional right of the party aggrieved." 414 U.S. at 348.
Why it matters: reframed the rule as cost-benefit deterrence (the engine behind Leon, Herring, Hudson) and carved grand juries out of it.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Lopez-Mendoza** (civil deportation) — both are SCOPE/forum limits where exclusion doesn't fully reach; Calandra = grand jury, Lopez-Mendoza = civil removal.

cards.front: A man's admission of unlawful presence follows an allegedly illegal arrest. He wants it excluded from his CIVIL deportation hearing. The Court holds the exclusionary rule generally doesn't apply there. Which case?
cards.back:
**INS v. Lopez-Mendoza (1984)**
Holding: The exclusionary rule generally does NOT apply in civil deportation/removal proceedings; under a cost-benefit analysis, the social costs of exclusion outweigh its limited deterrence in that civil context.
Signature: "the exclusionary rule need not be applied" in a civil deportation hearing.
Why it matters: a key SCOPE limit — the rule is a criminal-suppression remedy and doesn't automatically reach civil proceedings.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Calandra** (grand-jury questions) — both are forum/scope limits on the rule; Lopez-Mendoza = civil deportation, Calandra = grand jury.

cards.front: A defendant's statements were taken in violation of Miranda, so they're barred from the prosecution's case-in-chief. But he takes the stand and testifies inconsistently with them — and the Court lets the prosecution use the tainted statements to IMPEACH him. Which case?
cards.back:
**Harris v. New York (1971)**
Holding: Statements taken in violation of Miranda, though inadmissible in the case-in-chief, may be used to impeach a defendant who takes the stand and testifies inconsistently with them.
Signature: the shield of Miranda "cannot be perverted into a license to use perjury"; the impeachment exception.
Why it matters: a limit on the exclusionary remedy — suppressed evidence can still police a testifying defendant's credibility (truth-seeking outweighs deterrence here).
⚠ Don't confuse the forums: the prosecution still CANNOT use this illegally obtained evidence in its case-in-chief — Harris only permits IMPEACHMENT once the defendant testifies. Cf. **Calandra**/**Lopez-Mendoza** (other scope limits on the rule).

cards.front: Federal narcotics agents enter a man's apartment without a warrant, search it, and arrest him. He sues the agents personally for money damages for the Fourth Amendment violation — and the Court recognizes an implied right to sue even though no statute creates it. Which case?
cards.back:
**Bivens v. Six Unknown Named Agents (1971)**
Holding: There is an implied federal cause of action for money damages against federal officers who violate the Fourth Amendment, even absent a statute authorizing the suit.
Signature: the "Bivens action" — a damages remedy directly under the Constitution against federal agents.
Why it matters: the civil-remedy counterpart to the exclusionary rule (suppression keeps evidence out; Bivens makes the officer pay), aimed at deterring unconstitutional conduct.
⚠ Don't confuse the remedies: the exclusionary rule SUPPRESSES evidence in a criminal case; Bivens awards DAMAGES in a civil suit. For state/local officers the analog is a 42 U.S.C. § 1983 action, not Bivens (which is federal-officer only).
cards.cite: Bivens v. Six Unknown Named Agents, 403 U.S. 388 (1971)

cards.front: Mississippi deputies hang a suspect from a tree, then brutally whip him until he confesses to murder; the whipping marks are still on his body at trial, and the confession is the only evidence. Which case?
cards.back:
**Brown v. Mississippi (1936)**
Holding: A confession extracted by physical torture is involuntary and its use to convict violates Fourteenth Amendment due process.
Signature: the first case to reverse a state conviction for a coerced confession; the deputies' methods were "revolting to the sense of justice."
Why it matters: founds the pre-Miranda due-process "voluntariness" line — confessions are tested for voluntariness long before Miranda's bright-line warnings.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Connelly** (coercion must come from POLICE) — Brown is the paradigm of overt police coercion; Connelly is its absence.

cards.front: Police question a murder suspect under electric lights for 36 hours straight, in relays, with no sleep, until he confesses. No physical beating — but the Court calls the situation "inherently coercive." Which case?
cards.back:
**Ashcraft v. Tennessee (1944)**
Holding: Thirty-six hours of continuous, relay interrogation without sleep is "inherently coercive," rendering the resulting confession involuntary under due process.
Signature: "inherently coercive" — prolonged incommunicado interrogation alone can break the will, no blows required.
Why it matters: extends the voluntariness rule from physical torture (Brown) to psychological/duration coercion.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Brown** (physical torture) — Ashcraft shows coercion need not be physical; the marathon interrogation itself sufficed.

cards.front: Police have a rookie officer — the suspect's childhood friend, Bruno — falsely tell him that Bruno's job and pregnant wife are in jeopardy unless the suspect talks. Worn down by the fake sympathy, he confesses. Which case?
cards.back:
**Spano v. New York (1959)**
Holding: A confession produced by psychological pressure — here a friend's feigned distress plus persistent overnight questioning of a suspect who had a lawyer and asked for him — is involuntary; "the petitioner's will was overborne."
Signature: "will was overborne" by false-friend psychological coercion.
Why it matters: capstone of the totality-of-circumstances voluntariness era just before Miranda; concurrences urging a per se right to counsel foreshadow Massiah (6A).
⚠ Don't confuse with **Massiah** (6A right to counsel, post-indictment) — Spano's MAJORITY rests on due-process voluntariness, not the Sixth Amendment.

cards.front: A man with schizophrenia walks up to an officer on the street and confesses to murder, saying the "voice of God" commanded him to. There was no police pressure at all. Was the confession involuntary — and which case?
cards.back:
**Colorado v. Connelly (1986)**
Holding: A confession is "involuntary" for due-process purposes only when there is COERCIVE POLICE ACTIVITY; a suspect's mental illness or internal compulsion, without police overreaching, does not make it involuntary.
Signature: "coercive police activity is a necessary predicate" to a finding of involuntariness.
Why it matters: relocates voluntariness from the suspect's mind to police conduct — free will alone isn't the test; also sets a preponderance standard for waiver/voluntariness.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Brown / Ashcraft / Spano** (police coercion clearly present) — Connelly is the case where coercion is ABSENT, so the confession stands.

cards.front: Which case held that before any custodial interrogation police must warn a suspect of the right to remain silent, that anything said can be used against him, and the right to counsel (appointed if needed) — to protect the Fifth Amendment privilege?
cards.back:
**Miranda v. Arizona (1966)**
Holding: Statements from custodial interrogation are inadmissible unless police first gave the warnings and the suspect knowingly, voluntarily waived them; custody = "taken into custody or otherwise deprived of his freedom of action in any significant way."
Signature: "You have the right to remain silent..." — the four warnings safeguarding the 5A privilege against self-incrimination.
Why it matters: the bright-line replacement for the murky case-by-case voluntariness test; the spine of confession law.
⚠ Custody + interrogation BOTH required — lose either (Mathiason: no custody; Muniz booking: not interrogation) and no warnings are needed.

cards.front: Congress passed 18 U.S.C. § 3501 trying to make voluntariness — not the warnings — the sole admissibility test, effectively overruling Miranda by statute. Which case struck that down?
cards.back:
**Dickerson v. United States (2000)**
Holding: Miranda is a constitutional rule that Congress may not supersede by statute; § 3501 is unconstitutional and Miranda governs the admissibility of confessions in federal court.
Signature: Miranda "announced a constitutional rule" and "may not be in effect overruled by an Act of Congress."
Why it matters: cements Miranda's constitutional status and forecloses legislative repeal — written by Rehnquist, a longtime Miranda skeptic.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Vega v. Tekoh** — Dickerson keeps Miranda alive as a constitutional rule; Vega still holds a Miranda breach isn't itself a § 1983 violation.

cards.front: Police relentlessly question a man who was just shot by officers, in the ER, while he screams in pain and is never given Miranda warnings — but he is never charged and his statements are never used in any criminal trial. Can he sue for a Fifth Amendment violation? Which case?
cards.back:
**Chavez v. Martinez (2003)**
Holding: The Fifth Amendment Self-Incrimination Clause is a trial right; mere coercive, un-Mirandized questioning is not a completed constitutional violation where no statement is ever used in a criminal case — so no § 1983 claim on that theory (fragmented plurality).
Signature: the privilege is not violated until a compelled statement is "used in a criminal case."
Why it matters: the precursor to Vega — separates the act of questioning from the constitutional injury.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Vega v. Tekoh** (2022), which squarely held a Miranda violation alone never supports § 1983; Chavez left a possible substantive-due-process avenue open on remand.

cards.front: A nursing assistant is acquitted at trial, then sues the officer under § 1983 for damages, claiming the officer took an un-Mirandized statement that was used against him. Does a Miranda violation, by itself, support a civil damages suit? Which case?
cards.back:
**Vega v. Tekoh (2022)**
Holding: "A violation of the Miranda rules does not provide a basis for a § 1983 claim" — Miranda is a prophylactic rule, and its breach is not itself a violation of the Fifth Amendment right actionable in damages.
Signature: a Miranda violation ≠ a constitutional violation for civil-liability purposes (Alito, J.).
Why it matters: the remedy for a Miranda breach is suppression at the criminal trial, not money damages against the officer.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Dickerson** — Miranda is still a "constitutional rule" for suppression, yet (per Vega) not a free-standing § 1983 cause of action.

cards.front: An officer stops a driver, asks a few roadside questions and runs field-sobriety tests, then arrests him for a misdemeanor DUI — no warnings given roadside. The Court says the roadside questions weren't custodial, but Miranda still applies once he's arrested even for a misdemeanor. Which case?
cards.back:
**Berkemer v. McCarty (1984)**
Holding: (1) Miranda applies to ALL custodial interrogation regardless of the offense's severity — misdemeanors included; (2) the temporary, public detention of an ordinary traffic stop is NOT Miranda custody.
Signature: a routine traffic stop is presumptively non-custodial — brief, public, and not police-dominated like "station house" questioning.
Why it matters: defines the custody line at the roadside and rejects any "minor offense" exception to Miranda.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Quarles** (offense severity is irrelevant to custody) or treat a Terry/traffic stop as custody — Berkemer says it generally isn't, but a full DUI arrest is.

cards.front: A parolee voluntarily comes to the police station after an officer leaves a card asking him to call; he's told he is NOT under arrest, is falsely told his prints were at the scene, confesses, and then leaves freely. No warnings given. Custody? Which case?
cards.back:
**Oregon v. Mathiason (1977)**
Holding: A suspect who comes voluntarily to the station, is told he is not under arrest, and is free to leave is NOT in custody for Miranda — even inside a police building and even when confronted with a (false) claim of evidence.
Signature: Miranda turns on custody, not on a "coercive environment" generally or the station's location; no warnings were required here.
Why it matters: location at a police station does not equal custody — the test is restraint on freedom akin to formal arrest. (Reaffirmed by California v. Beheler.)
⚠ Don't confuse with a station-house arrest — voluntariness of the visit + freedom to leave is what defeats custody here.

cards.front: A prison inmate is taken from his cell to a conference room and questioned for hours about an unrelated crime, told he could leave and return to his cell at any time. Is a prisoner automatically "in custody" for Miranda just because he's incarcerated? Which case?
cards.back:
**Howes v. Fields (2012)**
Holding: Imprisonment alone does not make questioning custodial for Miranda; whether an inmate is "in custody" depends on the totality — here, told he could leave and return to his cell, he was not in Miranda custody.
Signature: there is no per se rule that questioning a prisoner is custodial; "standard conditions of confinement" don't add the extra restraint Miranda targets.
Why it matters: the custody test is functional, not status-based — even a prisoner can be non-custodial.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Mathiason / Berkemer** — same lesson (custody ≠ location/status), applied to the prison setting.

cards.front: A 13-year-old is pulled from class and questioned by police in a closed school conference room, with no warnings and no parent, until he confesses to burglaries. Does the child's AGE factor into whether he was "in custody"? Which case?
cards.back:
**J.D.B. v. North Carolina (2011)**
Holding: A child's age is a relevant factor in the Miranda custody analysis when it was known to or objectively apparent to the officer — because a reasonable child may feel restrained where an adult would not.
Signature: age is part of the objective "would a reasonable person feel free to leave" inquiry for juveniles.
Why it matters: injects an objective age factor into the otherwise person-neutral custody test.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Yarborough v. Alvarado** (2004), which declined to require age/experience in the custody test — J.D.B. carved out age for children specifically.

cards.front: On federal habeas, the Court declined to fault a state court for NOT factoring a 17-year-old's youth and inexperience into the objective custody test. Which case (the one J.D.B. later distinguished)?
cards.back:
**Yarborough v. Alvarado (2004)**
Holding: Under AEDPA deference, it was not unreasonable to omit a suspect's age and prior experience from the objective "in custody" analysis; the custody test focuses on the objective circumstances, not the individual's personal traits.
Signature: age/experience are not required elements of the objective custody inquiry (decided on habeas deference).
Why it matters: the high-water mark for a purely objective custody test — later limited for juveniles by J.D.B.
⚠ Don't confuse with **J.D.B.** (2011) — J.D.B. held a CHILD's age IS relevant to custody, distinguishing Yarborough.

cards.front: The lower court thought a suspect wasn't "in custody" partly because the officer didn't yet consider him a suspect — the officer's hidden, uncommunicated suspicions. Does an officer's unspoken focus matter to custody? Which case?
cards.back:
**Stansbury v. California (1994)**
Holding: Custody is determined by the objective circumstances of the interrogation, not by the subjective, undisclosed views of the officer or the suspect; an officer's uncommunicated suspicion is irrelevant.
Signature: "the objective circumstances" govern — a policeman's unarticulated focus on the suspect doesn't create or defeat custody.
Why it matters: locks custody to the objective "reasonable person" test (cf. Berkemer); subjective belief counts only if conveyed and it would affect how a reasonable person gauges his freedom.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Beckwith/Mathiason** facts — Stansbury's point is the LEGAL test (objective), not a particular setting.

cards.front: In the squad car after arrest, officers don't question the suspect — they just remark to each other that it would be a shame if a child from the nearby school for the handicapped found the missing shotgun and got hurt. He leads them to the gun. Was that "interrogation"? Which case?
cards.back:
**Rhode Island v. Innis (1980)**
Holding: "Interrogation" under Miranda includes not just express questioning but its "functional equivalent" — any words or actions police should know are reasonably likely to elicit an incriminating response; on these facts, the offhand remarks were NOT interrogation.
Signature: the "functional equivalent" test — "words or actions on the part of the police... reasonably likely to elicit an incriminating response."
Why it matters: defines the "interrogation" half of "custodial interrogation," focusing on the suspect's perceptions, not police intent.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Perkins** (undercover agent in a cell = no Miranda interrogation because no police-dominated coercion) or **Muniz** (booking questions exempt).

cards.front: An undercover officer poses as a fellow inmate in a cellblock and, without any warnings, coaxes a jailed suspect into bragging about a murder. No Miranda warnings were given. Admissible? Which case?
cards.back:
**Illinois v. Perkins (1990)**
Holding: Miranda warnings are not required when an undercover officer (or agent) posing as an inmate elicits statements from a suspect — because there is no police-dominated "coercive atmosphere"; the suspect doesn't know he's talking to law enforcement.
Signature: Miranda guards against coercion of custody-plus-known-interrogation; "the essential ingredients of a police-dominated atmosphere" are absent when the suspect thinks he's chatting with a cellmate.
Why it matters: custody alone isn't enough — Miranda's premise is the coercion of KNOWN official interrogation.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Innis** (overheard police remarks = possible interrogation) or **Massiah** (once 6A attaches post-charge, a jailhouse plant DOES violate the right to counsel).

cards.front: A DUI arrestee, unwarned, is asked his name, address, height, weight, and the date of his sixth birthday; his slurred, confused answers are used as evidence. Which questions were exempt from Miranda — and which case draws the line?
cards.back:
**Pennsylvania v. Muniz (1990)**
Holding: "Routine booking questions" (name, address, height, weight, age) fall within a booking exception to Miranda; but the "sixth-birthday" question called for a testimonial answer whose content was incriminating, so that response needed warnings (slurred delivery, by contrast, is non-testimonial physical evidence).
Signature: the "routine booking question" exception — biographical booking data is administrative, not interrogation.
Why it matters: carves a practical booking exception and separates testimonial content (covered by 5A) from physical manner of speech (not covered).
⚠ Don't confuse with **Innis** — Muniz is the OTHER kind of non-interrogation: administrative booking, not the "functional equivalent" inquiry.

cards.front: Officers chase an armed-rape suspect into a supermarket, catch and cuff him, find an empty shoulder holster, and — before any warnings — ask "Where is the gun?" He nods toward the cartons and says "the gun is over there." Admissible? Which case?
cards.back:
**New York v. Quarles (1984)**
Holding: There is a "public safety" exception to Miranda — when officers ask questions reasonably prompted by an immediate threat to public safety (e.g., a hidden loaded gun), the unwarned answers and the gun are admissible.
Signature: the "public safety" exception — "Where is the gun?" — a "narrow exception" where the need to neutralize danger outweighs the prophylactic warnings.
Why it matters: the principal carve-out from Miranda; objective threat, not the officer's motive, triggers it.
⚠ Don't confuse with the **booking exception (Muniz)** or **undercover (Perkins)** — Quarles is the EXIGENCY carve-out: warnings yield to an immediate safety threat.

cards.front: A suspect in custody says he wants a lawyer, so questioning stops — but the next morning, before counsel arrives, detectives come back to his cell and he confesses. The Court bars the confession with a bright-line rule. Which case?
cards.back:
**Edwards v. Arizona (1981)**
Holding: Once an accused invokes his Fifth Amendment right to counsel, police may not reinitiate interrogation until counsel has been made available, unless the accused himself initiates further communication.
Signature: the "Edwards rule" — a second-layer, prophylactic bright line on top of Miranda for invocations of counsel.
Why it matters: the anchor case for what happens after a suspect asks for a lawyer — all police-initiated questioning must cease.
