⚠ Don't confuse with **Mosley** (suspect invoked SILENCE, not counsel — police MAY re-approach later about a different crime if they scrupulously honor the right) or **Berghuis** (you must UNAMBIGUOUSLY invoke in the first place). Counsel invoked = everything stops; silence invoked = limited re-approach allowed.

cards.front: A suspect arrested for robberies invokes his right to remain silent; questioning stops. About two hours later a DIFFERENT detective, after fresh Miranda warnings, questions him about an unrelated murder and he makes statements. The Court admits them. Which case?
cards.back:
**Michigan v. Mosley (1975)**
Holding: After a suspect invokes the right to SILENCE, later statements are admissible if police "scrupulously honored" the invocation — here, questioning ceased, time passed, fresh warnings were given, and the second interrogation concerned a different crime.
Signature: the "scrupulously honored" standard for re-approach after silence is invoked.
Why it matters: invoking silence is not a permanent bar to all future questioning, unlike invoking counsel under Edwards.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Edwards** (invoking COUNSEL bars all police-initiated re-questioning until counsel is present) or **Roberson** (counsel invoked bars questioning even about an UNRELATED crime — opposite result from Mosley, because counsel, not silence, was invoked).

cards.front: During a military interrogation a suspect says, "Maybe I should talk to a lawyer." Agents clarify, he says he does NOT want a lawyer, and keeps talking. The Court holds this did not invoke his right to counsel. Which case?
cards.back:
**Davis v. United States (1994)**
Holding: A suspect must invoke the right to counsel UNAMBIGUOUSLY; an equivocal or ambiguous reference ("maybe I should talk to a lawyer") does not require police to stop questioning, and they need not even ask clarifying questions.
Signature: "Maybe I should talk to a lawyer" — the textbook ambiguous request; the Court refused to add a "third layer of prophylaxis."
Why it matters: sets the clear-statement rule for invoking counsel; if the request is ambiguous, questioning may continue.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Berghuis v. Thompkins** — Berghuis extended this same unambiguous-invocation requirement to the right to SILENCE. Davis = ambiguous counsel request; Berghuis = ambiguous/silent assertion of silence.

cards.front: A suspect is read his rights, never says he wants to remain silent or wants a lawyer, and stays almost completely silent for nearly three hours — then answers "yes" when asked if he prayed to God for forgiveness for the shooting. The Court admits it. Which case?
cards.back:
**Berghuis v. Thompkins (2010)**
Holding: The right to remain silent must be invoked UNAMBIGUOUSLY; merely staying silent does not invoke it, and a suspect who answers questions after a long silence has impliedly waived the right.
Signature: silence alone is not invocation — "he did not say that he wanted to remain silent or that he did not want to talk."
Why it matters: extended Davis's unambiguous-invocation rule from counsel to silence, and reaffirmed that waiver can be implied from a course of conduct.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Davis** (ambiguous request for COUNSEL) — Berghuis is the SILENCE analogue. And vs **Mosley/Edwards**: those govern re-approach AFTER a valid invocation; Berghuis is about failing to invoke at all.

cards.front: An inmate invokes counsel during questioning about abusing his son; police drop it and he returns to the general prison population. About two and a half years later a different officer re-Mirandizes and questions him, and he confesses. The Court adopts a bright-line break-in-custody rule. Which case?
cards.back:
**Maryland v. Shatzer (2010)**
Holding: Edwards protection ends after a 14-day break in Miranda custody; once 14 days pass, police may re-approach and seek a fresh waiver. Release back into the general prison population counts as a break in custody.
Signature: the "14-day rule" — "that period is 14 days," enough to shake off the residual coercion of prior custody.
Why it matters: puts a time limit on the otherwise indefinite Edwards bar; defines when the post-invocation shield lapses.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Minnick** (Edwards bar persists even after the suspect actually consulted a lawyer) — Shatzer is about a break in CUSTODY ending the bar, not about consulting counsel.

cards.front: After invoking counsel, a suspect actually MEETS with his appointed lawyer several times. Days later officers return — counsel not present — and he talks. The Court suppresses, holding the prior consultation didn't reopen the door. Which case?
cards.back:
**Minnick v. Mississippi (1990)**
Holding: Once counsel is invoked, Edwards bars police-initiated re-interrogation without counsel PRESENT — and that protection is not satisfied merely because the suspect has already consulted with an attorney.
Signature: "counsel present," not just "counsel consulted"; the bar is not extinguished by a prior lawyer visit.
Why it matters: closed the loophole that an intervening attorney consultation lifts the Edwards shield.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Shatzer** (a 14-day break in CUSTODY ends the Edwards bar) or **Edwards** itself (the foundational no-reinitiation rule). Minnick = consulting a lawyer is NOT enough to allow re-questioning.

cards.front: A suspect arrested for a burglary invokes his right to counsel. Three days later, a different officer who doesn't know about the invocation Mirandizes him and questions him about a SEPARATE burglary, and he confesses. The Court suppresses. Which case?
cards.back:
**Arizona v. Roberson (1988)**
Holding: The Edwards bar is not offense-specific — once a suspect invokes counsel, police may not interrogate him about ANY offense, including an unrelated one, without counsel present (and a second officer's ignorance of the invocation is no excuse).
Signature: invoking counsel shields against questioning on unrelated crimes too.
Why it matters: makes the Fifth Amendment Edwards rule broader than the offense-specific Sixth Amendment right.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Mosley** — Mosley involved invoking SILENCE (re-approach about a different crime is OK); Roberson involved invoking COUNSEL (no questioning on any crime). The invoked right is what flips the result.

cards.front: Unknown to a suspect, his sister hires a lawyer who phones the station; police tell the lawyer there'll be no questioning that night, then question the suspect anyway. He waives his rights and confesses. The Court upholds the waiver. Which case?
cards.back:
**Moran v. Burbine (1986)**
Holding: A Miranda waiver is valid even though police failed to tell the suspect that an attorney was trying to reach him; events outside the suspect's knowledge don't bear on whether HE knowingly and voluntarily waived his rights.
Signature: deception of the lawyer ≠ coercion of the suspect; the waiver turns on the suspect's own awareness.
Why it matters: the suspect — not the lawyer or family — holds the right; validity is judged from the suspect's perspective.
⚠ Note: this is the FIFTH Amendment (Miranda) rule; once the SIXTH Amendment right attaches at formal charging, such police conduct is judged far more strictly.

cards.front: Given his rights and a waiver form, a suspect says "I will talk to you but I am not signing any form" and then answers questions. The Court holds a valid waiver can exist even without an express, signed waiver. Which case?
cards.back:
**North Carolina v. Butler (1979)**
Holding: An express written or oral waiver is not required; a valid Miranda waiver may be inferred from the suspect's words and conduct — but silence alone is never enough, and the burden stays on the government.
Signature: waiver can be IMPLIED from a course of conduct (refusing to sign ≠ refusing to talk).
Why it matters: the foundation for "implied waiver," later relied on in Berghuis v. Thompkins.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Berghuis** — Butler establishes implied waiver; Berghuis applies it to a suspect who stayed mostly silent for hours and then answered.

cards.front: A man voluntarily answers police questions (NOT in custody, no Miranda needed), but goes silent and looks nervous when asked whether his shotgun would match the shell casings. Prosecutors use that silence against him at trial, and the Court allows it. Which case?
cards.back:
**Salinas v. Texas (2013)**
Holding (plurality): A suspect's silence during non-custodial, pre-arrest questioning is not protected by the Fifth Amendment unless he EXPRESSLY invokes the privilege against self-incrimination; prosecutors may comment on unprotected pre-custody silence.
Signature: you must "expressly invoke the privilege" — silence is not self-executing outside custody.
Why it matters: the privilege generally must be claimed; it doesn't automatically apply to voluntary, non-custodial encounters.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Berghuis** (custodial — silence doesn't invoke the right either, but the setting is post-Miranda custody) or **Miranda/Doyle** (post-warning custodial silence CANNOT be used against you). Salinas = no warnings owed, silence not yet protected.

cards.front: A suspect is told he has "the right to talk to a lawyer before answering any of our questions" and may invoke that right "at any time." He argues this never clearly said he could have a lawyer DURING questioning. The Court upholds the warnings. Which case?
cards.back:
**Florida v. Powell (2010)**
Holding: Miranda warnings need not be given in any precise formulation; warnings are adequate if they reasonably convey the suspect's rights. Telling a suspect he can talk to a lawyer before questioning and invoke that right at any time reasonably conveyed the right to counsel during interrogation.
Signature: no "precise formulation" required — substance over magic words.
Why it matters: the modern warning-adequacy standard; reasonable conveyance, not verbatim recital.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Duckworth v. Eagan** — the other warning-adequacy case, where "if and when you go to court" language was upheld. Powell = lawyer "before" questioning; Duckworth = appointment timing caveat.

cards.front: Police tell a suspect he has the right to counsel, then add that they have no way to give him a lawyer now, but one will be appointed "if and when you go to court." He argues this confused his rights. The Court upholds the warnings. Which case?
cards.back:
**Duckworth v. Eagan (1989)**
Holding: Warnings that include the phrase "if and when you go to court" do not render Miranda warnings inadequate; taken as a whole, the advisement reasonably conveyed the right to counsel before and during questioning.
Signature: the "if and when you go to court" advisement — an accurate description of when counsel is appointed, not a defect.
Why it matters: warnings are evaluated as a whole for reasonable conveyance, not parsed for a perfect script.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Powell** ("right to talk to a lawyer before" questioning, upheld) — both are warning-adequacy cases; Duckworth is the appointment-timing caveat, Powell is the "before questioning" phrasing.

cards.front: Officers get an un-Mirandized admission from a suspect in his living room ("yes, I was there"), then take him to the station, give full warnings, and obtain a complete signed confession. The Court admits the second statement. Which case?
cards.back:
**Oregon v. Elstad (1985)**
Holding: An initial, un-warned but voluntary statement does not automatically taint a later confession; if the suspect is then properly Mirandized and waives his rights, the second, warned statement is admissible.
Signature: a Miranda violation is not "fruit of the poisonous tree" the way a Fourth Amendment violation is — the warned confession can stand.
Why it matters: the lead case allowing a properly-warned confession after an innocent earlier Miranda lapse.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Seibert** — Elstad is the INNOCENT two-step (good faith, admissible); Seibert is the DELIBERATE question-first/warn-later strategy (impermissible). And vs **Patane** (physical fruits of an unwarned statement come in).

cards.front: As a deliberate department tactic, an officer interrogates a suspect with NO warnings until she confesses, gives her a short break, then Mirandizes her and walks her back through the SAME confession. The Court suppresses the warned repeat. Which case?
cards.back:
**Missouri v. Seibert (2004)**
Holding (plurality): A deliberate "question-first, warn-later" two-step interrogation designed to undermine Miranda is impermissible; midstream warnings given after a calculated unwarned confession are ineffective, so the post-warning statement is inadmissible.
Signature: the "question-first" / "two-step" technique — warnings that come too late can't function.
Why it matters: the limit on Elstad — good-faith sequencing is fine, but a strategy built to evade Miranda is not.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Elstad** — Elstad is the innocent two-step (admissible); Seibert is the DELIBERATE one (suppressed). The distinguishing fact is whether the two-step was a calculated tactic.

cards.front: Officers fail to finish the Miranda warnings, but the suspect voluntarily tells them where his Glock is. They seize the (illegal) pistol. The statement is suppressed — but the Court admits the GUN. Which case?
cards.back:
**United States v. Patane (2004)**
Holding (plurality): The physical fruit of a voluntary but unwarned statement is admissible; a failure to give Miranda warnings does not require suppression of the physical evidence (the gun) it leads to.
Signature: Miranda protects against compelled testimony — not against reliable physical evidence; the Court "answer[ed] the question presented in the negative."
Why it matters: physical fruits of a mere Miranda (not Fifth Amendment-coercion) violation are not excluded.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Elstad** (later WARNED statement admissible) or **Seibert** (deliberate two-step bad). Patane is specifically about PHYSICAL evidence — and requires that the statement be voluntary, not actually coerced.

cards.front: DEA agents approach a woman in an airport concourse, ask for her ID and ticket, and request she come to a nearby office. The Court gives us the benchmark for when an encounter becomes a seizure: would a reasonable person feel "free to leave"? Which case?
cards.back:
**United States v. Mendenhall (1980)**
Holding: A person is "seized" only if, in view of all the circumstances, a reasonable person would not have believed he was free to leave.
Signature: the "free to leave" test — indicators include the threatening presence of several officers, display of a weapon, physical touching, or language/tone signaling compliance is compelled.
Why it matters: the objective threshold for a show-of-authority seizure; the foundation of consensual-encounter analysis.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Bostick** (cramped bus → reframed as "free to decline/terminate," not "free to leave") or **Hodari D.** ("free to leave" is necessary but NOT sufficient — the suspect must also actually submit).

cards.front: Officers board a stopped interstate bus and work the aisle asking passengers for consent to search luggage. Because a bus passenger isn't going anywhere anyway, the Court reframes the seizure test for this setting. Which case?
cards.back:
**Florida v. Bostick (1991)**
Holding: On a bus (where the passenger isn't free to leave regardless), the seizure question is whether a reasonable person would feel free to decline the officers' requests or otherwise terminate the encounter.
Signature: the "free to decline / terminate the encounter" reformulation of Mendenhall.
Why it matters: adapts the seizure test to confined settings; cramped quarters alone don't make a bus sweep a seizure.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Mendenhall** (general "free to leave") or **Drayton** (later bus case: officers need NOT warn passengers of the right to refuse).

cards.front: Youths flee at the sight of an approaching police car; while running from a chasing officer, one tosses away a rock of crack cocaine, then is tackled. He argues the toss was the fruit of a seizure. Which case?
cards.back:
**California v. Hodari D. (1991)**
Holding: A show-of-authority seizure is not complete until the suspect submits; a suspect who keeps fleeing an uncomplied-with command is not yet seized, so contraband discarded mid-flight is not the fruit of a seizure.
Signature: "An arrest requires either physical force ... or, where that is absent, submission to the assertion of authority" — yelling "Stop!" at a fleeing suspect "is no seizure" until he yields.
Why it matters: adds the submission requirement to the show-of-authority branch; Mendenhall's "free to leave" is necessary but not sufficient.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Torres** (physical FORCE seizes at the instant applied, even if the suspect escapes — no submission needed) or **Mendenhall** (the "free to leave" threshold).

cards.front: A car is pulled over and the PASSENGER — not the driver — wants to challenge the legality of the stop. Was he seized when the car stopped? Which case?
cards.back:
**Brendlin v. California (2007)**
Holding: When a vehicle is stopped, a passenger is seized just as the driver is, and so may challenge the constitutionality of the stop.
Signature: a reasonable passenger would not feel free to leave a stopped car — he is seized from the moment the car comes to a halt.
Why it matters: gives passengers standing to contest the stop and to suppress evidence flowing from an unlawful one.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Hodari D.** (no seizure of a fleeing suspect until submission) — here the passenger submitted by remaining in the stopped car.

cards.front: Officers fire at a woman to stop her; the bullets strike her, but she keeps driving and escapes, only to be arrested later. Was she "seized" the moment she was shot, even though she got away? Which case?
cards.back:
**Torres v. Madrid (2021)**
Holding: The application of physical force to the body with intent to restrain is a seizure even if the person does not submit and is not subdued.
Signature: "the application of physical force to the body of a person with intent to restrain is a seizure even if the person does not submit and is not subdued" — a shot that hits seizes at that instant; a shot that misses does not.
Why it matters: fills the force-without-submission gap left by Hodari D.; intent to restrain is judged objectively, and a force seizure (absent submission) lasts only as long as the force is applied.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Hodari D.** (show-of-authority branch — needs submission) — Torres is the FORCE branch, no submission required.

cards.front: Police set up a tractor-trailer roadblock across a highway, behind a curve and unilluminated, to stop a fleeing car-thief; he crashes into it and is killed. Was the stop a Fourth Amendment "seizure"? Which case?
cards.back:
**Brower v. County of Inyo (1989)**
Holding: A seizure occurs only when there is a termination of freedom of movement through means intentionally applied — a roadblock designed to stop a fleeing suspect by crashing him is a seizure.
Signature: a seizure requires that the government terminate movement "through means intentionally applied"; the stop must be the very object of the means used, not an accidental result.
Why it matters: anchors the "intentional means" requirement — a seizure is the intended stopping mechanism, not an unintended consequence of police conduct.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Torres** (force on the body seizes even if the suspect escapes) — Brower is about the roadblock as the intended means of stopping.
cards.cite: Brower v. County of Inyo, 489 U.S. 593, 596-97 (1989)

cards.front: A man sees a patrol car approach and starts running; the police car simply drives alongside him for a short distance to see what he does. Is merely following someone in a cruiser a seizure? Which case?
cards.back:
**Michigan v. Chesternut (1988)**
Holding: Police driving alongside a person (an "investigatory pursuit") is not automatically a seizure; under the Mendenhall test the cruise-alongside here did not convey that the man was not free to leave.
Signature: rejected the rule that any investigatory pursuit is a seizure — the test is whether the police conduct would communicate to a reasonable person that he was not free to disregard them and go about his business.
Why it matters: applies Mendenhall to pursuit; absent a sufficient show of authority, following someone is not a seizure.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Brendlin** (an actual traffic stop IS a seizure of occupants) — Chesternut is mere paralleling, no command, no blocking.

cards.front: Three officers board a bus; one kneels in the driver's seat facing back while the others work the aisle asking for consent to pat down passengers — never telling anyone they may refuse. Were the passengers seized, and was consent valid without a warning? Which case?
cards.back:
**United States v. Drayton (2002)**
Holding: A bus sweep with consent-to-search requests is not a seizure, and consent can be voluntary even though officers do not advise passengers of their right to refuse.
Signature: no per-se warning requirement — "The Fourth Amendment ... does not require officers to advise bus passengers of their right not to cooperate."
Why it matters: applies Bostick to the worked example of a bus interdiction; lack of a refusal warning is just one factor, not a dealbreaker.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Bostick** (set the "free to decline" framework) — Drayton applies it and rejects a mandatory refusal warning.

cards.front: Without probable cause, police pick up a suspect, drive him to the station, and put him in an interrogation room for questioning — he was not told he was under arrest but would have been restrained had he tried to leave. Custodial stationhouse detention: stop, or arrest? Which case?
cards.back:
**Dunaway v. New York (1979)**
Holding: Detaining a suspect and transporting him to the station for custodial interrogation is the functional equivalent of an arrest and must be supported by probable cause — it is not a brief Terry-type stop justified on reasonable suspicion.
Signature: stationhouse detention for interrogation crosses from a Terry stop into a full seizure requiring probable cause.
Why it matters: marks the outer limit of Terry — you cannot use a "stop" to accomplish a custodial arrest without probable cause.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Terry** (brief on-scene stop on reasonable suspicion) — Dunaway is involuntary transport to the station, which needs PC.

cards.front: At 3 a.m. police wake a 17-year-old, handcuff him, and take him from his home wearing only his underwear on a January night, drive him to the crime scene and then to the station to question him — all without probable cause. Stop or arrest? Which case?
cards.back:
**Kaupp v. Texas (2003)**
Holding: Removing a handcuffed suspect from his home in his underwear and transporting him to the station for interrogation was an arrest requiring probable cause; lacking it, his ensuing confession was the fruit of an unlawful seizure.
Signature: "removal from one's house in handcuffs on a January night with nothing on but underwear ... to an interview room at law enforcement headquarters" — an arrest, not a consensual encounter.
Why it matters: a sharp, fact-vivid application of Dunaway; saying "we need to go and talk" does not make a custodial seizure consensual.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Dunaway** (same rule — stationhouse detention needs PC) — Kaupp is its memorable "underwear/handcuffs" fact pattern.

cards.front: A postal inspector arrests a suspect in a restaurant, in public, without an arrest warrant, on probable cause that he possessed stolen credit cards. Is a warrantless felony arrest in a public place valid? Which case?
cards.back:
**United States v. Watson (1976)**
Holding: A warrantless arrest in a public place is reasonable under the Fourth Amendment so long as the officer has probable cause — no arrest warrant is required.
Signature: the public-arrest rule — probable cause, not a warrant, is the constitutional requirement for an arrest made in public.
Why it matters: the baseline arrest-power case; the warrant line is drawn at the home's threshold, not at every arrest.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Payton** (to make that same arrest inside the suspect's OWN HOME you need an arrest warrant) — Watson governs the public place, Payton the home.

cards.front: A mother driving with her kids unbuckled is pulled over; the officer handcuffs her, books her, and jails her for a seatbelt violation — a fine-only misdemeanor carrying no jail time. Does the Fourth Amendment bar a full custodial arrest for so trivial an offense? Which case?
cards.back:
**Atwater v. City of Lago Vista (2001)**
Holding: The Fourth Amendment does not forbid a warrantless full custodial arrest for a minor criminal offense, even one punishable only by a fine, so long as the officer has probable cause.
Signature: no constitutional "jailable-offense" limit — a custodial arrest for a fine-only seatbelt violation is reasonable.
Why it matters: rejects a proportionality rule on arrest authority; the offense's triviality does not invalidate the arrest.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Gant/SITA** cases — Atwater is about the POWER to arrest at all, not the scope of any search that follows.
cards.cite: Atwater v. City of Lago Vista, 532 U.S. 318, 354 (2001)

cards.front: Plainclothes vice officers in an unmarked car stop a truck for minor traffic violations — really because they suspect drugs — and spot crack in plain view. The driver says the stop was pretextual. Does the officers' true motive invalidate a stop backed by probable cause? Which case?
cards.back:
**Whren v. United States (1996)**
Holding: A traffic stop supported by probable cause (or, for a stop, reasonable suspicion) of a traffic violation is reasonable regardless of the officer's actual subjective motive; pretext is irrelevant.
Signature: the constitutional reasonableness of a stop "does not depend on the actual motivations of the individual officers involved."
Why it matters: forecloses pretext challenges to traffic stops; the inquiry is objective — was there a violation? — not why the officer cared.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Devenpeck** (a related objective point for ARRESTS — PC can rest on any offense, not the one the officer named) or **Graham** (objective reasonableness for FORCE).

cards.front: An officer arrests a man for tape-recording their conversation — an offense that turns out not to be a crime — but the facts also gave probable cause for a different, uncited offense. Must the offense supporting probable cause be the one the officer announced? Which case?
cards.back:
**Devenpeck v. Alford (2004)**
Holding: An arrest is lawful so long as the known facts give probable cause for SOME criminal offense; the offense need not be the one the officer invoked, nor even "closely related" to it.
Signature: probable cause is judged objectively on the facts known — the officer's stated charge is not controlling.
Why it matters: rejects the "closely related offense" rule; the arrest's validity rises or falls on objective probable cause for any offense.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Whren** (motive irrelevant for a stop) — Devenpeck is the parallel rule for arrests: the named offense doesn't matter if PC exists for another.

cards.front: Officers arrest a driver for a misdemeanor that state law said should have gotten only a summons (no custodial arrest), then search him incident to that arrest. Does violating the state arrest statute make the arrest unconstitutional? Which case?
cards.back:
**Virginia v. Moore (2008)**
Holding: When an officer has probable cause to believe a person committed a crime in his presence, an arrest is reasonable under the Fourth Amendment even if state law barred arrest for that offense — and the search incident to it is valid.
Signature: the Fourth Amendment's reasonableness does not incorporate state arrest rules — a state-law violation is not a constitutional violation.
Why it matters: decouples Fourth Amendment reasonableness from state statutory limits; PC, not state procedure, is the constitutional measure.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Atwater** (no constitutional bar to arrest for a minor/fine-only offense) — Moore adds that a STATE-LAW bar to arrest also doesn't trigger the Fourth Amendment.

cards.front: A car is stopped with three men inside; officers find $763 in the glove box and five baggies of cocaine behind the back-seat armrest. No one admits ownership, so police arrest ALL THREE. Was there probable cause to arrest each occupant? Which case?
cards.back:
**Maryland v. Pringle (2003)**
Holding: Where drugs and cash are found in a car and no occupant claims them, an officer has probable cause to arrest all the occupants on a reasonable inference of common enterprise.
Signature: probable cause to arrest every occupant of a small shared car where contraband is accessible to all and ownership is disclaimed.
Why it matters: applies the totality-of-circumstances probable-cause standard to multi-occupant vehicles; mere presence plus accessible contraband can supply PC as to each.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Ybarra** (mere presence at a searched TAVERN gives no PC to frisk a patron) — Pringle is a small car shared by a few, supporting a common-enterprise inference.

cards.front: After a warrantless arrest, how soon must a neutral magistrate review probable cause before the suspect can be jailed pending charges? The Court attaches a presumptive number to "promptly." Which case?
cards.back:
**County of Riverside v. McLaughlin (1991)**
Holding: A judicial probable-cause determination after a warrantless arrest is presumptively prompt if made within 48 hours; beyond 48 hours the government bears the burden of showing a bona fide emergency or extraordinary circumstance.
Signature: the "48-hour" presumption that puts a clock on Gerstein's "promptly."
Why it matters: operationalizes Gerstein — a hearing inside 48 hours is presumptively timely; outside it, the burden flips to the State.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Gerstein** (established the REQUIREMENT of a prompt judicial PC determination) — McLaughlin supplies the 48-hour timing rule.
cards.cite: County of Riverside v. McLaughlin, 500 U.S. 44, 56-57 (1991)

cards.front: A person arrested without a warrant is held for an extended period before any judge ever reviews whether there was probable cause. Does the Fourth Amendment require a neutral judicial check after a warrantless arrest? Which case?
cards.back:
**Gerstein v. Pugh (1975)**
Holding: The Fourth Amendment requires a prompt judicial determination of probable cause as a prerequisite to any extended pretrial restraint following a warrantless arrest.
Signature: the "Gerstein hearing" — a neutral magistrate must promptly review probable cause for a person held after a warrantless arrest.
Why it matters: a warrantless arrest may begin detention, but a magistrate must promptly confirm probable cause to continue it.
⚠ Don't confuse with **McLaughlin** (added the 48-hour presumptive deadline for that hearing) — Gerstein created the requirement; McLaughlin timed it.

cards.front: To make a routine felony arrest, officers force entry into the suspect's OWN home without a warrant and without consent or exigency. "The Fourth Amendment has drawn a firm line at the entrance to the house." Which case?
cards.back:
**Payton v. New York (1980)**
Holding: Absent consent or exigent circumstances, police may not make a warrantless, nonconsensual entry into a suspect's own home to arrest him; an arrest warrant founded on probable cause implicitly authorizes entry when there is reason to believe the suspect is within.
Signature: "the Fourth Amendment has drawn a firm line at the entrance to the house" — an arrest warrant + reason to believe the suspect is home.
Why it matters: the home-arrest spine — the warrant requirement attaches at the threshold of the suspect's own dwelling.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Steagald** (to seize the suspect in a THIRD PARTY's home you need a SEARCH warrant) or **Watson** (public arrest needs only PC, no warrant).

cards.front: Armed only with an ARREST warrant for a fugitive, agents enter a THIRD PARTY's home to look for him and, while there, find the homeowner's contraband. Did the arrest warrant authorize entry into someone else's house? Which case?
cards.back:
**Steagald v. United States (1981)**
Holding: To search for the subject of an arrest warrant inside a third party's home, police need a SEARCH warrant (absent exigency or consent); an arrest warrant protects the suspect's liberty, not the homeowner's privacy.
Signature: the arrest-warrant/search-warrant split — an arrest warrant alone cannot justify entry into a non-suspect's home.
Why it matters: protects the third-party homeowner's distinct Fourth Amendment interest; the named suspect can't invoke Steagald (it's the homeowner's right).
⚠ Don't confuse with **Payton** (suspect's OWN home — arrest warrant suffices) — Steagald is the THIRD PARTY's home, which needs a search warrant.

cards.front: While arresting a robbery suspect inside his house, officers — fearing an accomplice might be hiding — do a quick look through the basement and adjoining spaces for people who could pose a danger. What standard governs this in-home sweep? Which case?
cards.back:
**Maryland v. Buie (1990)**
Holding: During an in-home arrest, officers may conduct a protective sweep — a quick, cursory look in spaces where a person could hide — on reasonable, articulable suspicion that the area harbors an individual posing a danger; spaces immediately adjoining the arrest may be swept automatically.
Signature: the "protective sweep" — cursory, limited to places a person could be, justified by RAS of danger (Terry/Long logic moved indoors).
Why it matters: defines the lawful scope of a safety sweep incident to an in-home arrest — it is not a full search.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Chimel/SITA** (search of the arrestee's grab-area for weapons/evidence) — Buie is a safety look for hidden PEOPLE, not a search for evidence.

cards.front: An officer shoots and kills an apparently unarmed teenager fleeing over a fence from a nighttime burglary, to stop his escape. Is deadly force on a non-dangerous fleeing felon constitutional? Which case?
cards.back:
**Tennessee v. Garner (1985)**
Holding: Deadly force against an apparently unarmed, non-dangerous fleeing suspect is an unreasonable seizure; deadly force is permissible only where necessary to prevent escape AND the officer has probable cause to believe the suspect poses a significant threat of death or serious physical injury.
Signature: apprehension by deadly force is a Fourth Amendment seizure — it cannot be used merely to stop a fleeing, non-dangerous felon.
Why it matters: ended the common-law "any fleeing felon" rule; deadly force requires a significant-threat justification.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Graham** (the GENERAL objective-reasonableness standard for all force) — Garner is its sharpest application to DEADLY force, and per Scott it's Graham applied, not a rigid on/off switch.

cards.front: A diabetic having an insulin reaction is roughly handled and injured by officers during an investigative stop; he sues for excessive force. Under what constitutional standard are excessive-force claims judged? Which case?
cards.back:
**Graham v. Connor (1989)**
Holding: Excessive-force claims arising from an arrest, investigatory stop, or other seizure of a free person are judged under the Fourth Amendment's objective-reasonableness standard — not substantive due process.
Signature: objective reasonableness "from the perspective of a reasonable officer on the scene, rather than with the 20/20 vision of hindsight"; three non-exclusive factors — severity of the crime, immediate threat, active resistance/flight — judged on the totality; intent is irrelevant.
Why it matters: the governing test for all police-force claims; locates the inquiry in the Fourth Amendment and makes it purely objective.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Garner** (the deadly-force application) — Graham is the general standard; per Scott, Garner is just Graham applied.

cards.front: A deputy ends a 90-mph chase by ramming the fleeing driver's bumper, sending him off the road and rendering him a quadriplegic. The whole event is captured on the patrol-car dash-cam. Was ramming a recklessly fleeing motorist reasonable? Which case?
cards.back:
**Scott v. Harris (2007)**
Holding: An officer's decision to ram a motorist whose reckless high-speed flight endangered the public did not violate the Fourth Amendment, even though it risked serious harm to the fleeing driver.
Signature: the dash-cam case — "Garner did not establish a magical on/off switch that triggers rigid preconditions whenever an officer's actions constitute 'deadly force'"; it is simply an application of Graham reasonableness.
Why it matters: clarifies that deadly-force analysis is not a separate rigid test — it is Graham's totality; a dangerous fleeing driver is a different calculus than Garner's non-dangerous felon.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Garner** (deadly force on a NON-dangerous fleeing felon is unreasonable) — Scott's driver endangered the public, flipping the reasonableness result.

cards.front: A high-school assistant vice principal opens a student's purse looking for cigarettes after she's caught smoking, finds rolling papers, then marijuana and a dealer's list. No warrant, no probable cause. Which case upheld the search?
cards.back:
**New Jersey v. T.L.O. (1985)**
Holding: A public-school official may search a student without a warrant or probable cause; the search need only be reasonable at its inception and reasonable in scope under the circumstances.
Signature: launched the "special needs, beyond the normal need for law enforcement" framework — schools don't need warrants/PC.
Why it matters: the foundational special-needs case; substitutes a reasonableness balancing test for the warrant/PC requirement in the school setting.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Vernonia/Earls** (suspicionless drug *testing* of students) — T.L.O. was an individualized search of one student based on suspicion.

cards.front: A school district makes all student *athletes* submit to suspicionless random urinalysis drug testing as a condition of playing sports. Which case upheld it?
cards.back:
**Vernonia School District 47J v. Acton (1995)**
Holding: Suspicionless random drug testing of public-school student athletes is reasonable under the special-needs doctrine, given athletes' reduced privacy expectations and the school's interest in deterring drug use.
Signature: first SCOTUS approval of suspicionless drug testing of students.
Why it matters: extends the special-needs balancing test to programmatic, suspicionless searches; the anchor for later school-testing cases.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Earls** (testing extended to *all extracurriculars*, not just athletes) or **Chandler v. Miller** (political candidates — struck down).
cards.cite: Vernonia School District 47J v. Acton, 515 U.S. 646 (1995)

cards.front: A school district expands suspicionless drug testing beyond athletes to every student in *any competitive extracurricular activity* — choir, band, academic team. Which case upheld it?
cards.back:
**Board of Education v. Earls (2002)**
Holding: Suspicionless drug testing of all students participating in competitive extracurricular activities is a reasonable special-needs search.
Signature: extended Vernonia from athletes to all extracurriculars.
Why it matters: broadens school suspicionless testing; full case name is Bd. of Educ. of Indep. Sch. Dist. No. 92 of Pottawatomie Cnty. v. Earls.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Vernonia** (student *athletes* only) — Earls is the broader "any extracurricular" extension.
cards.cite: Board of Education v. Earls, 536 U.S. 822 (2002)

cards.front: Federal regulations require blood and urine drug/alcohol testing of railroad employees after a major train accident, with no warrant and no individualized suspicion. Which case upheld it?
cards.back:
**Skinner v. Railway Labor Executives' Ass'n (1989)**
Holding: Post-accident drug and alcohol testing of railroad employees is a reasonable special-needs search; no warrant or individualized suspicion required given the safety interest.
Signature: confirmed that compelled drug/blood/urine testing is a Fourth Amendment search, but a permissible suspicionless one here.
Why it matters: with Von Raab, established the special-needs exception for safety- and integrity-sensitive employee testing.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Von Raab** (Customs employees, decided same day) — Skinner = railway workers after *accidents*.
cards.cite: Skinner v. Railway Labor Executives' Ass'n, 489 U.S. 602 (1989)

cards.front: The U.S. Customs Service requires suspicionless drug testing of employees seeking promotion to positions involving drug interdiction or carrying firearms. Which case upheld it?
cards.back:
**National Treasury Employees Union v. Von Raab (1989)**
Holding: Suspicionless drug testing of Customs employees seeking drug-interdiction or firearm-carrying positions is reasonable under the special-needs doctrine.
Signature: companion to Skinner; approved testing even without evidence of an existing drug problem in the workforce.
Why it matters: extends special-needs testing to integrity/safety-sensitive government jobs, not just post-accident contexts.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Skinner** (railway workers after accidents) or **Chandler v. Miller** (candidates for office — struck down as merely symbolic).
cards.cite: National Treasury Employees Union v. Von Raab, 489 U.S. 656 (1989)

cards.front: Georgia requires candidates for state political office to pass a drug test to qualify for the ballot. The Supreme Court struck it down. Which case?
cards.back:
**Chandler v. Miller (1997)**
Holding: Georgia's suspicionless drug-testing requirement for candidates for state office is unconstitutional — there was no concrete, special need beyond a symbolic show of commitment to the war on drugs.
Signature: the special-needs case that drew the line — symbolism is not a "special need."
Why it matters: shows suspicionless testing fails when the government can't show a real, substantial, non-law-enforcement need; the counterweight to Skinner/Von Raab.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Von Raab** (Customs drug interdiction — upheld) — Chandler had no genuine safety/integrity need, so it failed.

cards.front: A public hospital secretly drug-tests pregnant patients' urine and turns positive results over to police to prosecute or coerce them into treatment. Which case held this unconstitutional?
cards.back:
**Ferguson v. City of Charleston (2001)**
Holding: Covertly drug-testing pregnant patients and reporting results to law enforcement is an unreasonable search; the special-needs exception does NOT apply when the program's immediate objective is to generate evidence for law enforcement.
Signature: drew the special-needs / law-enforcement-purpose line.
Why it matters: a true "special need" must be divorced from the general interest in crime control; if the purpose is policing, you need a warrant or consent.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Skinner/Von Raab** (true safety/integrity needs — upheld) — Ferguson's program was, at bottom, law enforcement.
cards.cite: Ferguson v. City of Charleston, 532 U.S. 67 (2001)

cards.front: Police stop and search a man on the street with NO suspicion at all, solely because he is on parole and California law conditions parole on submitting to suspicionless searches. Which case upheld it?
cards.back:
**Samson v. California (2006)**
Holding: A suspicionless search of a parolee is reasonable; a parolee subject to a search condition has severely diminished privacy expectations, so officers need no individualized suspicion.
Signature: the high-water mark — parolees can be searched on *no* suspicion.
Why it matters: distinguishes parolees (closer to imprisonment) from probationers; anchors suspicionless supervision searches.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Griffin v. Wisconsin** (probationer — search needed *reasonable grounds*, not zero suspicion). Parolee = suspicionless; probationer = reasonable grounds.

cards.front: A probation officer, acting on a tip and a state regulation, searches a probationer's home without a warrant and finds a gun — on reasonable grounds, not probable cause. Which case upheld it?
cards.back:
**Griffin v. Wisconsin (1987)**
Holding: A warrantless search of a probationer's home pursuant to a valid regulation is reasonable when supported by "reasonable grounds"; probation is a special need permitting departure from the warrant/PC requirement.
Signature: the probationer special-needs case — "reasonable grounds," not probable cause.
Why it matters: established the supervision-search special need that Samson later extended to suspicionless parolee searches.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Samson** (parolee — *suspicionless* search OK). Griffin's probationer search still required reasonable grounds.

cards.front: Police conduct a warrantless inspection of a vehicle-dismantling junkyard under a state statute and find stolen parts. Which case upheld the search of this "closely regulated" business?
cards.back:
**New York v. Burger (1987)**
Holding: A warrantless administrative inspection of a closely (pervasively) regulated business — here, an automobile junkyard — is reasonable if there's a substantial government interest, the inspection is necessary to the regulatory scheme, and the statute provides a constitutionally adequate substitute for a warrant.
Signature: the closely-regulated-industry exception (three-part Burger test).
Why it matters: defines when no warrant is needed to inspect a regulated business; evidence of crime found during a valid administrative inspection is admissible.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Patel** (hotels are NOT closely regulated — registry ordinance struck down) or **Camara** (ordinary commercial/residential inspections still need an area warrant).

cards.front: A city housing inspector demands to enter a residence for a routine code inspection without a warrant; the occupant refuses and is prosecuted. Which case held administrative inspections require a warrant?
cards.back:
**Camara v. Municipal Court (1967)**
Holding: Administrative inspections of private property generally require a warrant, but it may be an "area warrant" issued on reasonable legislative/administrative standards rather than individualized probable cause.
Signature: created the administrative "area warrant" and applied the Fourth Amendment to code inspections.
Why it matters: the origin of administrative-search doctrine; replaced the old rule (Frank v. Maryland) that inspections were outside the Fourth Amendment.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Burger/closely-regulated businesses** (no warrant needed) — Camara is the default rule requiring an (area) warrant for ordinary inspections.

cards.front: A city ordinance lets police inspect any hotel's guest registry on demand, with criminal penalties for refusal and no chance for the hotel to object first. Which case struck it down facially?
cards.back:
**City of Los Angeles v. Patel (2015)**
Holding: A hotel guest-registry inspection ordinance is facially unconstitutional because it gives operators no opportunity for pre-compliance review before an impartial decisionmaker; hotels are not a closely regulated industry.
Signature: facial Fourth Amendment challenges to inspection statutes are permitted; pre-compliance review is required.
Why it matters: caps the closely-regulated-industry exception (hotels don't qualify) and demands a neutral check before forced administrative inspections.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Burger** (junkyards ARE closely regulated — inspection upheld). Patel: hotels are not, so the demand-on-pain-of-arrest scheme failed.
cards.cite: City of Los Angeles v. Patel, 576 U.S. 409 (2015)

cards.front: State police set up a highway sobriety checkpoint and briefly stop every car to look for drunk drivers — no individualized suspicion for any stop. Which case upheld it?
cards.back:
**Michigan Dept. of State Police v. Sitz (1990)**
Holding: Suspicionless sobriety (DUI) checkpoints are constitutional; the state's interest in combating drunk driving and the checkpoint's effectiveness outweigh the brief, minimal intrusion on motorists.
Signature: the case that blessed the DUI roadblock.
Why it matters: applies the Brown v. Texas / special-needs balancing test to vehicle checkpoints; the model for permissible suspicionless stops.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Edmond** (drug-interdiction checkpoint — unconstitutional) or **Lidster** (info-seeking checkpoint about a *prior* crime — OK). Sitz = highway *safety* (DUI).
cards.cite: Michigan Dept. of State Police v. Sitz, 496 U.S. 444 (1990)

cards.front: A city runs vehicle checkpoints where a drug dog walks around every stopped car — the program's primary purpose is general narcotics interdiction. Which case struck it down?
cards.back:
**City of Indianapolis v. Edmond (2000)**
Holding: A checkpoint program whose primary purpose is to detect ordinary criminal wrongdoing / general crime control (here, drug interdiction) is unconstitutional; suspicionless checkpoints require a purpose beyond the general interest in crime control.
Signature: the "primary purpose" test for checkpoints.
Why it matters: the key limit on Sitz — checkpoints are OK only for special non-crime-control purposes (highway safety, border), not as a general policing dragnet.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Sitz** (DUI — OK) or **Lidster** (info-seeking about a past crime — OK). Edmond fails because its purpose was *general crime control*.
cards.cite: City of Indianapolis v. Edmond, 531 U.S. 32 (2000)

cards.front: Police set up a checkpoint a week after a fatal hit-and-run, stopping cars only to hand out flyers and ask if drivers witnessed the earlier crash. Which case upheld it?
cards.back:
**Illinois v. Lidster (2004)**
Holding: An information-seeking checkpoint — stopping motorists to ask whether they witnessed an earlier crime committed by *someone else* — is constitutional; it is not aimed at determining the stopped drivers' own wrongdoing.
Signature: the "witness/information" checkpoint exception to Edmond.
Why it matters: distinguishes Edmond — a checkpoint seeking the public's help about a past crime serves a purpose distinct from general crime control directed at the stopped driver.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Edmond** (checkpoint to catch the stopped drivers' own crimes — unconstitutional). Lidster sought information about *another's* crime.

cards.front: Border Patrol operates a permanent checkpoint on an interstate well inside the U.S. and refers motorists to secondary inspection based largely on apparent Mexican ancestry, with no individualized suspicion. Which case upheld the stops?
cards.back:
**United States v. Martinez-Fuerte (1976)**
Holding: Brief stops at fixed/permanent interior immigration checkpoints are constitutional without any individualized suspicion; routine questioning and referral to secondary inspection are reasonable.
Signature: the fixed interior immigration checkpoint case.
Why it matters: established that suspicionless checkpoint stops can be reasonable — a key forerunner cited in Sitz and Edmond; the immigration analog to highway checkpoints.
⚠ Don't confuse with **roving Border Patrol stops** (Brignoni-Ponce — those need reasonable suspicion). Fixed checkpoints = no suspicion needed; roving patrols = reasonable suspicion.

cards.front: A customs officer at an international mail facility opens incoming letter-class mail from abroad on reasonable cause to suspect contraband, without a warrant. Which case held routine border searches need no warrant or probable cause?
cards.back:
**United States v. Ramsey (1977)**
Holding: Routine searches at the international border (including incoming international mail) require neither a warrant nor probable cause; the border-search exception rests on the sovereign's inherent authority to protect itself.
Signature: "searches made at the border... are reasonable simply by virtue of the fact that they occur at the border."
Why it matters: the foundational border-search-exception case; border is the paradigm of a search reasonable without any individualized suspicion.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Montoya de Hernandez** (a *non-routine* border search — prolonged detention of a person — which DOES require reasonable suspicion).

cards.front: Customs agents at the border remove, disassemble, and reassemble a vehicle's gas tank — finding hidden drugs — without any reasonable suspicion. Which case held this is a routine border search?
cards.back:
**United States v. Flores-Montano (2004)**
Holding: The government's authority to conduct suspicionless searches of vehicles at the border includes disassembling and reassembling a gas tank; this property search is routine and needs no reasonable suspicion.
Signature: clarified that heightened-suspicion limits apply to intrusions on the *person's dignity*, not to property like a car.
Why it matters: confirms the broad border-search power over property; a vehicle teardown at the border is routine.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Montoya de Hernandez** (intrusive search of a *person's body* — needs reasonable suspicion). Flores-Montano = property (gas tank), so suspicionless.

cards.front: Customs agents at the airport suspect a traveler is smuggling drugs in her digestive tract and detain her for ~16 hours until she passes the balloons. Which case approved the prolonged detention?
cards.back:
**United States v. Montoya de Hernandez (1985)**
Holding: The prolonged detention of a suspected alimentary-canal (balloon) smuggler at the border is reasonable when customs officers have *reasonable suspicion* that the traveler is smuggling contraband internally.
Signature: the standard for *non-routine* border searches of the person — reasonable suspicion.
Why it matters: marks the line where the otherwise-suspicionless border power requires some individualized suspicion (intrusive searches of the body / prolonged detention).
⚠ Don't confuse with **Ramsey/Flores-Montano** (routine border searches — NO suspicion needed). Montoya = non-routine bodily detention → reasonable suspicion required.
cards.cite: United States v. Montoya de Hernandez, 473 U.S. 531 (1985)

cards.front: CIRCUIT SPLIT (issue of first impression — persuasive, not nationwide binding): which circuit case held that a *forensic* search of a phone or laptop at the border requires reasonable suspicion that the device contains *digital contraband*?
cards.back:
**United States v. Cano (9th Cir. 2019)**
Holding: Manual border searches of a cell phone need no suspicion, but a *forensic* (Cellebrite-type) device search requires reasonable suspicion that the device itself contains contraband — and the border-search exception covers only searches for contraband, not evidence of past/future crimes.
Signature: "border officials may conduct a forensic cell phone search only when they reasonably suspect that the cell phone to be searched itself contains contraband."
Why it matters: the 9th Circuit's rule in the live circuit split over device searches at the border (building on the en banc **United States v. Cotterman**, 709 F.3d 952 (9th Cir. 2013)); persuasive only, NOT nationwide binding — SCOTUS has not resolved it.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Touset** (11th Cir. — NO suspicion needed for device searches) or **Aigbekaen/Kolsuz** (4th Cir. — individualized suspicion with a transnational *nexus*).
cards.cite: United States v. Cano, 934 F.3d 1002 (9th Cir. 2019)

cards.front: CIRCUIT SPLIT (persuasive, not nationwide binding): which circuit case held that NO suspicion at all is required for a forensic search of an electronic device at the border, treating it like any other property?
cards.back:
**United States v. Touset (11th Cir. 2018)**
Holding: The Fourth Amendment requires no suspicion — not even reasonable suspicion — for a forensic search of an electronic device at the border; devices are property, and reasonable suspicion is required only for highly intrusive searches of a *person's body*.
Signature: "no suspicion is necessary to search electronic devices at the border."
Why it matters: the most government-friendly side of the border-device circuit split; persuasive only, NOT nationwide binding — it expressly notes its disagreement with the 4th and 9th Circuits.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Cano** (9th Cir. — forensic search needs reasonable suspicion of digital contraband) or **Aigbekaen/Kolsuz** (4th Cir. — individualized suspicion with a border nexus).
cards.cite: United States v. Touset, 890 F.3d 1227 (11th Cir. 2018)

cards.front: CIRCUIT SPLIT (persuasive, not nationwide binding): which circuit case held that a forensic border search of a device requires individualized suspicion of an offense bearing a *nexus* to the border-search exception's purposes (so suspicion of a purely domestic crime won't do)?
cards.back:
**United States v. Aigbekaen (4th Cir. 2019)**
Holding: A nonroutine/forensic border search of a digital device requires individualized suspicion of a transnational offense with a *nexus* to the border-search exception's purposes (national security, contraband, duties, unlawful entry); suspicion of a purely domestic crime is insufficient.
Signature: added the "transnational nexus" requirement (applying United States v. Kolsuz, 890 F.3d 133 (4th Cir. 2018)).
Why it matters: the 4th Circuit's distinctive middle position in the device-search split — it asks not just *how much* suspicion but *of what*; persuasive only, NOT nationwide binding.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Cano** (9th — reasonable suspicion of digital *contraband*) or **Touset** (11th — no suspicion needed at all).
cards.cite: United States v. Aigbekaen, 943 F.3d 713 (4th Cir. 2019)

cards.front: A veteran officer watches two men repeatedly walk past a store window and confer, suspects a casing for a daylight robbery, stops them, and pats down their outer clothing — finding a pistol. The Court approves the stop and the weapons pat-down on less than probable cause. Which case?
cards.back:
**Terry v. Ohio (1968)**
Holding: On reasonable, articulable suspicion that criminal activity is afoot an officer may make a brief investigative stop, and on RAS that the person is armed and presently dangerous may conduct a limited protective frisk of the outer clothing for weapons.
Signature: "specific and articulable facts" — more than a hunch, less than probable cause; the frisk is for weapons only.
Why it matters: the foundation of stop-and-frisk; two SEPARATE showings — RAS-to-stop (crime afoot) and RAS-to-frisk (armed & dangerous).
⚠ Don't confuse with **Robinson** SIA (a full search of the arrestee) — a Terry frisk is a weapons pat-down only, on RAS, with no arrest.

cards.front: An officer who has watched a man talk to known addicts tells him "You know what I am after," then thrusts his hand straight into the man's pocket and pulls out heroin — no initial pat for weapons. The Court suppresses the drugs. Which case?
cards.back:
**Sibron v. New York (1968)**
Holding: A frisk requires RAS that the person is armed and dangerous, and is limited to a pat-down for weapons; reaching directly into a pocket for narcotics is not a protective frisk and is unlawful.
Signature: the officer "thrust his hand into Sibron's pocket" looking for narcotics, not weapons — scope unrelated to officer safety.
Why it matters: Terry's companion case; the frisk is for WEAPONS, not evidence, and the search must match its protective justification.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Terry** (a proper weapons pat-down on RAS armed & dangerous) — Sibron is the same day's counter-example of an unlawful evidence-hunting search.

cards.front: A known informant walks up to an officer and says a man in a nearby car is carrying narcotics and has a gun at his waist. The officer reaches in and pulls a loaded revolver from exactly that spot. The Court upholds the frisk on the tip. Which case?
cards.back:
**Adams v. Williams (1972)**
Holding: Reasonable suspicion (and a frisk) may rest on a reliable informant's tip, not only the officer's own first-hand observation.
Signature: rejected the argument that RAS "can only be based on the officer's personal observation."
Why it matters: opens the door to tip-based RAS; the reliable-informant anchor of the anonymous-tip spectrum.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Florida v. J.L.** (a BARE anonymous tip that a person has a gun is NOT enough) — Adams involved a known, accountable informant.

cards.front: During a lawful weapons pat-down the officer feels a small lump, decides it's not a weapon, but keeps squeezing and sliding it between his fingers until he concludes it's crack cocaine, then seizes it. The Court suppresses it. Which case?
cards.back:
**Minnesota v. Dickerson (1993)**
Holding: "Plain feel" — if during a lawful Terry frisk an object's incriminating character is immediately apparent by touch, it may be seized; but contraband found only through further squeezing/manipulation beyond the weapons search is unlawful.
Signature: an object "whose contour or mass makes its identity immediately apparent" may be seized — here it was not, so the seizure failed.
Why it matters: the tactile analog of plain view; defines what a Terry frisk may lawfully seize.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Arizona v. Hicks** (moving stereo gear to read serial numbers) — same flaw: incriminating nature was NOT immediately apparent without a further search.

cards.front: Officers stop a driver who has swerved into a ditch, see a hunting knife on the floorboard, and — without arresting him — search the passenger compartment for weapons, finding marijuana. The Court extends Terry's frisk rationale to the car's interior. Which case?
cards.back:
**Michigan v. Long (1983)**
Holding: On specific, articulable facts giving RAS the suspect is dangerous and may gain immediate control of weapons, police may conduct a protective search of the passenger compartment limited to areas where a weapon may be placed or hidden — a "Terry frisk" of the car.
Signature: the protective sweep is scope-limited to where a weapon could be; no arrest required.
Why it matters: applies Terry's frisk to vehicles; distinct from a search incident to arrest.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Belton/Gant** vehicle SITA (requires an ARREST; broader scope) — Long is a weapons frisk of the car on RAS, no arrest needed.

cards.front: A man holding a bag stands near a building in a known heavy-narcotics area, sees a four-car police caravan, and bolts — unprovoked headlong flight. Officers chase, catch him, and frisk out a handgun. The Court finds RAS. Which case?
cards.back:
**Illinois v. Wardlow (2000)**
Holding: Unprovoked headlong flight upon noticing police, combined with presence in a high-crime area, can furnish reasonable suspicion for a Terry stop.
Signature: "Headlong flight — wherever it occurs — is the consummate act of evasion."
Why it matters: flight is a permissible RAS factor; but it was flight PLUS the high-crime context, not either alone.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Florida v. J.L.** (a bare anonymous gun tip, no flight, no corroboration — NOT enough). High-crime area alone is also not enough.

cards.front: An anonymous caller predicts a woman will leave a particular apartment at a particular time, drive a brown Plymouth station wagon to a named motel, carrying cocaine. Police watch it all unfold as predicted, then stop her. The Court calls it a "close case" but upholds the stop. Which case?
cards.back:
**Alabama v. White (1990)**
Holding: An anonymous tip can supply reasonable suspicion when sufficiently corroborated by police observation — especially of the tipster's prediction of future behavior, which shows inside knowledge.
Signature: "a close case," but the corroborated prediction gave the tip adequate indicia of reliability.
Why it matters: the middle of the tip spectrum — predictive corroboration can rescue an anonymous tip.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Florida v. J.L.** (bare tip, no predictive detail — NOT enough) or **Navarette** (a 911 caller reporting reckless driving — contemporaneous eyewitness reliability).

cards.front: An anonymous caller says only that a young Black male in a plaid shirt at a particular bus stop is carrying a gun. Police go, find a matching youth, frisk him, and find a gun — but the only thing corroborated was his location and appearance. The Court suppresses it. Which case?
cards.back:
**Florida v. J.L. (2000)**
Holding: A bare anonymous tip that a person is carrying a gun, without more, is NOT reasonable suspicion for a stop and frisk; an accurate description of a readily observable location and appearance does not show knowledge of concealed criminal activity.
Signature: no "firearm exception" to the reliability requirement — the tip must be reliable in its assertion of illegality, not just identification.
Why it matters: the floor of the anonymous-tip spectrum — bare tips fail.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Alabama v. White** (corroborated PREDICTION) or **Navarette** (contemporaneous 911 eyewitness of reckless driving). J.L. had neither.

cards.front: A 911 caller reports that a silver Ford pickup just ran her off the road, giving the plate and location. Police find the truck, follow it, and stop it without seeing any bad driving themselves — finding 30 pounds of marijuana. The Court upholds the stop on the call. Which case?
cards.back:
**Navarette v. California (2014)**
Holding: A 911 call reporting dangerous/reckless driving can supply reasonable suspicion for a stop when it bears adequate indicia of reliability — an eyewitness, contemporaneous report, on a recorded/traceable 911 system.
Signature: "the call bore adequate indicia of reliability for the officer to credit the caller's account."
Why it matters: a reliable 911 tip can justify a stop even without the officer's own corroboration of the offense.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Florida v. J.L.** (bare, untraceable tip — fails) — the 911 system's traceability and the contemporaneous eyewitness account supplied reliability here.

cards.front: A traveler pays $2,100 cash for tickets from a roll of twenties, flies under a name not matching his phone, takes a brief trip to Miami, appears nervous, and checks no luggage — each fact innocent alone. The Court says together they are RAS. Which case?
cards.back:
**United States v. Sokolow (1989)**
Holding: Factors each individually consistent with innocent travel (a drug-courier profile) can, taken together, amount to reasonable suspicion.
Signature: "Any one of these factors is not by itself proof of any illegal conduct ... but ... taken together they amount to reasonable suspicion."
Why it matters: validates the totality approach and profile-type factors — innocence of each piece doesn't defeat RAS of the whole.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Arvizu** (the anti-"divide-and-conquer" command) — same totality theme, different angle: Sokolow says innocent factors can combine; Arvizu forbids parsing them in isolation.

cards.front: A border agent stops a minivan on a remote smuggling route — a stiff wave, kids' knees propped oddly high, a registered-to-a-smuggling-area plate. The Court of Appeals dissected each factor away; SCOTUS reverses, condemning that approach. Which case?
cards.back:
**United States v. Arvizu (2002)**
Holding: Reasonable suspicion is judged on the totality of the circumstances — the "whole picture" — and reviewing courts may NOT use a "divide-and-conquer" analysis that evaluates and discards each factor in isolation.
Signature: "Terry ... precludes this sort of divide-and-conquer analysis."
Why it matters: locks in deferential, holistic RAS review crediting an officer's experience.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Sokolow** (innocent factors can combine into RAS) — Arvizu is the procedural twin: don't parse the factors apart.

cards.front: Officers deduce from tracks in the desert and a pattern of crossings that a particular vehicle ("Chevron") will be carrying smuggled aliens on a given night, and stop it. Which case gave us the "particularized and objective basis" / "whole picture" formulation of reasonable suspicion?
cards.back:
**United States v. Cortez (1981)**
Holding: Reasonable suspicion requires a particularized and objective basis for suspecting the particular person stopped, assessed on the totality of the circumstances — the "whole picture."
Signature: "the totality of the circumstances — the whole picture — must be taken into account ... a particularized and objective basis."
Why it matters: the canonical definition of RAS, quoted in nearly every later stop case.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Terry** (the original "specific and articulable facts" frisk case) — Cortez supplies the totality/"whole picture" phrasing the Court now repeats.

cards.front: Police in one town issue a "wanted" flyer naming a suspect in a completed armed robbery. Officers in another town, relying on the flyer, make a Terry stop — not to prevent an ongoing crime but to investigate a past one. The Court upholds the stop. Which case?
cards.back:
**United States v. Hensley (1985)**
Holding: A Terry stop may be made on reasonable suspicion, grounded in specific and articulable facts, that a person was involved in or is wanted for a COMPLETED felony — and officers may rely on a wanted flyer/bulletin issued on RAS.
Signature: extended Terry from crime-prevention to investigating past crimes; the "wanted flyer" / collective-knowledge stop.
Why it matters: Terry is not limited to imminent/ongoing offenses; a flyer issued on RAS justifies the stop by the acting officer.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Terry/Adams** (suspicion of ongoing criminal activity) — Hensley authorizes stops to investigate already-completed felonies.

cards.front: An officer runs a truck's plate, learns the registered owner's license is revoked, and — without seeing the driver or any violation — stops the truck on the inference the owner is driving. The Court upholds it. Which case?
cards.back:
**Kansas v. Glover (2020)**
Holding: When an officer lacks information negating the inference that the registered owner is the driver, reasonable suspicion may rest on the common-sense inference that the owner (whose license is revoked) is behind the wheel.
Signature: RAS may rest on a common-sense inference, not just direct observation — "the stop is reasonable."
Why it matters: officers may draw reasonable probabilistic inferences for RAS; the inference dissolves if facts on hand negate it (e.g., driver visibly doesn't match the owner).
⚠ Don't confuse with **Delaware v. Prouse** (a RANDOM, suspicionless plate/license check is unconstitutional) — Glover had a specific, individualized inference of a violation.

cards.front: An officer stops a car for a single broken brake light, mistakenly believing state law requires two working ones (it doesn't). The mistake of law was reasonable, and the Court upholds the stop and the cocaine found on consent. Which case?
cards.back:
**Heien v. North Carolina (2014)**
Holding: A traffic stop can rest on an officer's objectively reasonable mistake of law (as well as a reasonable mistake of fact); a reasonable legal error can supply the reasonable suspicion for the stop.
Signature: no reason a reasonable mistake of fact counts "but not ... a similarly reasonable mistake of law."
Why it matters: cabined — the mistake must itself be OBJECTIVELY reasonable; ignorance of clear, settled law does not qualify.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Whren** (subjective MOTIVE is irrelevant if there's an objective violation) — Heien is about a reasonable MISTAKE, not pretext.

cards.front: An officer finishes writing a warning ticket, then makes the driver wait 7–8 extra minutes for a drug dog to arrive and sniff the car — the ticket business already done. The Court holds the added time unlawful. Which case?
cards.back:
**Rodriguez v. United States (2015)**
Holding: A traffic stop may last no longer than needed to complete its "mission"; absent independent reasonable suspicion, police may not prolong it — even briefly — for an unrelated dog sniff after the mission is done.
Signature: the seizure "become[s] unlawful if it is prolonged beyond the time reasonably required to complete th[e] mission"; the measure is diligence.
Why it matters: kills the "de minimis extension" idea — adding time off-mission needs its own RAS; multitasking that adds no time is fine.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Caballes** (a dog sniff DURING a lawful, unprolonged stop is not a search) — Rodriguez is about ADDING time after the mission ends.

cards.front: Agents detain a suspected drug-running pickup and its companion car for about 20 minutes while they sort out the convoy and confirm suspicions. The defendant argues the stop was too long to be a Terry stop. The Court disagrees — there's no rigid time limit. Which case?
cards.back:
**United States v. Sharpe (1985)**
Holding: There is no rigid time limit on an investigative stop; the question is whether police diligently pursued a means of investigation likely to confirm or dispel their suspicion quickly. A roughly 20-minute stop was reasonable.
Signature: examine "whether the police diligently pursued [the] investigation" — diligence, not a stopwatch.
Why it matters: duration is a diligence test; "it took X minutes, therefore unlawful" is not the standard.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Royer** (the detention became too INTRUSIVE — moving him to a small room, holding his ticket/luggage — ripening into a de facto arrest) — Sharpe is about DURATION, judged by diligence.

cards.front: Detectives flag a "drug-courier-profile" airline passenger, keep his ticket and ID, move him to a small police room, retrieve his checked bags, and get "consent" to open them — finding marijuana. The Court says the detention had become a de facto arrest. Which case?
cards.back:
**Florida v. Royer (1983)**
Holding: An investigative detention must use the least intrusive means reasonably available and stay within Terry's limits; when it becomes too intrusive (holding ID, moving the suspect, retaining luggage) it ripens into a de facto arrest requiring probable cause — here absent, so the "consent" was tainted.
Signature: a Terry stop's scope must be "carefully tailored"; over-intrusive detention swallows the rule that seizures need probable cause.
Why it matters: defines when an investigative stop crosses into an arrest — a scope/intrusiveness limit (not a clock).
⚠ Don't confuse with **Sharpe** (the issue was DURATION — ~20 min OK on diligence) — Royer turned on INTRUSIVENESS, not length.

cards.front: During a valid Terry stop on a report of an assault, an officer asks a man for his name eleven times under a state stop-and-identify statute; the man refuses and is arrested for that refusal. The Court upholds the conviction. Which case?
cards.back:
**Hiibel v. Sixth Judicial District Court (2004)**
Holding: A state stop-and-identify statute that requires a suspect to disclose his NAME during a valid Terry stop is consistent with the Fourth Amendment (and does not violate the Fifth on these facts).
Signature: "A state law requiring a suspect to disclose his name in the course of a valid Terry stop is consistent with Fourth Amendment prohibitions."
Why it matters: a name may be compelled during a lawful stop — but the STOP must be valid first, and the statute reaches only the name.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Brown v. Texas** (no valid RAS for the stop — so you CANNOT be punished for refusing to identify). Hiibel = valid stop; Brown = invalid stop.
cards.cite: Hiibel v. Sixth Judicial Dist. Court of Nev., 542 U.S. 177, 188 (2004)

cards.front: Officers in an alley in a high-drug area see a man who "looked suspicious," stop him with no articulable facts, demand he identify himself, and arrest him under a stop-and-identify statute when he refuses. The Court reverses the conviction. Which case?
cards.back:
**Brown v. Texas (1979)**
Holding: A person cannot be detained and required to identify himself — or punished for refusing — absent reasonable suspicion that he is or has been engaged in criminal conduct. "Looked suspicious" with no supporting facts is not RAS.
Signature: the detention and demand for ID "violated the Fourth Amendment because the officers lacked any reasonable suspicion."
Why it matters: a stop-and-identify demand is only as good as the RAS underlying the stop; no RAS, no lawful demand.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Hiibel** (a VALID Terry stop — so compelling a name was OK). Brown had NO valid stop, so the ID demand failed.

cards.front: Officers stop a car for an expired plate and, as a matter of routine, order the DRIVER to step out; as he does, they spot a bulge and frisk a loaded revolver. The Court approves the order-out as a matter of course. Which case?
cards.back:
**Pennsylvania v. Mimms (1977)**
Holding: Once a vehicle is lawfully stopped for a traffic violation, an officer may order the DRIVER out of the car as a matter of course; the marginal intrusion is outweighed by officer safety. No separate suspicion needed for the order-out.
Signature: order the driver out "without violating the Fourth Amendment."
Why it matters: ordering occupants out is a control measure, not a search — needs no suspicion (the later FRISK still needs RAS armed & dangerous).
⚠ Don't confuse with **Maryland v. Wilson** (extends order-out to PASSENGERS) or **Arizona v. Johnson** (FRISKING a passenger needs RAS armed & dangerous).

cards.front: On a speeding stop the officer notices a nervous front-seat PASSENGER and orders him out of the car; as he steps out, crack cocaine drops to the ground. The Court extends the order-out rule from drivers to passengers. Which case?
cards.back:
**Maryland v. Wilson (1997)**
Holding: An officer making a lawful traffic stop may order the PASSENGERS, as well as the driver, out of the car pending completion of the stop — again as a control measure needing no separate suspicion.
Signature: "an officer making a traffic stop may order passengers to get out of the car pending completion of the stop."
Why it matters: extends Mimms to passengers; the order-out (no suspicion) is distinct from a frisk (RAS required).
⚠ Don't confuse with **Mimms** (the DRIVER) or **Arizona v. Johnson** (FRISKING a passenger needs RAS armed & dangerous). Order out = no suspicion; pat down = RAS.

cards.front: During a traffic stop for suspended registration, an officer who suspects a back-seat PASSENGER is armed (gang attire, a scanner, prison talk) pats him down and finds a gun — even though she has no suspicion he committed any crime. The Court upholds the frisk. Which case?
cards.back:
**Arizona v. Johnson (2009)**
Holding: An officer may frisk a passenger during a lawful traffic stop on reasonable suspicion that the passenger is armed and dangerous — no separate suspicion that the passenger is engaged in criminal activity is required.
Signature: "the police must harbor reasonable suspicion that the person subjected to the frisk is armed and dangerous."
Why it matters: completes the traffic triad — order out (no suspicion) vs. frisk the person (RAS armed) vs. frisk the car (RAS of a weapon, Long).
⚠ Don't confuse with **Wilson/Mimms** (ordering occupants OUT needs no suspicion) — Johnson's PAT-DOWN requires RAS armed & dangerous.

cards.front: A patrolman stops a car at random — no traffic violation, no suspicion, just to check the license and registration — and smells marijuana. The Court holds these discretionary, standardless spot checks unconstitutional. Which case?
cards.back:
**Delaware v. Prouse (1979)**
