Holding: Random, suspicionless stops of motorists to check license and registration are unreasonable; an officer needs at least articulable and reasonable suspicion of a violation. (Neutral, systematic checkpoints are not foreclosed.)
Signature: standardless, discretionary spot stops fail; the RAS floor for discretionary vehicle stops.
Why it matters: establishes that a traffic stop needs RAS (or PC); set up the checkpoint cases that allow suspicionless but systematic stops.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Martinez-Fuerte / Sitz** (FIXED, neutral checkpoints — no individualized suspicion needed) — Prouse bans ROVING discretionary stops, not systematic checkpoints.

cards.front: A roving Border Patrol crew on a highway near the border stops a car solely because its occupants "appear to be of Mexican ancestry" and questions them about immigration status. The Court holds that single factor cannot justify the stop. Which case?
cards.back:
**United States v. Brignoni-Ponce (1975)**
Holding: A roving Border Patrol may stop a vehicle near the border to question occupants only on reasonable suspicion, based on specific articulable facts, that they are aliens illegally in the country; apparent Mexican ancestry ALONE is not enough.
Signature: "this factor alone would justify neither a reasonable belief that they were aliens, nor ... that the car concealed other aliens."
Why it matters: applies Terry's reasonable-suspicion standard to roving immigration stops; ancestry may be one relevant factor but never the sole basis.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Martinez-Fuerte** (FIXED interior immigration checkpoints — brief stops with NO individualized suspicion). Roving patrols need RAS; fixed checkpoints don't.

cards.front: FBI agents attach a listening-and-recording device to the OUTSIDE of a public telephone booth to overhear a bookmaker's calls — no physical entry of the booth. "The Fourth Amendment protects people, not places." Which case?
cards.back:
**Katz v. United States (1967)**
Holding: Electronic eavesdropping that invades a justified expectation of privacy is a search even with no physical trespass; overruled Olmstead's trespass-only view.
Signature: "the Fourth Amendment protects people, not places"; the two-prong reasonable-expectation-of-privacy test — (1) subjective expectation, (2) one society accepts as reasonable — is from **Harlan's CONCURRENCE**, not the majority.
Why it matters: founds the privacy theory of a "search."
⚠ Don't confuse with **Jones/Jardines** (trespass theory — physical intrusion) — Katz is the privacy theory, no trespass needed. The famous two-part test is Harlan's concurrence, not the Katz majority.
cards.cite: Katz v. United States, 389 U.S. 347, 351, 361 (1967) (Harlan, J., concurring)

cards.front: Prohibition agents wiretap phone lines out on the public street — no physical entry of the defendants' homes or offices — and the Court holds it is NOT a search. (Brandeis dissents: "the right to be let alone.") Which case?
cards.back:
**Olmstead v. United States (1928)**
Holding: Wiretapping with no physical trespass into the premises and no seizure of tangible things was not a Fourth Amendment search — a pure property/trespass framing.
Signature: Brandeis, J., dissenting — "the right to be let alone."
Why it matters: the pre-Katz rule; overruled on the privacy point by Katz (1967), but its property/entry instinct was later revived by Jones (2012).
⚠ Don't confuse with **Katz** — Katz overruled Olmstead and held a bug needs no trespass to be a search.
cards.cite: Olmstead v. United States, 277 U.S. 438, 457, 464 (1928); id. at 478 (Brandeis, J., dissenting)

cards.front: Agents secretly attach a GPS tracker to the undercarriage of a suspect's car and monitor its movements for 28 days. The Court finds a search by reviving the old PROPERTY/TRESPASS theory. Which case?
cards.back:
**United States v. Jones (2012)**
Holding: Physically installing a GPS device on a vehicle (an "effect") to obtain information is a search under the trespass theory — no need to reach Katz privacy.
Signature: revived the trespass test — "The Government physically occupied private property for the purpose of obtaining information." Two elements: (1) physical intrusion on a protected area, (2) intent to obtain information.
Why it matters: the controlling modern trespass-search case; "Katz did not narrow the Fourth Amendment's scope" — trespass and privacy now run in parallel.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Katz** (privacy theory) or **Knotts** (beeper, public roads, no trespass installation by govt = no search).
cards.cite: United States v. Jones, 565 U.S. 400, 404-05, 408 & n.5 (2012)

cards.front: Police walk a drug-detection dog up to the FRONT PORCH of a house to sniff for marijuana. The implied license covers "the Nation's Girl Scouts and trick-or-treaters" — but not this. Which case?
cards.back:
**Florida v. Jardines (2013)**
Holding: Bringing a drug dog onto the home's curtilage (the front porch) to investigate exceeded the implied license to approach and knock — a trespassory search under the Jones property theory.
Signature: the implied license lets a visitor "approach the home by the front path, knock promptly, wait briefly... and then... leave," as "managed without incident by the Nation's Girl Scouts and trick-or-treaters."
Why it matters: applies the trespass theory to curtilage; a knock-and-talk has a limited SCOPE.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Place/Caballes** (a dog sniff of luggage or a car is NOT a search) — Jardines is a search because the dog entered the curtilage.

cards.front: Agents aim a thermal-imaging device at a home from the street to detect heat from marijuana grow-lamps inside. Sense-enhancing tech "not in general public use," pointed at a home. Which case?
cards.back:
**Kyllo v. United States (2001)**
Holding: Using sense-enhancing technology not in general public use to obtain information about the interior of a home that could not otherwise be known without physical intrusion is a search, presumptively unreasonable without a warrant.
Signature: "the technology in question is not in general public use"; preserves "that degree of privacy against government that existed when the Fourth Amendment was adopted."
Why it matters: the home gets special protection against new surveillance tech.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Ciraolo/Riley** (naked-eye aerial observation of curtilage = NOT a search) — Kyllo is a search because tech pierced the home's interior.

cards.front: Without a warrant, police have the phone company install a PEN REGISTER recording the numbers a robbery suspect dials from his home phone. No reasonable expectation of privacy in numbers dialed. Which case?
cards.back:
**Smith v. Maryland (1979)**
Holding: Installing a pen register to record dialed numbers is not a search — a caller has no legitimate expectation of privacy in the numbers he voluntarily conveys to the phone company.
Signature: third-party doctrine — petitioner "voluntarily conveyed numerical information to the telephone company" and thus "assumed the risk" it would be disclosed.
Why it matters: the dialed-numbers leg of the third-party doctrine (with Miller).
⚠ Don't confuse with **Carpenter** — extended cell-site LOCATION data IS a search; Smith covers only dialed numbers, not pervasive location tracking.

cards.front: The government subpoenas a suspect's BANK RECORDS (checks, deposit slips, statements) without a warrant. The depositor has no privacy interest in records he handed over to the bank. Which case?
cards.back:
**United States v. Miller (1976)**
Holding: A bank depositor has no legitimate expectation of privacy in financial information voluntarily conveyed to banks and exposed to their employees in the ordinary course of business — so obtaining bank records is not a search.
Signature: third-party doctrine — "voluntarily conveyed to... banks and exposed to their employees."
Why it matters: the business-records leg of the third-party doctrine (with Smith); spurred the Bank Secrecy / Right to Financial Privacy statutes.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Carpenter**, which declined to mechanically extend Miller/Smith to digital cell-site location data.

cards.front: A government informant wears a hidden radio transmitter (a "wire") and lets agents listen in real time to incriminating conversations the suspect has with him. No warrant. Which case?
cards.back:
**United States v. White (1971)**
Holding: A defendant has no Fourth Amendment protection against an informant who, with the defendant's consent to the conversation, simultaneously transmits or records it for the government — no warrant needed.
Signature: assumption of risk — one who confides in a companion "necessarily risks" that the companion is reporting to police; "the risk is his."
Why it matters: the wired-informant rule; consensual electronic monitoring by a party to the conversation is not a search.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Katz** (a bug on a booth, no party consenting, IS a search) — in White a participant consented.

cards.front: A "false friend" invited into the defendant's hotel suite secretly reports the defendant's jury-tampering statements to the government. The Fourth Amendment does not protect "misplaced confidence." Which case?
cards.back:
**Hoffa v. United States (1966)**
Holding: No search occurs when a person voluntarily confides wrongdoing to a companion who turns out to be a government informant — the speaker relied on misplaced trust, not on a protected privacy interest.
Signature: "the Fourth Amendment [does not] protect[] a wrongdoer's misplaced belief that a person to whom he voluntarily confides his wrongdoing will not reveal it."
Why it matters: the false-friend / misplaced-trust doctrine, foundation for White's assumption-of-risk rule.
⚠ Don't confuse with **White** — Hoffa is the unwired false friend; White extends it to one wearing a transmitter.

cards.front: The government obtains 127 days of historical CELL-SITE LOCATION INFORMATION from a robbery suspect's wireless carrier — mapping his every movement — without a warrant. Which case?
cards.back:
**Carpenter v. United States (2018)**
Holding: Acquiring extended historical cell-site location information is a search requiring a warrant; people have a reasonable expectation of privacy in "the whole of their physical movements."
Signature: a digital-age limit on the third-party doctrine — sharing location data with a carrier does not strip it of protection.
Why it matters: third-party doctrine does NOT mechanically extend to pervasive digital location tracking.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Smith/Miller** (dialed numbers, bank records — no privacy under third-party doctrine) — Carpenter carves out long-term location data.

cards.front: Officers ignore a locked gate and "No Trespassing" signs, walk past them onto secluded farmland, and find a marijuana field. The Court holds the OPEN FIELDS get no Fourth Amendment protection. Which case?
cards.back:
**Oliver v. United States (1984)**
Holding: Open fields receive no Fourth Amendment protection — even fenced, posted land — because they are not an area where intimate activities deserving shelter occur; only curtilage carries the home's protection.
Signature: "No Trespassing" signs and fences are irrelevant; "open fields do not provide the setting for those intimate activities that the Amendment is intended to shelter."
Why it matters: reaffirms and entrenches Hester's open-fields doctrine.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Hester** (the 1924 origin) — Oliver is the modern reaffirmation holding signage/fencing immaterial.

cards.front: 1924 — the ORIGIN of the open-fields doctrine: revenue agents watch a moonshiner in a field beyond his home, and the Court says the Fourth Amendment's protection of "persons, houses, papers, and effects" does not reach open fields. Which case?
cards.back:
**Hester v. United States (1924)**
Holding: The Fourth Amendment's protection does not extend to open fields, which the common law has always distinguished from the curtilage.
Signature: the open-fields doctrine's origin (Holmes, J.).
Why it matters: the root case for open fields vs. curtilage; also the abandonment-by-flight origin (defendant dropped containers while fleeing).
⚠ Don't confuse with **Oliver** — Hester is the 1924 origin; Oliver (1984) reaffirms it and adds that fences/"No Trespassing" signs don't matter.

cards.front: A barn sits about 60 yards from the ranch house, outside the fence around the home; officers cross the outer fence and peer in. Which case gave us the FOUR-FACTOR test for curtilage vs. open fields?
cards.back:
**United States v. Dunn (1987)**
Holding: Sets the four-factor curtilage test; a barn ~60 yards from the house and outside the fence surrounding the home was not curtilage (so within the open fields).
Signature: the **four factors** — (1) proximity to the home; (2) whether within an enclosure surrounding the home; (3) the nature of the uses to which the area is put; (4) steps taken to protect it from observation by passersby.
Why it matters: the operative test for drawing the curtilage line.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Jardines** (front porch — clearly curtilage) — Dunn is the test you apply to closer calls like barns and outbuildings.

cards.front: Acting on a tip, police fly a fixed-wing plane at 1,000 feet over a fenced backyard and spot marijuana with the NAKED EYE. Naked-eye aerial observation from navigable airspace. Which case?
cards.back:
**California v. Ciraolo (1986)**
Holding: Warrantless naked-eye aerial observation of a fenced curtilage from public navigable airspace (1,000 ft) is not a search — no reasonable expectation of privacy from the air.
Signature: lawful vantage, not entry — what is knowingly exposed to the public, even in the curtilage, is not protected.
Why it matters: the home/curtilage can be observed (not entered) from a place police may lawfully be.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Kyllo** (sense-enhancing tech aimed at the home IS a search) — Ciraolo is naked-eye only. Pairs with **Riley** (helicopter at 400 ft).

cards.front: An officer circles a HELICOPTER at 400 feet over a partially-open greenhouse in a backyard and, through gaps in the roof, sees marijuana with the naked eye. Which case?
cards.back:
**Florida v. Riley (1989)**
Holding: Naked-eye observation of curtilage from a helicopter lawfully in public airspace at 400 feet is not a search — any member of the public could legally have flown there and observed the same.
Signature: extends Ciraolo to helicopters at low altitude (no FAA-floor limit), where such flight is routine.
Why it matters: aerial observation of curtilage from lawful airspace is not a search, even by helicopter.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Ciraolo** (fixed-wing at 1,000 ft) — Riley is the helicopter-at-400-ft variant; both reach "no search."

cards.front: Police have the trash collector hand over the opaque garbage bags a suspect left at the CURB for pickup, then search them for drug evidence. No warrant. Which case?
cards.back:
**California v. Greenwood (1988)**
Holding: There is no reasonable expectation of privacy in garbage bags left for collection at the curb, outside the curtilage; a warrantless search of curbside trash is not a Fourth Amendment violation.
Signature: curbside trash is "readily accessible to animals, children, scavengers, snoops, and other members of the public" — abandonment / no REP.
Why it matters: leading abandonment-of-privacy case; note the limit — trash must be OUTSIDE the curtilage.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Bond** (squeezing a bus passenger's bag IS a search) — giving up trash abandons privacy; a bailment does not.

cards.front: Agents subject a traveler's LUGGAGE at an airport to a trained narcotics dog. The Court calls the canine sniff "sui generis" — it reveals only contraband — and holds it is NOT a search. Which case?
cards.back:
**United States v. Place (1983)**
Holding: A canine sniff of luggage in a public place is not a Fourth Amendment search because it is minimally intrusive and discloses only the presence or absence of contraband.
Signature: the sniff is "sui generis" — it "discloses only the presence or absence of narcotics, a contraband item."
Why it matters: founds the dog-sniff-is-not-a-search line (extended in Caballes).
⚠ Don't confuse with **Jardines** — a sniff at a home's front porch (curtilage) IS a search; Place involves luggage in public.

cards.front: During an otherwise lawful traffic stop for speeding, a second officer walks a drug dog around the car and it alerts. The sniff revealed only contraband, so no privacy interest was compromised. Which case?
cards.back:
**Illinois v. Caballes (2005)**
Holding: A dog sniff conducted during a concededly lawful traffic stop that reveals only the location of contraband does not violate the Fourth Amendment — there is no legitimate privacy interest in possessing contraband.
Signature: "any interest in possessing contraband cannot be deemed legitimate"; a contraband-only sniff "compromises no legitimate privacy interest."
Why it matters: extends Place to traffic stops (so long as the stop isn't prolonged for the sniff — cf. Rodriguez).
⚠ Don't confuse with **Jardines** (dog on the curtilage = search) — Caballes is a car on a lawful stop.

cards.front: Which case held that a drug-dog's RELIABILITY for probable cause is judged by the totality of the circumstances — and that satisfactory performance in a bona fide training/certification program can suffice — rejecting a rigid evidentiary checklist?
cards.back:
**Florida v. Harris (2013)**
Holding: Whether a dog's alert furnishes probable cause is a totality-of-the-circumstances question; evidence of a dog's satisfactory performance in a certification or training program can itself provide reason to trust the alert — no rigid checklist of field-record evidence is required.
Signature: rejects an "inflexible" evidentiary checklist; "the better measure of a dog's reliability... comes away from the field, in controlled testing environments."
Why it matters: the standard for proving drug-dog reliability (probable cause), the companion to Place/Caballes.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Place/Caballes** (whether a sniff is a SEARCH) — Harris is about the RELIABILITY of the alert for probable cause.

cards.front: Officers put a radio BEEPER in a chloroform container and track the suspect's car as he drives over PUBLIC ROADS to a cabin. No reasonable expectation of privacy in one's movements on public thoroughfares. Which case?
cards.back:
**United States v. Knotts (1983)**
Holding: Monitoring a beeper to track a vehicle's movements over public roads is not a search — a person traveling on public thoroughfares has no reasonable expectation of privacy in his movements.
Signature: "A person traveling in an automobile on public thoroughfares has no reasonable expectation of privacy in his movements from one place to another."
Why it matters: the public-movements baseline; later cabined by Carpenter (long-term aggregate location) and Jones (the trespass of installing the device).
⚠ Don't confuse with **Karo** — monitoring a beeper INSIDE a private home reveals what public surveillance cannot, and IS a search.

cards.front: Agents use a beeper to confirm that a container is located INSIDE a private residence — revealing a fact about the home's interior they could not have learned from outside. Which case?
cards.back:
**United States v. Karo (1984)**
Holding: Using a beeper to monitor an object's location inside a private residence is a search, because it reveals information about the interior of the home that could not be obtained by visual surveillance from outside.
Signature: "Indiscriminate monitoring of property that has been withdrawn from public view would present far too serious a threat to privacy interests in the home."
Why it matters: the beeper-inside-the-home limit on Knotts — the home interior is protected.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Knotts** — tracking a beeper on PUBLIC roads is not a search; Karo is the moment it goes INSIDE a home.

cards.front: A Border Patrol agent boards a bus and physically SQUEEZES and manipulates a passenger's soft-sided carry-on bag in the overhead rack, feeling a brick-like object. Which case?
cards.back:
**Bond v. United States (2000)**
Holding: An agent's exploratory physical manipulation of a bus passenger's carry-on luggage was a Fourth Amendment search — the passenger retained a reasonable expectation of privacy in his bag; a bailment is not abandonment.
Signature: "Physically invasive inspection is simply more intrusive than purely visual inspection."
Why it matters: handing a bag to a carrier (bailment) does not abandon privacy; tactile exploration of an effect is a search.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Greenwood** (curbside trash IS abandoned, no REP) — Bond's bag was a bailment, so privacy was retained.

cards.front: Police get an anonymous letter predicting a couple's drug-running travel; officers corroborate the innocent details of the prediction and obtain a warrant. The Court tosses the rigid two-prong informant test in favor of a flexible standard. Which case?
cards.back:
**Illinois v. Gates (1983)**
Holding: Probable cause from an informant's tip is judged by the **totality of the circumstances** — the issuing magistrate makes a practical, common-sense decision whether there is a fair probability of crime; an informant's veracity, reliability, and basis of knowledge are relevant factors but not independent, dispositive hurdles.
Signature: "totality-of-the-circumstances" probable cause; corroboration of predicted future conduct can carry a deficient tip.
Why it matters: abandoned the rigid Aguilar-Spinelli two-prong test; the controlling federal standard for probable cause from tips.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Aguilar / Spinelli** (the abandoned rigid two-prong: basis of knowledge AND veracity each required) — Gates folds them into one flexible balance.

cards.front: A warrant affidavit recites only that officers 'have received reliable information from a credible person' that narcotics are at the home — no facts about how the informant knew or why he's believable. Which case set the (now-defunct) test that struck this down?
cards.back:
**Aguilar v. Texas (1964)**
Holding: A magistrate may issue a warrant on an informant's hearsay only if the affidavit shows **(1) the informant's basis of knowledge** (how he got the information) **and (2) his veracity/reliability** (why he should be believed).
Signature: the two-prong informant test — "basis of knowledge" + "veracity."
Why it matters: with Spinelli, the rigid pre-Gates framework for evaluating informant tips.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Gates** (1983), which **replaced** this rigid two-prong with a totality-of-the-circumstances balance.

cards.front: An FBI affidavit pairs an informant's bare tip that a man is running a phone-based bookmaking operation with surveillance showing he visits an apartment with two phones. The Court refines the two-prong informant test — and finds the tip still falls short. Which case?
cards.back:
**Spinelli v. United States (1969)**
Holding: Refined Aguilar's two-prong test — a tip that fails the prongs can be salvaged only if independent corroboration is detailed enough to show the informant gained his information in a reliable way; the bare tip plus innocent corroboration here was insufficient.
Signature: the elaborated Aguilar-Spinelli two-prong analysis.
Why it matters: marked the high-water mark of the rigid two-prong approach — expressly **abandoned by Illinois v. Gates** in favor of totality of the circumstances.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Gates** (totality test that overruled this approach) or **Draper** (where the corroborated tip DID establish probable cause).

cards.front: An informant tells an agent that a man will arrive on a specific train from Chicago wearing particular clothes and walking fast; the man steps off exactly as described. The Court holds the verified details established probable cause to arrest. Which case?
cards.back:
**Draper v. United States (1959)**
Holding: When police independently corroborate the predictive details of an informant's tip — right down to the suspect's clothing and arrival — the verified accuracy gives probable cause that the remaining, unverified assertion (he's carrying drugs) is also true.
Signature: corroborated informant tip = probable cause; the model the later tip cases build on.
Why it matters: the foundational corroboration case; Gates relied on its logic that confirming predicted details vouches for the tip as a whole.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Spinelli** (corroboration of merely innocent, non-predictive detail there was NOT enough) — Draper's corroboration was of specific predicted future conduct.

cards.front: A defendant claims the affidavit supporting his search warrant contained deliberate lies. Which case entitles him to a hearing to challenge the truthfulness of a facially valid warrant affidavit?
cards.back:
**Franks v. Delaware (1978)**
Holding: A defendant is entitled to an evidentiary hearing on a warrant affidavit if he makes a **substantial preliminary showing** that the affiant included a false statement **knowingly and intentionally, or with reckless disregard for the truth**, and the falsehood was necessary to the probable-cause finding; if proven and the remaining content is insufficient, the warrant is voided and the fruits suppressed.
Signature: the "Franks hearing" for deliberate or reckless falsehoods in a warrant affidavit.
Why it matters: the mechanism for attacking the veracity (not just the facial sufficiency) of a warrant; a knowing or reckless falsehood is also the first of Leon's four exceptions to the good-faith rule (good-faith reliance won't save such a warrant).
⚠ Mere negligence or innocent mistake in the affidavit does NOT trigger a Franks hearing — the false statement must be intentional or reckless.

cards.front: A state Attorney General — who was also the chief investigator and prosecutor in the case — personally signs the search warrant for the suspect's car. The Court holds the warrant invalid because of who issued it. Which case?
cards.back:
**Coolidge v. New Hampshire (1971)**
Holding: A warrant must be issued by a **neutral and detached magistrate**; a warrant signed by the state Attorney General (who was leading the prosecution) is void because he was not neutral — a per se rule of disqualification, so the search stands as if there were no warrant at all.
Signature: "neutral and detached magistrate" — the issuer cannot be the prosecutor/investigator.
Why it matters: anchors the neutral-magistrate requirement of the Warrant Clause (also the plain-view doctrine's origin case).
⚠ Don't confuse with **Lo-Ji Sales** (a magistrate who started neutral but ABANDONED that role by joining the search party) — Coolidge's issuer was disqualified from the outset.

cards.front: A town justice signs an open-ended warrant to seize 'obscene' items at an adult bookstore, then personally rides along with the police, browses the store, and decides item-by-item what to seize. The Court invalidates it. Which case?
cards.back:
**Lo-Ji Sales, Inc. v. New York (1979)**
Holding: A magistrate forfeits his neutral-and-detached character when he joins and effectively leads the search party and makes on-the-scene seizure decisions; the open-ended warrant (filled in during the search) was an unconstitutional general warrant lacking particularity.
Signature: the magistrate who became "an adjunct law enforcement officer."
Why it matters: shows the neutral-magistrate requirement is about conduct, not just title; one of Leon's named exceptions where good faith won't save a warrant.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Coolidge** (issuer disqualified from the start — the prosecutor) — here a proper magistrate abandoned neutrality during execution.
cards.cite: Lo-Ji Sales, Inc. v. New York, 442 U.S. 319 (1979)

cards.front: An ATF agent's warrant describes the place to be searched but, in the box for the items to be seized, mistakenly describes the house itself — listing no weapons or contraband at all. The Court holds the warrant facially void. Which case?
cards.back:
**Groh v. Ramirez (2004)**
Holding: A warrant that utterly **fails to describe the persons or things to be seized** is facially invalid under the Particularity Clause — even if the supporting affidavit is particular — and the search is presumptively unreasonable; the officer who drafted and led it was not entitled to qualified immunity because no reasonable officer could believe such a warrant valid.
Signature: particularity is non-delegable — the **warrant itself** must describe what may be seized; "so facially deficient" no good-faith reliance is reasonable.
Why it matters: the modern Particularity Clause anchor; ties to Leon's fourth good-faith exception (facially deficient warrants).
⚠ Don't confuse with **Maryland v. Garrison** (a reasonable, honest mistake about the premises was upheld) — Groh's defect was a facial failure of particularity, not a good-faith error.

cards.front: Officers get a warrant for 'the third-floor apartment,' reasonably believing there's only one; in fact there are two, and they begin searching the wrong tenant's unit before realizing the mistake. The Court upholds the search. Which case?
cards.back:
**Maryland v. Garrison (1987)**
Holding: A warrant's validity is judged on the information reasonably available to officers when they sought it; a reasonable, good-faith mistake about the premises (here, that one apartment occupied the whole floor) does not invalidate the warrant or the search conducted before the error became apparent.
Signature: the **reasonable-mistake** rule for warrant execution — "sufficient particularity" judged by what was reasonably knowable.
Why it matters: tempers particularity with objective reasonableness; officers must stop once they realize they're in the wrong place.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Groh** (a facial particularity failure — NOT excused) — Garrison was an honest, reasonable mistake about a particular-on-its-face warrant.

cards.front: A warrant is issued to search a home for child pornography, but it won't take effect unless and until a controlled package is actually delivered and taken inside. Which case upheld this kind of warrant?
cards.back:
**United States v. Grubbs (2006)**
Holding: **Anticipatory warrants** — warrants that take effect only upon a future 'triggering condition' — are not categorically unconstitutional; they require the magistrate to find it now probable that (1) the triggering condition will occur and (2) if it does, contraband will be at the place to be searched.
Signature: anticipatory warrants are valid; "no different in principle from ordinary warrants."
Why it matters: confirms probable cause can rest on conditions to be satisfied in the future, so long as both probabilities are established up front.
⚠ The Fourth Amendment does not require the triggering condition itself to be spelled out on the face of the warrant.

cards.front: Which case held that the common-law requirement that police knock and announce their presence before entering a home is part of the Fourth Amendment reasonableness inquiry?
cards.back:
**Wilson v. Arkansas (1995)**
Holding: The common-law **knock-and-announce** principle — that officers must announce their presence and authority before forcibly entering a dwelling — is an element of the Fourth Amendment reasonableness analysis; but it is not a rigid rule and yields to countervailing law-enforcement interests (threat of violence, destruction of evidence, escape).
Signature: knock-and-announce is constitutionally embedded in 'reasonableness.'
Why it matters: constitutionalized the knock-and-announce rule and framed it as flexible, setting up the exceptions.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Richards** (held there's no blanket exception for felony drug cases — it's case-by-case) or **Hudson** (the violation does NOT trigger the exclusionary rule — remedy bucket).

cards.front: A state court tried to create a blanket rule that police never have to knock and announce when executing a warrant in a felony drug investigation. The Supreme Court rejected the categorical exception. Which case?
cards.back:
**Richards v. Wisconsin (1997)**
Holding: There is **no blanket exception** to knock-and-announce for entire categories of crime (e.g., felony drug cases); the no-knock decision must be evaluated case-by-case — officers need **reasonable suspicion** that knocking would be dangerous, futile, or would allow destruction of evidence.
Signature: rejected a per se drug-case exception; established the 'reasonable suspicion' standard for a no-knock entry.
Why it matters: defines the threshold for dispensing with knock-and-announce — reasonable suspicion, made individually.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Wilson** (which first put knock-and-announce in the Fourth Amendment) — Richards sets the standard for excusing it.

cards.front: Officers with a drug warrant knock, shout 'police search warrant,' wait 15 to 20 seconds with no answer, then break the door with a battering ram — catching the suspect in the shower. The Court holds the short wait reasonable. Which case?
cards.back:
**United States v. Banks (2003)**
Holding: A **15-to-20-second wait** after knocking and announcing, in a drug case, was reasonable before forced entry — the relevant clock is the time it would take to destroy the evidence (flush the cocaine), not the time to reach the front door; reasonableness is judged on the totality of the circumstances.
Signature: 15-20 seconds = reasonable wait when exigency (evidence destruction) is in play.
Why it matters: gives a concrete benchmark for how long police must wait before forcing entry on a knock-and-announce warrant.
⚠ The reasonable wait turns on the exigency (imminent disposal of drugs), not on whether the occupant actually heard or had time to answer.

cards.front: As officers arrive to execute a search warrant on a house, they detain the resident who is leaving and hold him while they search. The Court holds they may detain occupants during the search. Which case?
cards.back:
**Michigan v. Summers (1981)**
Holding: A warrant to search a residence for contraband carries the limited authority to **detain the occupants of the premises** while the search is conducted — a detention justified by officer safety, orderly completion of the search, and preventing flight, and supported by the warrant itself (no separate probable cause needed).
Signature: occupants may be detained incident to executing a search warrant.
Why it matters: the foundational rule allowing detention during warrant execution — later cabined geographically by Bailey.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Bailey** (the detention power is limited to occupants in the **immediate vicinity** of the premises) or **Ybarra** (a search warrant for a place does NOT authorize searching bystanders).

cards.front: Officers with a search warrant watch a man leave the house, follow him, and stop him about a mile away, then detain him under the authority to hold occupants during a search. The Court holds the detention unlawful. Which case?
cards.back:
**Bailey v. United States (2013)**
Holding: The Summers rule authorizing detention of occupants during a search-warrant execution is **limited to the immediate vicinity of the premises**; once a person has left and is no longer in the immediate vicinity, his detention must be justified on other grounds (e.g., Terry reasonable suspicion or probable cause).
Signature: the spatial limit on Summers — detention only in the 'immediate vicinity.'
Why it matters: caps Summers geographically so police can't follow and seize departed occupants on the warrant alone.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Michigan v. Summers** (which authorizes the detention in the first place — at or near the scene) — Bailey is the limiting case.

cards.front: Police have a warrant to search a tavern and its bartender for heroin; while there, they pat down and search a customer who simply happened to be drinking at the bar. The Court suppresses what they found on him. Which case?
cards.back:
**Ybarra v. Illinois (1979)**
Holding: A warrant to search a place (and a named person) does **not** authorize searching other people who happen to be present; a search of a person requires probable cause **particularized with respect to that person** — mere presence at a lawfully searched premises is not enough.
Signature: "probable cause, particularized with respect to that person" — no searching the patrons.
Why it matters: the limit on premises warrants — they don't sweep in bystanders; each person searched needs individualized justification.
⚠ Don't confuse with **Michigan v. Summers** (occupants of a home may be **detained** during a search) — Ybarra bars **searching** mere bystanders absent individualized probable cause.

cards.front: In Fourth Amendment analysis, distinguish plain view as an OBSERVATION from the plain-view DOCTRINE.
cards.back: Observation = merely looking at what is knowingly exposed is no search and no Fourth Amendment event (no REP in the exposed). The doctrine = a recognized exception that justifies a warrantless SEIZURE of an item the officer comes across, requiring all three Horton prongs.

cards.front: Per Arizona v. Hicks, is an officer's merely looking at what is already exposed to view a Fourth Amendment search?
cards.back: No. "[A] truly cursory inspection—one that involves merely looking at what is already exposed to view, without disturbing it—is not a 'search' for Fourth Amendment purposes."

cards.front: What kind of Fourth Amendment intrusion does the plain-view DOCTRINE (as opposed to the observation) justify?
cards.back: A warrantless SEIZURE of an item the officer comes across — not the looking itself, which needs no justification.

cards.front: What is the cardinal error / common pitfall when applying the plain-view doctrine?
cards.back: Conflating the observation with the doctrine: seeing the exposed is free, but SEIZING an item requires all three Horton prongs.

cards.front: State Horton v. California's three-prong test for a warrantless plain-view SEIZURE.
cards.back: (1) The officer is lawfully in the position from which the item can be plainly viewed; (2) the item's incriminating character is "immediately apparent" (= probable cause, without manipulation); and (3) the officer has a lawful right of access to the item itself.

cards.front: What is the FIRST prong of Horton's plain-view-seizure test?
cards.back: The officer must be lawfully in the position (lawful vantage) from which the item can be plainly seen.

cards.front: What is the SECOND prong of Horton's plain-view-seizure test?
cards.back: The item's incriminating character must be "immediately apparent" — meaning probable cause, reached without manipulating the item.

cards.front: What is the THIRD prong of Horton's plain-view-seizure test?
cards.back: The officer must have a lawful right of access to the item itself.

cards.front: Horton, on prong 2: "not only must the item be in plain view; its incriminating character must also be {{c1::'immediately apparent.'}}"
cards.back: 'immediately apparent.'

cards.front: Quote Horton on the relationship between lawful vantage and lawful right of access (prongs 1 and 3).
cards.back: "[N]ot only must the officer be lawfully located in a place from which the object can be plainly seen, but he or she must also have a lawful right of access to the object itself."

cards.front: Why is a lawful VANTAGE not the same as a lawful RIGHT OF ACCESS under Horton? Give the page's example.
cards.back: An officer may lawfully SEE contraband through a window from the sidewalk yet still lack the authority to ENTER and seize it — vantage to view does not supply the right of access to the item.

cards.front: Per Arizona v. Hicks, what standard does "immediately apparent" require, and is reasonable suspicion enough?
cards.back: Probable cause is required; reasonable suspicion / a hunch is not enough. "We now hold that probable cause is required."

cards.front: In Hicks, why did the Court reject a lesser standard than probable cause for "immediately apparent"?
cards.back: "To say otherwise would be to cut the 'plain view' doctrine loose from its theoretical and practical moorings."

cards.front: Which case held that moving stereo equipment a few inches to read its serial number was a separate search?
cards.back: Arizona v. Hicks (1987). Officer Nelson's moving of the equipment "did constitute a 'search' separate and apart from" the lawful objective of his entry.

cards.front: Under Hicks, what happens the moment an officer manipulates (moves, turns, opens) an item to develop probable cause?
cards.back: He crosses from observation into a SEARCH separate from the lawful entry. Plain view must be apparent as the officer already lawfully stands — without turning, lifting, or opening.

cards.front: Common pitfall: an officer seizes an item in plain view on a hunch that it is contraband. What is wrong?
cards.back: "Immediately apparent" means probable cause (Hicks), not reasonable suspicion — a hunch will not support a plain-view seizure.

cards.front: Which case is the doctrinal origin of the modern plain-view doctrine (a plurality on plain view)?
cards.back: Coolidge v. New Hampshire (1971).

cards.front: Per Coolidge, what limiting principle bars using plain view as a general exploratory search?
cards.back: Plain view "may not be used to extend a general exploratory search from one object to another until something incriminating at last emerges."

cards.front: Is inadvertent discovery still required for a plain-view seizure? Which cases bear on this?
cards.back: No. Coolidge's plurality required inadvertence; Horton expressly abandoned it. An officer who fully expects to find the item may still seize it if the three prongs are met.

cards.front: Common pitfall: an officer assumes that because he expected to find an item, plain view cannot apply. Why is this wrong?
cards.back: Horton dropped the inadvertence requirement; a prior expectation of finding the item does not defeat a plain-view seizure when the three prongs are satisfied.

cards.front: State v. Tarantino (N.C. — illustrative, non-binding; pair with Hicks/Horton): do tiny cracks in a wall surrender a reasonable expectation of privacy?
cards.back: No. "The presence of tiny cracks ... is not the kind of exposure which serves to eliminate a reasonable expectation of privacy." An officer who must make a "probing examination" through them to see inside conducts a search.

cards.front: What is the binding FEDERAL authority that the illustrative state case State v. Tarantino must be paired with?
cards.back: Arizona v. Hicks (and Horton) — Tarantino is state, illustrative, non-binding; it illustrates the observation-versus-enhanced-observation line but does not state the federal rule.

cards.front: Commonwealth v. Herlth (Pa. Super., en banc — illustrative, non-binding; pair with Hicks/Horton): was shining a flashlight through a one-inch hole in a closed shoebox a search?
cards.back: Yes. "Trooper Adams' act of shining a flashlight into the hole of the closed shoebox was a search" — using an artificial aid to brighten/peer into a closed container in a home was an unlawful search that plain view did not justify.
cards.cite: Commonwealth v. Herlth, 2026 PA Super 114 (Pa. Super. June 5, 2026) (en banc), slip op. at 7, 22

cards.front: Which out-of-state case does Commonwealth v. Herlth adopt, and what fully-exposed-structure case does it distinguish?
cards.back: Herlth adopts State v. Tarantino and distinguishes United States v. Dunn (an open, fully exposed barn). (Both Herlth and Tarantino are illustrative, non-binding.)

cards.front: Common pitfall: an instructor cites Herlth or Tarantino as the federal rule on enhanced observation. Why is this wrong?
cards.back: Both are state, illustrative, non-binding decisions — always pair them with the controlling federal authority (Hicks/Horton); never present them as the federal rule.

cards.front: Common pitfall: using a flashlight to peer into a closed container to create plain view. What does the page (Herlth/Tarantino, illustrative) say?
cards.back: Using an artificial aid to peer into a closed container can itself be a search, not a permissible plain-view observation — moving/opening or probing to develop incriminating character crosses into a search.

cards.front: What SCOTUS case supplies the premise of the digital plain-view section — that digital is constitutionally different?
cards.back: Riley v. California (2014): physical-world categorical exceptions do not transfer automatically to a phone; "get a warrant."

cards.front: On a digital device, what two questions split apart, creating the digital plain-view problem?
cards.back: (1) WHETHER the device can be seized and searched at all (generally yes, if it could house the evidence sought); and (2) WHERE WITHIN the device officers may look. Because responsive data can be hidden anywhere, a broad search threatens to become a roving license.

cards.front: FLAG: How resolved is the question of digital-warrant SCOPE and whether plain view captures non-responsive data?
cards.back: Unresolved. Courts diverge sharply (search-latitude, particularity/no-per-se, and use-restriction approaches), and SCOTUS has NOT resolved it. The shared concern: keeping digital warrants from becoming general warrants (Coolidge; Riley).

cards.front: Digital-scope split — search-latitude view (State v. Volle, Kan. — persuasive, non-binding): what does it permit and what limit does it keep?
cards.back: Because relevant data may be stored anywhere, broad SEARCHING may be permissible, but "the warrant must still include a meaningful limiting principle tying the authorized seizure to evidence of a specified offense."

cards.front: State v. Volle (persuasive, non-binding) distinguishes which two scopes on a digital device?
cards.back: Search scope vs seizure scope: officers may need broad SEARCH latitude (data anywhere), but the SEIZURE must stay tethered to evidence of the specified offense.

cards.front: Digital-scope split — particularity / no-per-se view (People v. Hughes, Mich. — persuasive, non-binding): what rule does it decline to adopt?
cards.back: It declines "a rule that it is always reasonable for an officer to review the entirety of the digital data seized pursuant to a warrant on the basis of the mere possibility that evidence may conceivably be found anywhere on the device."

cards.front: Why does People v. Hughes (persuasive, non-binding) reject a per se whole-device-review rule?
cards.back: Such a per se rule "would effectively nullify the particularity requirement of the Fourth Amendment in the context of cell-phone data."

cards.front: Digital-scope split — use-restriction view (State v. Mansor, Or. — state constitution, persuasive only): what limit does it impose on nonresponsive data?
cards.back: "[T]he state should not be permitted to use information obtained in a computer search if the warrant did not authorize the search for that information, unless some other warrant exception applies" — a USE restriction rather than letting plain view sweep it in.

cards.front: Why is State v. Mansor only persuasive (not binding) on the federal digital plain-view question?
cards.back: It was decided under the Oregon Constitution (a state-constitutional ground), so it is persuasive only on the federal Fourth Amendment question.

cards.front: United States v. Morton (5th Cir. en banc — persuasive, non-binding): what does the concurrence suggest about plain view in the digital world, and did it decide it?
cards.back: It suggests "it would be unsurprising if the Court ... recognized an exception to another longstanding Fourth Amendment doctrine, this time plain view" — raising the digital general-warrant problem but NOT deciding it (the case resolved on good-faith grounds).
cards.cite: United States v. Morton, 46 F.4th 331, 341 (5th Cir. 2022) (en banc)

cards.front: United States v. Tuggle (7th Cir. — persuasive, non-binding): upholding long-term pole-camera surveillance under current doctrine, what did the court warn about the future?
cards.back: It warned that "it might soon be time to revisit the Fourth Amendment test established in Katz" as digital/surveillance technologies proliferate.
cards.cite: United States v. Tuggle, 4 F.4th 505, 527 (7th Cir. 2021)

cards.front: Common pitfall: treating a phone warrant as a license to roam and keep whatever turns up. What is the danger and the safer practice?
cards.back: That is the digital general-warrant trap; scope and use are contested (Hughes/Volle/Mansor/Morton) and unresolved at SCOTUS. When unsure, get a narrower warrant — or a second warrant for a newly discovered offense.

cards.front: Which Coolidge principle is the unifying guardrail (the throughline) running from the physical plain-view rule to the digital general-warrant concern?
cards.back: Coolidge's anti-general-exploratory-search principle — plain view may not extend a general exploratory search — which guards against digital warrants becoming general warrants.

cards.front: Texas v. Brown (plurality): does "immediately apparent" require that the officer be certain the item is evidence/contraband?
cards.back: No. It requires only probable cause, not near-certainty. The plurality warned the phrase "immediately apparent" "was very likely an unhappy choice of words, since it can be taken to imply that an unduly high degree of certainty ... is necessary," when in fact "probable cause is a flexible, common-sense standard."

cards.front: Texas v. Brown (plurality) on the phrase "immediately apparent": it "was very likely an {{c1::unhappy choice of words}}, since it can be taken to imply that an unduly high degree of certainty ... is necessary."
cards.back: unhappy choice of words

cards.front: Texas v. Brown (plurality): is shining a flashlight to illuminate the interior of a car a Fourth Amendment search?
cards.back: No. "[S]hining his flashlight to illuminate the interior of Brown's car trenched upon no right secured to the latter by the Fourth Amendment." Using a flashlight on what is already exposed (an open car interior) is not a search.

cards.front: Common pitfall: over-reading "immediately apparent" as requiring certainty that an item is contraband. What is the correction (Brown, plurality)?
cards.back: It demands probable cause, not near-certainty — the phrase was "an unhappy choice of words" (Brown); a "flexible, common-sense" probability suffices.

cards.front: Minnesota v. Dickerson: does plain view have a touch analog, and what is it called?
cards.back: Yes — plain feel. If during a lawful Terry frisk an object's contour or mass makes its identity as contraband immediately apparent, its warrantless seizure is justified "by the same practical considerations that inhere in the plain-view context."

cards.front: Quote Minnesota v. Dickerson's plain-feel rule (when an object felt during a lawful frisk may be seized).
cards.back: "If a police officer lawfully pats down a suspect's outer clothing and feels an object whose contour or mass makes its identity immediately apparent, there has been no invasion of the suspect's privacy beyond that already authorized by the officer's search for weapons; if the object is contraband, its warrantless seizure would be justified by the same practical considerations that inhere in the plain-view context."

cards.front: Minnesota v. Dickerson: why did the seizure fail on the facts of the case itself?
cards.back: The officer determined the lump was contraband only after "squeezing, sliding and otherwise manipulating the contents of the defendant's pocket" — manipulation beyond the weapons frisk. That exceeded the lawful frisk and was a search.

cards.front: How is Minnesota v. Dickerson the touch-analog of Arizona v. Hicks?
cards.back: Both turn on manipulation: develop probable cause by manipulating an item (moving the stereo in Hicks; squeezing/sliding the lump in Dickerson) and you have crossed from observation into a separate search.

cards.front: Common pitfall: squeezing or sliding a lump felt during a Terry frisk to figure out what it is. Why is this a problem (Dickerson)?
cards.back: Manipulating an object felt in a Terry frisk to develop probable cause exceeds the weapons frisk and is a search (Dickerson) — the touch-analog of moving the stereo in Hicks.

cards.front: Carpenter v. United States: what is it cited for in the digital plain-view section, and what is it NOT?
cards.back: It is cited for digital CONTEXT only — that digital-era data (CSLI) receives distinct Fourth Amendment treatment given "the seismic shifts in digital technology." It is CSLI/third-party law and is NOT plain-view authority.

cards.front: Digital-scope split — search-latitude pole, federal exemplar (United States v. Burgess, 10th Cir. — persuasive): what latitude does it allow for computer searches?
cards.back: "It is unrealistic to expect a warrant to prospectively restrict the scope of a search by directory, filename or extension ... that process must remain dynamic" — though "that is not to say methodology is irrelevant."
cards.cite: United States v. Burgess, 576 F.3d 1078, 1093-94 (10th Cir. 2009)

cards.front: Digital-scope split — over-retention axis (United States v. Ganias, 2d Cir. en banc — persuasive): on what ground did the en banc court resolve the years-long retention of forensic mirror images?
cards.back: On the good-faith exception, expressly leaving the Fourth Amendment over-retention question OPEN: "we need not and do not decide whether the Government violated the Fourth Amendment, and we affirm the judgment of the district court." The 2014 panel had found a violation.
cards.cite: United States v. Ganias, 824 F.3d 199, 221 (2d Cir. 2016) (en banc)

cards.front: Name the federal circuits in the digital plain-view material and characterize the state of the law.
cards.back: 10th Cir. (Burgess — search latitude), 2d Cir. en banc (Ganias — over-retention left open on good faith), 5th Cir. en banc (Morton — flags a possible digital plain-view exception, not decided), 7th Cir. (Tuggle — warns Katz may need revisiting). Treat it as an approach-spectrum, not a clean circuit conflict; SCOTUS has not resolved it.

cards.front: What are the three rungs of the Fourth Amendment "ladder" of proof, from lowest to highest?
cards.back: A bare hunch (authorizes nothing); reasonable, articulable suspicion (authorizes a brief stop and frisk); probable cause (authorizes an arrest, full search, or warrant). The quantum required climbs with the intrusion.

cards.front: Define probable cause (the quantum required for an arrest, full search, or warrant).
cards.back: Probable cause exists when the known facts give a fair probability that a crime has occurred or that evidence will be found in a particular place — measured by the totality of the circumstances through the eyes of a reasonable, experienced officer.

cards.front: Define reasonable, articulable suspicion and state what it authorizes.
cards.back: Specific facts and rational inferences pointing to criminal activity — more than a hunch but well short of probable cause. It authorizes only a brief investigative stop and protective frisk, not an arrest or full search.

cards.front: By what overall measure are both probable cause and reasonable suspicion judged?
cards.back: The totality of the circumstances, viewed through the eyes of a reasonable, experienced officer. Neither standard reduces to a fixed number.

cards.front: How does Brinegar v. United States (1949) frame probable cause?
cards.back: As practical, non-technical probabilities on which reasonable and prudent people act — not technical certainty.

cards.front: What did Terry v. Ohio (1968) hold is the minimum required for a brief investigative stop and protective frisk?
cards.back: Reasonable, articulable suspicion grounded in specific facts and rational inferences — not an officer's inchoate, unparticularized suspicion or hunch.

cards.front: Fill in Terry v. Ohio: due weight must be given, not to the officer's {{inchoate and unparticularized suspicion or "hunch"}}, but to the specific reasonable inferences he is entitled to draw from the facts in light of his experience.
cards.back: inchoate and unparticularized suspicion or "hunch"

cards.front: What standard for probable cause from an informant's tip did Illinois v. Gates (1983) adopt?
cards.back: The totality of the circumstances — a practical, common-sense "fair probability" inquiry. An informant's veracity, reliability, and basis of knowledge are relevant factors, but not independent, dispositive hurdles.

cards.front: Per Illinois v. Gates, what is the task of the issuing magistrate (quote the standard)?
cards.back: "To make a practical, common-sense decision whether, given all the circumstances set forth in the affidavit before him, including the 'veracity' and 'basis of knowledge' of persons supplying hearsay information, there is a fair probability that contraband or evidence of a crime will be found in a particular place."

cards.front: What was the Aguilar-Spinelli two-prong test, and is it still good law?
cards.back: HISTORY — superseded. It required an informant's affidavit to show (1) the informant's basis of knowledge and (2) his veracity/reliability as independent hurdles. Illinois v. Gates (1983) abandoned this rigid two-prong test in favor of totality of the circumstances.

cards.front: Distinction: How did the informant-tip analysis change from Aguilar-Spinelli to Illinois v. Gates?
cards.back: Under Aguilar-Spinelli, veracity and basis of knowledge were two independent prongs that each had to be satisfied. Under Gates, they survive only as relevant factors within a single totality-of-the-circumstances "fair probability" inquiry — a deficiency in one can be offset by a strong showing on the other.

cards.front: What did Alabama v. White (1990) hold about anonymous tips and reasonable suspicion?
cards.back: An anonymous tip can supply reasonable suspicion when police corroborate it — especially by verifying the tipster's accurate prediction of future conduct, which shows inside knowledge.

cards.front: What did Florida v. J.L. (2000) hold about a bare anonymous tip that a person is carrying a gun?
cards.back: Without more, it is NOT reasonable suspicion for a stop and frisk. An accurate description of a person's readily observable location and appearance does not show knowledge of concealed criminal activity.

cards.front: Distinction: Why does an anonymous tip support reasonable suspicion in Alabama v. White but not in Florida v. J.L.?
cards.back: In White the tip was corroborated by an accurate prediction of future conduct, showing inside knowledge. In J.L. the tip described only readily observable facts (location and appearance) and predicted nothing, so it did not show knowledge of concealed criminal activity.

cards.front: What did Navarette v. California (2014) hold about a 911 call reporting dangerous driving?
cards.back: It can supply reasonable suspicion for a stop when it bears adequate indicia of reliability — an eyewitness, contemporaneous report made on a recorded/traceable 911 system.

cards.front: In Navarette v. California, what features made the 911 tip reliable enough to support reasonable suspicion?
cards.back: The caller claimed eyewitness knowledge, made the report contemporaneously with the dangerous driving, and used the recorded, traceable 911 system (which deters false reports).

cards.front: What did Illinois v. Wardlow (2000) hold can furnish reasonable suspicion for a Terry stop?
cards.back: Unprovoked, headlong flight upon noticing police, combined with presence in a high-crime area, can furnish reasonable suspicion.

cards.front: Facts-to-case: A suspect in a high-crime area takes unprovoked, headlong flight the moment he notices police. Which case holds this can furnish reasonable suspicion for a Terry stop?
cards.back: Illinois v. Wardlow (2000).

cards.front: What did United States v. Arvizu (2002) hold about how reasonable suspicion is evaluated?
cards.back: It is judged on the totality of the circumstances — the "whole picture." Reviewing courts may NOT use a "divide-and-conquer" analysis that evaluates and discards each factor in isolation.

cards.front: Which two cases forbid the "divide-and-conquer" approach of evaluating and discarding each fact in isolation?
cards.back: United States v. Arvizu (2002) for reasonable suspicion and District of Columbia v. Wesby (2018) for probable cause — the magistrate and the court must weigh the whole picture, not pick off facts one by one.
cards.cite: District of Columbia v. Wesby, 583 U.S. 48, 63 (2018)

cards.front: What did District of Columbia v. Wesby (2018) hold about evaluating probable cause?
cards.back: Probable cause is a totality-of-the-circumstances inquiry; courts must not divide-and-conquer the facts by viewing each in isolation and discarding it.
cards.cite: District of Columbia v. Wesby, 583 U.S. 48, 63 (2018)

cards.front: What did Maryland v. Pringle (2003) hold when drugs and cash are found in a car and no occupant claims them?
cards.back: An officer has probable cause to arrest all the occupants on a reasonable inference of common enterprise.

cards.front: Probable cause must be particularized to a place or person — so how does Maryland v. Pringle reach every occupant of a car?
cards.back: Pringle allows a reasonable inference of common enterprise: where contraband is found in a car and no one claims it, that inference particularizes probable cause to each occupant. Particularity does not require individual-by-individual proof.

cards.front: What did Devenpeck v. Alford (2004) hold about the offense that must support an arrest?
cards.back: Probable cause is judged on the objective facts known to the officer; an arrest stands if those facts give probable cause for SOME offense — it need not be the offense the officer named, nor even closely related to it.

cards.front: Significance: Why does Devenpeck v. Alford matter for how probable cause is assessed?
cards.back: It confirms probable cause is an objective test — the officer's subjective stated reason or the label of the charge is irrelevant; what matters is whether the known objective facts support some criminal offense.

cards.front: What did Florida v. Harris (2013) hold about a trained drug dog's alert and probable cause?
cards.back: Whether the alert furnishes probable cause is a totality-of-the-circumstances question; a dog's satisfactory performance in a certification or training program can itself supply reason to trust the alert — no rigid checklist of field-record evidence is required.

cards.front: What appellate standard of review did Ornelas v. United States (1996) set for reasonable suspicion and probable cause?
cards.back: De novo — the ultimate RS/PC questions are reviewed fresh on appeal, while the underlying historical facts are reviewed only for clear error (with due weight to an officer's inferences).

cards.front: Fill in Ornelas v. United States: "We hold that the ultimate questions of reasonable suspicion and probable cause to make a warrantless search should be reviewed {{de novo}}."
cards.back: de novo

cards.front: Common pitfall: What is the error in treating reasonable suspicion and probable cause as the same thing?
cards.back: They are different rungs. Reasonable suspicion buys only a brief stop and frisk; it does NOT buy an arrest or a full search. The officer must articulate which standard the facts actually support.

cards.front: Common pitfall: Why is it wrong for an instructor to say "probable cause is 51%"?
cards.back: Neither standard reduces to a fixed percentage. Brinegar and Gates speak of practical, common-sense probabilities; quantifying probable cause as a number invents a rule the Supreme Court has never adopted.

cards.front: Common pitfall: "Possibilities, not probabilities." What does this CREW maxim warn against?
cards.back: Both standards turn on probabilities, not bare possibility. A fact that merely makes crime possible is not enough — Terry requires specific reasonable inferences, not an inchoate, unparticularized hunch. (Instructor maxim, not a case cite.)

cards.front: Search incident to arrest (SITA): what two areas does a lawful custodial arrest authorize officers to search without a warrant?
cards.back: (1) The arrestee's person, and (2) the area within the arrestee's immediate control (the "wingspan").

cards.front: What are the two rationales (the "twin engines") that justify a search incident to arrest?
cards.back: Officer safety and evidence preservation.

cards.front: SITA: what two predicate conditions must the arrest and search satisfy for the exception to apply?
cards.back: The arrest must be a lawful custodial arrest (not a mere citation), and the search must be contemporaneous with it.

cards.front: Chimel v. California (1969): what is the scope of a search incident to arrest?
cards.back: The arrestee's person plus the area within his immediate control (the "wingspan") — the area from which he might gain a weapon or destructible evidence.

cards.front: Chimel defined "the area within his immediate control" to mean the area from within which he might gain possession of a {{weapon or destructible evidence}}.
cards.back: weapon or destructible evidence

cards.front: Which SCOTUS case is the foundational source of the "wingspan" limit on a search incident to arrest?
cards.back: Chimel v. California (1969).

cards.front: United States v. Robinson (1973): what does a lawful custodial arrest permit as to the arrestee's person?
cards.back: A full search of the person — categorically, with no separate case-by-case showing that weapons or evidence are present.

cards.front: Robinson: "It is the fact of the lawful {{arrest}} which establishes the authority to search ... a full search of the person is ... also a 'reasonable' search."
cards.back: arrest

cards.front: How does the search of the person under Robinson (custodial arrest) differ from a Terry frisk in what must be shown?
cards.back: A Terry frisk needs case-by-case justification that weapons/evidence are present; the Robinson search of the person needs none — the lawful custodial arrest alone is enough.

cards.front: Riley v. California (2014): does the SITA bright-line rule reach the digital data on a cell phone seized incident to arrest?
cards.back: No. Officers must generally get a warrant before searching the phone's digital data.

cards.front: Riley v. California: state the Court's one-line answer for what police must do before searching a cell phone seized incident to arrest.
cards.back: "Get a warrant."

cards.front: Riley: why do the Chimel rationales not justify searching a phone's digital data incident to arrest?
cards.back: Digital data cannot itself be used as a weapon to harm the officer or to effectuate the arrestee's escape (and the data is not destroyed by securing the phone).

cards.front: Riley: what may officers still do with a phone incident to arrest, even without a warrant?
cards.back: They may seize the phone and inspect the physical device (e.g., for a razor blade hidden in the case) — but may not browse its digital data without a warrant.

cards.front: Common pitfall: an officer browses a phone's contents "incident to arrest." Why is this wrong?
cards.back: Riley forbids searching phone data on the arrest alone; the officer must seize it and get a warrant. (Inspecting the physical handset for a weapon is still fine.)

cards.front: New York v. Belton (1981): what is the SCOPE (the "where") of a vehicle search incident to arrest?
cards.back: The passenger compartment of the automobile and any containers found within it.

cards.front: Belton: within the passenger compartment, may officers examine the contents of containers found there?
cards.back: Yes — Belton's scope expressly includes examining the contents of any containers found within the passenger compartment.

cards.front: Arizona v. Gant (2009): state both triggers under which a vehicle search incident to arrest is lawful.
cards.back: (1) The arrestee is unsecured and within reaching distance of the passenger compartment at the time of the search, OR (2) it is reasonable to believe evidence relevant to the crime of arrest might be found in the vehicle.

cards.front: Gant prong 1: state the officer-safety/Chimel trigger for a vehicle search incident to arrest (verbatim).
cards.back: Police may search "only when the arrestee is unsecured and within reaching distance of the passenger compartment at the time of the search."

cards.front: Gant prong 2: state the evidentiary trigger for a vehicle search incident to arrest (verbatim).
cards.back: When it is "reasonable to believe evidence relevant to the crime of arrest might be found in the vehicle."

cards.front: Belton vs. Gant: which case fixes the scope/where of a vehicle SITA and which fixes the trigger/when?
cards.back: Belton fixes the scope (the "where" — passenger compartment + containers); Gant fixes the trigger (the "when" — the two-prong gate). "Gant is the gate, Belton is the room."

cards.front: What did Gant do to Belton doctrinally?
cards.back: Gant re-tethered Belton to Chimel: Belton's scope no longer authorizes a vehicle search by itself — you must first satisfy one of Gant's two triggers to reach Belton's scope.

cards.front: Common pitfall: treating Belton as authorizing an automatic vehicle search on arrest. Why is that wrong after Gant?
cards.back: After Gant there is no per se vehicle search on arrest — you must satisfy one of Gant's two triggers first; cuffing and securing the arrestee usually kills prong 1.

cards.front: Under Gant, what is usually the effect of routinely cuffing and securing the arrestee on the availability of a vehicle SITA?
cards.back: It usually kills prong 1 (arrestee no longer unsecured/within reach), leaving only prong 2 (reason to believe evidence of the crime of arrest is in the vehicle).

cards.front: United States v. Davis (4th Cir. 2021, persuasive): how far does Gant's first (reachability) prong extend?
cards.back: To non-vehicular containers — officers may search such a container (e.g., a backpack) incident to arrest only when the arrestee is unsecured and within reaching distance of it at the time of the search.
cards.cite: United States v. Howard Davis, 997 F.3d 191, 199 (4th Cir. 2021)

cards.front: Davis (4th Cir., persuasive): why was the backpack search not a valid search incident to arrest on the facts?
cards.back: Because Davis was secured and not within reaching distance of his backpack when the officer searched it, so there was no factual basis for a SITA under Gant's first holding.
cards.cite: United States v. Howard Davis, 997 F.3d 191, 202 (4th Cir. 2021)

cards.front: What is the precedential weight of United States v. Davis (4th Cir. 2021) on extending Gant prong 1 to containers, and how should it be framed?
cards.back: Persuasive Fourth Circuit authority only — not a SCOTUS rule. Use it narrowly and pair it with the binding Chimel/Gant anchors.
cards.cite: United States v. Howard Davis, 997 F.3d 191 (4th Cir. 2021)

cards.front: On whether Gant prong 1 applies outside vehicles, what is the circuit posture noted on the SITA page?
cards.back: Open but leaning uniform: the 3d, 9th, and 10th Circuits agree with the 4th and no circuit holds otherwise, but the 5th and 8th expressly declined to reach it — so it is not a clean split.

cards.front: How does a booking/inventory search differ from a search incident to arrest in what justifies it?
cards.back: SITA is justified by the arrest; an inventory is administrative caretaking justified by a standardized procedure (securing property, protecting against claims, guarding against dangerous items) — not by the arrest itself.

cards.front: Common pitfall: rescuing a failed SITA with an inventory theory. What condition must the inventory still satisfy?
cards.back: The predicate seizure/impoundment must itself be valid; a failed SITA cannot be saved by an inventory theory unless the impoundment was independently reasonable (the Braxton trap).

cards.front: Illinois v. Lafayette (1983): what may police search as part of booking an arrested person?
cards.back: Any container or article in the arrestee's possession, in accordance with established inventory procedures, as part of the routine procedure incident to incarceration.

cards.front: South Dakota v. Opperman (1976): when is a vehicle inventory search reasonable?
cards.back: When conducted pursuant to standard police procedures and not as a pretext concealing an investigatory motive.

cards.front: Colorado v. Bertine (1987): when is police discretion during an inventory search permissible?
cards.back: Only when exercised according to standardized criteria and on the basis of something other than suspicion of evidence of criminal activity.

cards.front: Bertine: "Our decisions have always adhered to the requirement that inventories be conducted according to {{standardized criteria}}."
cards.back: standardized criteria

cards.front: Florida v. Wells (1990): state the central guardrail on inventory searches.
cards.back: An inventory search must not be a ruse for a general rummaging to discover incriminating evidence; the policy must be designed to produce an inventory.

cards.front: Wells: "an inventory search must not be a {{ruse}} for a general rummaging in order to discover incriminating evidence."
cards.back: ruse

cards.front: Synthesizing Lafayette/Opperman/Bertine and Wells: what two requirements make an inventory search lawful (apart from a valid impoundment)?
cards.back: (a) It follows standardized criteria (Lafayette, Bertine, Opperman), and (b) it is not a ruse for investigation (Wells).

cards.front: United States v. Evans (10th Cir. 1991, persuasive): why was the inventory of the carry-on bag upheld?
cards.back: The officer followed the standardized inventory policy, there was no probable cause beforehand, and there was no evidence the search was a ruse — the textbook valid inventory.
cards.cite: United States v. Evans, 937 F.2d 1534, 1539 (10th Cir. 1991)

cards.front: United States v. Braxton (10th Cir. 2023, persuasive): why was the evidence suppressed despite the government's inevitable-discovery argument?
cards.back: The impoundment itself was not shown to be reasonable — a companion had arrived at Braxton's request and repeatedly asked to take the backpack, a reasonable alternative to impoundment. No valid impoundment means no valid inventory.
cards.cite: United States v. Braxton, 61 F.4th 830, 833 (10th Cir. 2023)

cards.front: Braxton (10th Cir., persuasive): is compliance with a written inventory/property policy a safe harbor?
cards.back: No — "the existence of and compliance with such a policy does not by itself establish a reasonable community-caretaking rationale." Policy compliance alone cannot save an unjustified impoundment.
cards.cite: United States v. Braxton, 61 F.4th 830, 838 (10th Cir. 2023)

cards.front: Evans vs. Braxton (both 10th Cir., persuasive): what is the contrast they teach about inventory searches?
cards.back: Evans is the valid inventory (policy followed, no ruse, valid predicate); Braxton is the cautionary one — suppression where the predicate impoundment itself was unjustified and an alternative to impoundment existed.

cards.front: Braxton (10th Cir., persuasive): what must officers consider before impounding property to support a later inventory?
cards.back: Reasonable alternatives to impoundment — e.g., releasing the property to a present companion who asks to take it.
cards.cite: United States v. Braxton, 61 F.4th 830, 833 (10th Cir. 2023)

cards.front: Knowles v. Iowa (1998): does writing a traffic citation (instead of making a custodial arrest) authorize a full search incident to arrest?
cards.back: No. There is no "search incident to citation" — without a custodial arrest, the search-incident-to-arrest authority does not apply.

cards.front: Knowles v. Iowa: why did the Court decline to extend Robinson's bright-line full-search rule to a citation situation?
cards.back: Because the concern for officer safety is not present to the same extent, and the concern for destruction or loss of evidence is not present at all once a citation is issued.

cards.front: Knowles v. Iowa: once a driver is stopped for speeding and issued a citation, what does the Court say about the evidence-preservation rationale?
cards.back: It is absent — "all the evidence necessary to prosecute that offense had been obtained" once the citation issues.

cards.front: Virginia v. Moore (2008): an arrest violates a state arrest statute but is backed by probable cause — is the arrest and the search incident to it a Fourth Amendment violation?
cards.back: No. A state-law arrest violation does not violate the Fourth Amendment; with probable cause, the arrest and the SITA are constitutionally reasonable and the SITA is valid.

cards.front: Virginia v. Moore: what is the principle about the relationship between state arrest law and Fourth Amendment reasonableness?
cards.back: State law does not define Fourth Amendment reasonableness — "it is not the province of the Fourth Amendment to enforce state law," and the Amendment does not require exclusion of evidence from a constitutionally permissible arrest.

cards.front: Common pitfall: assuming a state-law arrest defect bars the search incident to arrest. Why is that wrong under Moore?
cards.back: An arrest valid for 4A purposes (probable-cause-backed) supports the SITA even if it broke a state arrest statute — do not argue suppression on a state-law violation alone.

cards.front: Thornton v. United States (2004): how far does Belton's vehicle search-incident-to-arrest rule reach as to who was in the car?
cards.back: It reaches "recent occupants," not just those inside the vehicle — so long as the arrestee is the sort of recent occupant Thornton was, officers may search the vehicle incident to the arrest.

cards.front: Thornton: "So long as an arrestee is the sort of '{{recent occupant}}' of a vehicle such as petitioner was here, officers may search that vehicle incident to the arrest."
cards.back: recent occupant

cards.front: Thornton v. United States: what did Justice Scalia's concurrence in the judgment propose that the Court later adopted as Gant's second prong?
cards.back: Limiting Belton searches "to cases where it is reasonable to believe evidence relevant to the crime of arrest might be found in the vehicle."
cards.cite: Thornton v. United States, 541 U.S. 615, 632 (2004) (Scalia, J., concurring in the judgment)

cards.front: Trace the doctrinal arc of vehicle search incident to arrest from Belton through Thornton to Gant.
cards.back: Belton (scope = passenger compartment + containers) -> Thornton (extends to "recent occupants" + Scalia's evidence-of-the-offense idea) -> Gant (the two-prong gate that controls when a vehicle SITA is lawful).

cards.front: Birchfield v. North Dakota (2016): on a DUI arrest, may police take a warrantless breath test as a search incident to arrest? A warrantless blood test?
cards.back: Breath yes — a warrantless breath test may be administered as SITA. Blood no — a warrantless blood test may not; it needs a warrant (or another exception).

cards.front: Birchfield v. North Dakota: why does the Court permit a warrantless breath test but not a warrantless blood test incident to a DUI arrest?
cards.back: Because breath tests are significantly less intrusive than blood tests and in most cases amply serve law enforcement interests.

cards.front: Common pitfall: taking a warrantless blood draw as a search incident to arrest on a DUI. Why is that wrong under Birchfield?
