cards.back: Information that reveals 'the privacies of life.' Only the phrase 'the privacies of life' is the Court's own language; the three-prong framing itself is interpretive gloss.

cards.topic: The Third-Party Doctrine and Digital Surveillance
cards.front: In Kyllo v. United States, what did the Court hold about thermal imaging of a home?
cards.back: Using a thermal imager (sense-enhancing technology not in general public use) to detect interior details that would otherwise require physical intrusion is a search, presumptively unreasonable without a warrant.

cards.topic: The Third-Party Doctrine and Digital Surveillance
cards.front: Kyllo: 'Where, as here, the Government uses a device that is ___, to explore details of the home that would previously have been unknowable without physical intrusion, the surveillance is a "search"...'
cards.back: not in general public use

cards.topic: The Third-Party Doctrine and Digital Surveillance
cards.front: What separate line does Kyllo draw for surveillance technology?
cards.back: Sense-enhancing technology 'not in general public use' used to expose the interior of a home is a search.

cards.topic: The Third-Party Doctrine and Digital Surveillance
cards.front: What is the status of applying Carpenter to newer surveillance tech (pole cameras, license-plate readers, drones)?
cards.back: Unsettled — there is no settled federal rule; lower courts divide. It is the open frontier of the doctrine.

cards.topic: The Third-Party Doctrine and Digital Surveillance
cards.front: What is the scale/mosaic dividing line for evaluating new surveillance tech under Carpenter?
cards.back: A few fixed cameras capturing public movement tend to stay on the Smith/Knotts (not-a-search) side; comprehensive, city-wide, persistent tracking pulls toward Carpenter (warrant required).

cards.topic: The Third-Party Doctrine and Digital Surveillance
cards.front: In United States v. Hay (10th Cir. 2024), what did the court hold about ~2 months of pole-camera surveillance of a home's public-view exterior?
cards.back: It was not a search — the camera captured only what was visible to passersby from a public vantage, and the court held Carpenter did not abrogate its prior pole-camera precedent.
cards.cite: United States v. Hay, 95 F.4th 1304 (10th Cir. 2024)

cards.topic: The Third-Party Doctrine and Digital Surveillance
cards.front: How should United States v. Hay's pole-camera ruling be treated as authority?
cards.back: As one circuit's persuasive view, not a settled federal rule — pole-camera law is split and unsettled; circuits and state high courts divide on whether long-term pole-camera surveillance of a home is a search.
cards.cite: United States v. Hay, 95 F.4th 1304 (10th Cir. 2024)

cards.topic: The Third-Party Doctrine and Digital Surveillance
cards.front: In United States v. Porter (5th Cir. Mar. 17, 2026), what did the court hold about use of an automatic license-plate reader (ALPR)?
cards.back: Use of an ALPR for vehicle-location data was not a search, and the ALPR hit supplied reasonable suspicion for the stop. (Circuit — persuasive.)
cards.cite: United States v. Porter, No. 25-60163 (5th Cir. Mar. 17, 2026)

cards.topic: The Third-Party Doctrine and Digital Surveillance
cards.front: How must United States v. Porter (the ALPR case) be cited, and what cite must you avoid?
cards.back: Cite by docket: No. 25-60163 (5th Cir. Mar. 17, 2026). It is too recent for a confirmed F.4th reporter cite — never assert a reporter cite for it.
cards.cite: United States v. Porter, No. 25-60163 (5th Cir. Mar. 17, 2026)

cards.topic: The Third-Party Doctrine and Digital Surveillance
cards.front: In Robinson v. Commonwealth (Va. Ct. App. Apr. 7, 2026) (state — illustrative, non-binding), what did the court hold about a Flock ALPR camera network?
cards.back: A 172-camera Flock ALPR network was not a search — no REP in a vehicle's public movements, and it was not the 'near perfect surveillance' that triggered a warrant in Carpenter. (State — illustrative, non-binding.)
cards.cite: Robinson v. Commonwealth (Va. Ct. App. Apr. 7, 2026)

cards.topic: The Third-Party Doctrine and Digital Surveillance
cards.front: How must Robinson v. Commonwealth (the Flock ALPR case) be cited and weighted?
cards.back: Cite by court and date only — Va. Ct. App., Apr. 7, 2026 — as state, illustrative, non-binding. It is too recent for a confirmed S.E.2d reporter cite; never assert a reporter cite or a 2024 date for it.
cards.cite: Robinson v. Commonwealth (Va. Ct. App. Apr. 7, 2026)

cards.topic: The Third-Party Doctrine and Digital Surveillance
cards.front: In Leaders of a Beautiful Struggle v. Baltimore Police Dep't (4th Cir. 2021, en banc), what did the court hold about a city-wide aerial wide-area surveillance program?
cards.back: The program tracking everyone's public movements required a warrant under Carpenter — the scale/mosaic counterpoint to the ALPR cases.
cards.cite: Leaders of a Beautiful Struggle v. Baltimore Police Dep't, 2 F.4th 330 (4th Cir. 2021) (en banc)

cards.topic: The Third-Party Doctrine and Digital Surveillance
cards.front: Contrast the ALPR cases (Porter, Robinson) with Leaders of a Beautiful Struggle on the scale/mosaic line.
cards.back: The ALPR cases (limited camera capture of public movement) found no search; Leaders of a Beautiful Struggle (comprehensive city-wide aerial tracking) required a warrant — scale/persistence is the dividing line.

cards.topic: The Third-Party Doctrine and Digital Surveillance
cards.front: Is there a Supreme Court case directly governing police drone surveillance?
cards.back: No — there is no SCOTUS drone decision. Drone law is emerging, jurisdiction-dependent, and unsettled.

cards.topic: The Third-Party Doctrine and Digital Surveillance
cards.front: Which SCOTUS aerial-surveillance cases are the analogues for drones — and why might they not fit?
cards.back: The manned-aircraft lineage — Ciraolo, Riley, and Dow Chemical — is the bridge, but drones may not fit: they fly below navigable airspace, can hover/persist/zoom, and are cheap and pervasive, pulling toward Kyllo and Carpenter.

cards.topic: The Third-Party Doctrine and Digital Surveillance
cards.front: In California v. Ciraolo, what did the Court hold about naked-eye aerial observation of a fenced backyard from ~1,000 ft?
cards.back: It is not a search — the curtilage was knowingly exposed to any aircraft lawfully in public navigable airspace.

cards.topic: The Third-Party Doctrine and Digital Surveillance
cards.front: In Florida v. Riley, what did the plurality hold about naked-eye helicopter observation into curtilage at 400 ft?
cards.back: It was not a search (plurality), where the aircraft was in lawful, non-rare public airspace — the closest SCOTUS analogue for low-altitude drones.

cards.topic: The Third-Party Doctrine and Digital Surveillance
cards.front: In Dow Chemical Co. v. United States, what did the Court hold about EPA aerial photography of an industrial complex with a commonly available camera?
cards.back: It was not a search; sense-enhancement by widely available technology over open commercial areas did not cross the line — the contrast to Kyllo's 'not in general public use.'
cards.cite: Dow Chemical Co. v. United States, 476 U.S. 227 (1986)

cards.topic: The Third-Party Doctrine and Digital Surveillance
cards.front: Distinguish Kyllo from Dow Chemical on the surveillance-technology line.
cards.back: Kyllo: tech 'not in general public use' exposing a home's interior is a search. Dow Chemical: a commonly available aerial camera over commercial premises is not a search.

cards.topic: The Third-Party Doctrine and Digital Surveillance
cards.front: What is the pitfall of over-reading Carpenter?
cards.back: Treating it as a general repeal of the third-party doctrine. It is narrow — Smith and Miller remain good law, and it does not disturb conventional surveillance or ordinary business records.

cards.topic: The Third-Party Doctrine and Digital Surveillance
cards.front: Why is it a pitfall to treat Ciraolo/Riley as settled drone law?
cards.back: They predate drones and Carpenter; low, persistent, zoom-capable drones may not fit them cleanly. Drone law remains emerging and unsettled.

cards.front: Golden Rule #1 of the CSSI instructor maxims: how does articulation affect whether an action survives a suppression challenge?
cards.back: The more you articulate WHY you acted, the more likely the action is upheld. Reasonableness is judged on the specific, articulable facts the officer can name, not on a hunch.

cards.front: Which Supreme Court case supplies the legal core for Golden Rule #1 (articulate the facts), framing reasonableness around 'the facts available to the officer at the moment of the seizure'?
cards.back: Terry v. Ohio — reasonableness turns on whether the facts available to the officer at the moment of the seizure would warrant a person of reasonable caution in the belief that the action was appropriate.

cards.front: Terry v. Ohio standard for Golden Rule #1: reasonableness asks whether 'the facts available to the officer at the moment of the {{seizure}} or the search' warrant 'a man of reasonable {{caution}}' in the belief the action was appropriate.
cards.back: seizure; caution

cards.front: "Strive for Five" (CSSI articulation drill) — what is it?
cards.back: A training habit of naming at least five articulable factors for any action. It is a teaching device to force real-time articulation, NOT a five-factor legal requirement.

cards.front: Common pitfall: why is it wrong to teach "Strive for Five" as a legal element of Fourth Amendment analysis?
cards.back: There is no five-factor requirement anywhere in Fourth Amendment law. One decisive fact can suffice and ten weak ones may not. Four factors do not 'fail' and five do not 'pass' — it is a habit, not a threshold.

cards.front: What is the CSSI articulation form taught for Golden Rule #1 (how to state a justification)?
cards.back: State the opinion first, then "because →" the facts. It is an articulation drill (form of expression), not a legal standard.

cards.front: Common pitfall under Golden Rule #1: why is articulating reasons after the fact (inventing them for the report or the stand) worth little?
cards.back: Reasonableness is judged on the facts known at the moment of the action (Terry, 392 U.S. at 21-22). A suppression court reconstructs what the officer knew contemporaneously; reasons manufactured later carry little weight.

cards.front: Golden Rule #2 of the CSSI instructor maxims: how does the seriousness of the crime or circumstance affect reasonableness?
cards.back: The more serious the crime or circumstance, the more reasonable the action is viewed. Severity widens what the Fourth Amendment will tolerate (e.g., true emergencies justify warrantless home entry).

cards.front: Which Supreme Court case supplies the legal core for Golden Rule #2, making 'the severity of the crime at issue' an express factor in the reasonableness balance?
cards.back: Graham v. Connor — seizure reasonableness is judged by an objective standard from the perspective of a reasonable officer on the scene, weighing among other factors 'the severity of the crime at issue.'

cards.front: Under Graham v. Connor, from whose perspective and under what standard is the reasonableness of a seizure judged?
cards.back: From the perspective of a reasonable officer on the scene, under an objective standard — not with 20/20 hindsight.

cards.front: Graham v. Connor force-reasonableness factors: the court weighs 'the {{severity of the crime}} at issue,' whether the suspect poses an {{immediate threat}}, and whether he is actively {{resisting}}.
cards.back: severity of the crime; immediate threat; resisting

cards.front: Golden Rule #2 — what is the key limit on the idea that seriousness widens reasonableness?
cards.back: Seriousness is a multiplier on reasonableness, not a bypass. It widens the lens but never removes the requirement of articulable facts; severity is a factor (Graham), not a warrant exception.

cards.front: Common pitfall under Golden Rule #2: what mistake do officers make by 'letting serious crime do all the work'?
cards.back: Treating a grave offense as if it alone authorizes a sweep, entry, or prolonged detention. Severity is only one factor; the officer still needs the specific facts the relevant warrant exception requires.

cards.front: Golden Rule #3 of the CSSI instructor maxims: in what currency does the Fourth Amendment deal?
cards.back: The Fourth Amendment deals in PROBABILITIES, not POSSIBILITIES. A bare possibility is never enough; reasonableness runs on a hierarchy of probabilities.

cards.front: Which case crisply states the core of Golden Rule #3 with the line: 'In dealing with probable cause … we deal with probabilities'?
cards.back: Brinegar v. United States — probable cause deals with probabilities, which are 'the factual and practical considerations of everyday life on which reasonable and prudent men … act.'

cards.front: Brinegar v. United States: probabilities are 'the factual and practical considerations of {{everyday life}} on which reasonable and prudent men, not {{legal technicians}}, act.'
cards.back: everyday life; legal technicians

cards.front: How does Illinois v. Gates describe probable cause (anchoring Golden Rule #3, probabilities not possibilities)?
cards.back: As 'a fluid concept — turning on the assessment of probabilities in particular factual contexts — not readily, or even usefully, reduced to a neat set of legal rules.'

cards.front: What probable-cause test did Illinois v. Gates adopt?
cards.back: The totality-of-the-circumstances test — probable cause is a practical, common-sense probability judgment made on the whole picture, not a rigid checklist.

cards.front: Illinois v. Gates: 'probable cause is a {{fluid}} concept — turning on the assessment of {{probabilities}} in particular factual contexts.'
cards.back: fluid; probabilities

cards.front: Distinction (Golden Rule #3): why are 'someone could be inside' or 'drugs might be there' insufficient?
cards.back: That is the language of POSSIBILITY. The Fourth Amendment runs on PROBABILITY (Brinegar; Gates). Every justification must be pushed onto the burden ladder and the actual rung named.

cards.front: Name the burden-of-proof ladder, in order from lowest to highest, used to translate Fourth Amendment actions onto a probability scale.
cards.back: Mere suspicion → reasonable suspicion → probable cause → preponderance → clear and convincing → beyond a reasonable doubt.

cards.front: On the burden-of-proof ladder, what does the lowest rung — 'mere suspicion' — justify?
cards.back: Nothing. Mere suspicion (a hunch) is not enough to justify any Fourth Amendment intrusion.

cards.front: On the burden-of-proof ladder, what action does 'reasonable suspicion' authorize, and which case anchors that tier?
cards.back: A brief investigative Terry stop and frisk. The reasonable-suspicion tier comes from Terry v. Ohio.

cards.front: On the burden-of-proof ladder, what does 'probable cause' authorize, and which cases anchor that tier?
cards.back: An arrest and a search warrant. The probable-cause tier is anchored to Gates (totality-of-the-circumstances probability) and Brinegar (probabilities of everyday life).

cards.front: What does Maryland v. Buie require before officers may conduct a protective sweep beyond the spaces immediately adjoining the place of arrest?
cards.back: 'Articulable facts which, taken together with the rational inferences from those facts, would warrant a reasonably prudent officer in believing that the area to be swept harbors an individual posing a danger to those on the arrest scene.'

cards.front: Under Maryland v. Buie, is a protective sweep automatic incident to an in-home arrest?
cards.back: No — a protective sweep is 'decidedly not automati[c].' It requires a reasonable, articulable suspicion that the house harbors a person posing a danger; routine sweeps as a matter of course are unlawful.

cards.front: Under Maryland v. Buie, what is the permitted scope and duration of a protective sweep?
cards.back: A cursory inspection of only those spaces where a person may be found, lasting no longer than necessary to dispel the reasonable suspicion of danger.

cards.front: Why is Maryland v. Buie used to illustrate all three Golden Rules at once?
cards.back: It packs them into one doctrine: Rule 1 (articulate the danger), Rule 2 (a real safety threat), and Rule 3 (a probability of danger, not a bare possibility).

cards.front: Under Gaetjens v. Winnebago County (7th Cir. — persuasive, not binding), when may officials enter a home without a warrant under the emergency-aid exigency?
cards.back: When the officer had an objectively reasonable basis to believe someone inside needed immediate help — entry 'to render assistance or prevent harm to persons or property within.'
cards.cite: Gaetjens v. Winnebago County, 4 F.4th 487, 493-94 (7th Cir. 2021)

cards.front: What is the precedential weight of Gaetjens v. Winnebago County, and which Golden Rule does it illustrate?
cards.back: Persuasive only — it is a Seventh Circuit decision, not binding SCOTUS authority. It illustrates Golden Rule #2: seriousness/urgency (a medical emergency) widens what is reasonable, here a warrantless entry.
cards.cite: Gaetjens v. Winnebago County, 4 F.4th 487 (7th Cir. 2021)

cards.front: Common pitfall: what limits the scope of a warrantless entry justified by an exigency (e.g., emergency aid)?
cards.back: An emergency justifies entry for the emergency and no further. Officers must stay inside the nexus and purpose that justified entry; evidence gathered after the protective/aid purpose is satisfied risks suppression as exceeding the exception's scope.

cards.front: What three requirements make up the emergency-aid exigency as stated in the CSSI materials (anchored to Gaetjens, 7th Cir.)?
cards.back: (1) an objectively reasonable basis to believe a person inside needs immediate aid (or the premises pose an immediate safety threat); (2) a nexus between the emergency and the area entered; (3) scope limited to addressing the emergency.
cards.cite: Gaetjens v. Winnebago County, 4 F.4th 487, 493-94 (7th Cir. 2021)

cards.front: How should state-court protective-sweep decisions be treated relative to the Golden Rules?
cards.back: As persuasive, illustrative only — never as the controlling rule. The binding federal authority for protective sweeps is Maryland v. Buie.

cards.front: What kind of authority are the 3 Golden Rules and "Strive for Five" themselves?
cards.back: Instructor-development maxims and articulation habits — NOT legal tests or case holdings. Each maxim's legal core is separately anchored to verified federal authority (Terry, Graham, Brinegar, Gates, Buie).

cards.front: Under the Fourth Amendment, what is a traffic stop, and what justifies it?
cards.back: A seizure of the vehicle's occupants, justified — like a Terry stop — by reasonable articulable suspicion (or probable cause) of a traffic or criminal violation.

cards.front: What did Delaware v. Prouse hold about random license/registration stops?
cards.back: Random, suspicionless stops of motorists to check license and registration are unreasonable; an officer needs at least articulable and reasonable suspicion of a violation.

cards.front: Does Prouse forbid all suspicionless vehicle stops, including checkpoints?
cards.back: No — Prouse bars random, standardless discretionary stops; it does not foreclose neutral systematic checkpoints/roadblocks.

cards.front: What did Whren v. United States hold about an officer's pretextual motive for a traffic stop?
cards.back: Pretext is irrelevant — an objective traffic violation / probable cause justifies the stop regardless of the officer's actual subjective motivation.

cards.front: Whren: "________ play no role in ordinary, probable-cause Fourth Amendment analysis."
cards.back: Subjective intentions

cards.front: After Whren, is the constitutional reasonableness of a traffic stop a subjective or objective inquiry?
cards.back: Objective — the stop's reasonableness does not depend on the actual motivations of the individual officers involved.

cards.front: Under what constitutional provision is race-based selective traffic enforcement challenged — and is it the Fourth Amendment?
cards.back: The Equal Protection Clause, not the Fourth Amendment. Whren is not a green light for racial profiling; the two doctrines must not be conflated.

cards.front: Common pitfall: why is it wrong to teach Whren as cover for racial profiling?
cards.back: Whren only blesses a pretextual stop built on an objective violation; race-based enforcement remains unconstitutional under Equal Protection. Conflating pretext with race is the error.

cards.front: What did Rodriguez v. United States hold about prolonging a traffic stop?
cards.back: Police may not extend a stop beyond its mission — even briefly — for unrelated investigation absent independent reasonable suspicion.

cards.front: Under Rodriguez, what governs a traffic stop's lawful duration?
cards.back: Its mission — addressing the violation and ordinary incidents (license, registration, warrant checks, issuing the ticket).

cards.front: Rodriguez: a seizure justified by a police-observed traffic violation "become[s] unlawful if it is prolonged beyond the time reasonably required to complete th[e] ________" of issuing a ticket.
cards.back: mission

cards.front: Under Rodriguez, what is the measure of a stop's reasonable duration?
cards.back: Diligence — whether the police diligently pursued the stop's mission.

cards.front: Under Rodriguez, when are unrelated questions or unavoidable downtime during a stop permissible?
cards.back: So long as they do not add time to the stop — multitasking during the mission is fine; adding time is not.

cards.front: Common pitfall: is a brief, 'de minimis' dog-sniff delay tacked on after the mission is complete lawful under Rodriguez?
cards.back: No — even a brief added delay is unlawful without independent reasonable suspicion. The question is not 'how long' but 'did it add time beyond the diligently-pursued mission.'

cards.front: What did Heien v. North Carolina hold about an officer's mistake of law?
cards.back: An objectively reasonable mistake of law can supply the reasonable suspicion needed to justify a stop.

cards.front: Heien: why should a reasonable mistake of law be treated the same as a reasonable mistake of fact?
cards.back: There is no reason under the text of the Fourth Amendment or precedent why a result acceptable via a reasonable mistake of fact should be unacceptable via a similarly reasonable mistake of law.

cards.front: How is Heien cabined — does it excuse an officer's ignorance of clear law?
cards.back: No. The mistake must itself be objectively reasonable; Heien is not a license to be ignorant of clear, settled law.

cards.front: What did Pennsylvania v. Mimms hold about ordering occupants out of a lawfully stopped car?
cards.back: An officer may order the driver out of a lawfully detained vehicle as a matter of course, without violating the Fourth Amendment.

cards.front: What did Maryland v. Wilson add to the Mimms order-out rule?
cards.back: An officer making a traffic stop may order passengers — not just the driver — out of the car pending completion of the stop.

cards.front: Distinguish Mimms and Wilson: who may be ordered out of a lawfully stopped car, and under which case?
cards.back: The driver under Mimms (1977); the passengers under Wilson (1997). Together they cover all occupants.

cards.front: Ordering occupants out of a lawfully stopped car: is it a search, and does it need separate suspicion?
cards.back: No to both — it is a control measure, not a search, and needs no separate suspicion.

cards.front: What did Michigan v. Long hold about protective searches of a vehicle?
cards.back: Terry's protective-frisk rationale extends to cars: police may search the passenger compartment for weapons on reasonable suspicion the suspect is dangerous and may gain immediate control of weapons.

cards.front: State the Michigan v. Long protective-search test (verbatim standard).
cards.back: "[T]he search of the passenger compartment of an automobile, limited to those areas in which a weapon may be placed or hidden, is permissible if the police officer possesses a reasonable belief based on 'specific and articulable facts which, taken together with the rational inferences from those facts, reasonably warrant' the officer in believing that the suspect is dangerous and the suspect may gain immediate control of weapons."

cards.front: What is the scope of a Michigan v. Long protective vehicle frisk?
cards.back: Tied to the weapon — limited to the areas (and containers) of the passenger compartment where a weapon may be placed or hidden, and no further.

cards.front: What predicate does a Michigan v. Long protective vehicle frisk require — and does it require an arrest?
cards.back: Reasonable suspicion that a weapon is present / the suspect is dangerous. It requires no arrest — it is a Terry frisk for cars.

cards.front: Common pitfall: how does the Long protective vehicle frisk differ from a search incident to arrest (Belton/Gant)?
cards.back: The Long frisk needs only reasonable suspicion of a weapon, requires no arrest, and is scope-limited to weapon-sized areas; it is not the broader Belton/Gant search incident to arrest. (SITA is treated on its own page.)

cards.front: What did United States v. Vinton (D.C. Cir. — persuasive) hold about a Long protective search after the detainee is handcuffed?
cards.back: The Long/Terry protective vehicle search is not abated by handcuffing or removing the detainee — because a Terry detainee, unlike an arrestee, may be released back to the car and could then access a weapon. (Persuasive D.C. Circuit only; point rests in a footnote.)
cards.cite: United States v. Vinton, 594 F.3d 14, 24-25 n.3 (D.C. Cir. 2010)

cards.front: What is the precedential weight of United States v. Vinton, and how should it be taught alongside Michigan v. Long?
cards.back: Persuasive D.C. Circuit authority only (not binding nationwide), and its protective-search point rests in a footnote — pair it with binding Michigan v. Long; do not assert it as settled across all circuits.
cards.cite: United States v. Vinton, 594 F.3d 14 (D.C. Cir. 2010)

cards.front: Distinction: do handcuffs end the weapon threat for a Terry protective vehicle search (Vinton) the same way Gant's secured-arrestee limit applies to SITA?
cards.back: No — for a Terry protective search the detainee may be released back to the car, so handcuffs do not end the threat (Vinton). Contrast SITA, where Gant's secured-arrestee limit does apply.

cards.front: What is the reasonable-suspicion predicate from Terry v. Ohio that underlies traffic-stop doctrine?
cards.back: An investigative detention needs specific, articulable reasonable suspicion — weight goes not to an inchoate, unparticularized suspicion or 'hunch' but to specific reasonable inferences drawn from the facts in light of the officer's experience.

cards.front: Which case: an officer stops a car based on a belief about the law that turns out to be wrong, but the legal error was objectively reasonable?
cards.back: Heien v. North Carolina (2014) — a reasonable mistake of law can supply reasonable suspicion.

cards.front: Which case: after a traffic ticket is handled, the officer holds the driver for a dog sniff without independent reasonable suspicion?
cards.back: Rodriguez v. United States (2015) — extending the stop beyond its mission, even briefly, is unlawful absent independent reasonable suspicion.

cards.front: Where do Belton (vehicle SITA scope) and Gant (vehicle SITA trigger) belong relative to traffic-stop doctrine?
cards.back: They are search-incident-to-arrest cases — cross-referenced here only; their full treatment lives on the Search Incident to Arrest page. They are not the traffic-stop protective-frisk rule.

cards.front: What did Brendlin v. California hold about a passenger during a traffic stop?
cards.back: A passenger is seized by the stop just as the driver is, and so may challenge the constitutionality of the stop.

cards.front: Why is Brendlin v. California load-bearing for a suppression motion?
cards.back: Because the passenger is seized, the passenger has standing to move to suppress the fruits of an unlawful stop — not just the driver.

cards.front: Brendlin: "When a police officer makes a traffic stop, the driver of the car is seized within the meaning of the Fourth Amendment. … We hold that a ________ is seized as well and so may challenge the constitutionality of the stop."
cards.back: passenger

cards.front: What did Arizona v. Johnson hold about frisking a passenger during a traffic stop?
cards.back: An officer may frisk a passenger only on reasonable suspicion that the person is armed and dangerous; no separate suspicion that the passenger is engaged in criminal activity is required.

cards.front: Arizona v. Johnson: state the verbatim standard for patting down a driver or passenger during a traffic stop.
cards.back: "To justify a patdown of the driver or a passenger during a traffic stop, however, just as in the case of a pedestrian reasonably suspected of criminal activity, the police must harbor reasonable suspicion that the person subjected to the frisk is armed and dangerous."

cards.front: Common pitfall: does authority to order a passenger out of the car (Wilson) include authority to frisk that passenger?
cards.back: No — two different thresholds. Wilson lets you order a passenger out with no suspicion; Johnson requires reasonable suspicion the passenger is armed and dangerous before any pat-down. Ordering out is not authority to frisk.

cards.front: Distinguish the three control/safety measures during a traffic stop: order out (Mimms/Wilson), frisk the person (Johnson), and frisk the car (Long) — what predicate does each need?
cards.back: Order out (Mimms/Wilson): no suspicion. Frisk the person (Johnson): reasonable suspicion the person is armed and dangerous. Frisk the car's passenger compartment (Long): reasonable suspicion of a weapon.

cards.front: What did Illinois v. Caballes hold about a dog sniff during a lawful traffic stop?
cards.back: A dog sniff during a concededly lawful traffic stop is not a Fourth Amendment search — it reveals only the location of contraband no one has a right to possess.

cards.front: Illinois v. Caballes: state the verbatim holding on the dog sniff.
cards.back: "A dog sniff conducted during a concededly lawful traffic stop that reveals no information other than the location of a substance that no individual has any right to possess does not violate the Fourth Amendment."

cards.front: Under Caballes, if the sniff itself is not a search, what is the only constitutional question — and how does it tie to Rodriguez?
cards.back: Whether the sniff prolonged the stop beyond its mission. The only defect is added time (Rodriguez); Caballes is the antecedent that the Rodriguez mission rule polices.

cards.front: After a dog sniff (Caballes) gives a positive alert, what does the alert supply?
cards.back: Probable cause to search the vehicle under the automobile exception.

cards.front: Common pitfall: is "the dog sniff is the violation" a correct statement of the law?
cards.back: Wrong — Caballes: the sniff itself is not a search. The only constitutional defect is added time (Rodriguez), not the sniff.

cards.front: What did United States v. Sharpe hold about the duration of an investigative stop?
cards.back: There is no rigid time limit — the question is whether police diligently pursued a means of investigation likely to confirm or dispel their suspicions quickly.

cards.front: Sharpe: in assessing a stop's duration, courts examine whether the police "________ pursued a means of investigation that was likely to confirm or dispel their suspicions quickly."
cards.back: diligently

cards.front: Common pitfall: is "the stop took X minutes, therefore it was unlawful" the correct test for a stop's duration?
cards.back: Wrong — Sharpe: the test is diligence (did police pursue a means likely to confirm or dispel suspicion quickly), not a fixed number of minutes. Duration is a diligence test, not a stopwatch.

cards.front: What did Kansas v. Glover hold about reasonable suspicion to stop a vehicle?
cards.back: When an officer lacks information negating the inference that the registered owner (whose license is revoked) is the driver, the stop is reasonable — reasonable suspicion may rest on a common-sense inference.

cards.front: Kansas v. Glover: state the verbatim holding (the closing sentence).
cards.back: "We hold that when the officer lacks information negating an inference that the owner is the driver of the vehicle, the stop is reasonable."

cards.front: Under Glover, must the reasonable-suspicion predicate come from direct observation of the driver?
cards.back: No — officers may draw reasonable inferences from probabilities (e.g., the registered owner is likely the driver) so long as nothing on hand negates the inference. It pairs with Prouse's RAS floor and Terry's "not a hunch."

cards.front: Which case: an officer runs a plate, learns the registered owner's license is revoked, and stops the car without seeing who is driving?
cards.back: Kansas v. Glover (2020) — absent information negating the inference that the owner is the driver, the stop is reasonable.

cards.front: (5A / Miranda cross-doctrine) What did Berkemer v. McCarty hold about whether a routine traffic stop is Miranda "custody"?
cards.back: An ordinary traffic stop is Terry-like, not a formal arrest, and does NOT place the motorist in Miranda 'custody'; roadside questioning during a routine stop does not require Miranda warnings. This is a Fifth Amendment / Miranda point, not a Fourth Amendment reasonableness holding.

cards.front: (5A / Miranda cross-doctrine) Berkemer v. McCarty: state the verbatim analogy the Court drew for the usual traffic stop.
cards.back: "In both of these respects, the usual traffic stop is more analogous to a so-called 'Terry stop,' see Terry v. Ohio, 392 U. S. 1 (1968), than to a formal arrest."

cards.front: Is Berkemer v. McCarty a Fourth Amendment reasonableness holding, and why is it taught on the traffic-stops page?
cards.back: No — it is a Fifth Amendment / Miranda holding (a traffic stop is not custodial interrogation). It is included only to reinforce the seizure framing (the stop is Terry-like), not as a 4A reasonableness rule.

cards.front: What are the TWO independent theories under which government conduct becomes a Fourth Amendment "search"?
cards.back: (1) Trespass/property — a physical intrusion on a constitutionally protected area to obtain information (Jones); (2) reasonable expectation of privacy (Katz). Either one alone suffices.

cards.front: Under the two-theories framework, what is the relationship between the trespass theory and the Katz privacy theory — must conduct satisfy both, or is one enough?
cards.back: The two theories are independent — satisfying either one alone makes the conduct a search; neither is required to also satisfy the other.

cards.front: How does the TRESPASS theory of a search differ from the PRIVACY (Katz) theory?
cards.back: Trespass focuses on a physical intrusion onto a protected area (person, house, paper, effect) to obtain information; privacy focuses on invading an expectation society accepts as reasonable, with no physical intrusion required at all.

cards.front: Are the trespass theory and the privacy theory of a search co-extensive — and if not, how do they relate?
cards.back: Not co-extensive. They overlap but are distinct — a trespass is often, but not always, also a privacy violation, and a privacy invasion can occur with no trespass.

cards.front: What TWO elements does the Jones trespass test for a search require?
cards.back: (1) A physical intrusion on a constitutionally protected area, conjoined with (2) intent to obtain information. Trespass alone is not enough.
cards.cite: United States v. Jones, 565 U.S. 400, 408 n.5 (2012)

cards.front: Jones (trespass test): "{{c1::Trespass alone does not qualify}}, but there must be conjoined with that ... an attempt to find something or to obtain information."
cards.back: Trespass alone does not qualify — there must also be intent/attempt to obtain information.
cards.cite: United States v. Jones, 565 U.S. 400, 408 n.5 (2012)

cards.front: Under the trespass theory, the physical intrusion must be onto which constitutionally protected areas?
cards.back: The Fourth Amendment's enumerated categories: "persons, houses, papers, and effects."

cards.front: The familiar two-prong reasonable-expectation-of-privacy test — does it come from the Katz MAJORITY or from a concurrence?
cards.back: From Justice Harlan's CONCURRENCE, not the majority opinion.
cards.cite: Katz v. United States, 389 U.S. 347, 361 (1967) (Harlan, J., concurring)

cards.front: What are the two prongs of Justice Harlan's Katz expectation-of-privacy test?
cards.back: (1) A person exhibited an actual (subjective) expectation of privacy; and (2) one society is prepared to recognize as "reasonable."
cards.cite: Katz v. United States, 389 U.S. 347, 361 (1967) (Harlan, J., concurring)

cards.front: What is the SUBJECTIVE prong of Harlan's Katz privacy test?
cards.back: That the person exhibited an actual (subjective) expectation of privacy.
cards.cite: Katz v. United States, 389 U.S. 347, 361 (1967) (Harlan, J., concurring)

cards.front: What is the OBJECTIVE prong of Harlan's Katz privacy test?
cards.back: That the expectation is one society is prepared to recognize as "reasonable."
cards.cite: Katz v. United States, 389 U.S. 347, 361 (1967) (Harlan, J., concurring)

cards.front: What rule did the Katz MAJORITY actually state (as opposed to Harlan's concurrence)?
cards.back: "The Fourth Amendment protects people, not places"; and "[w]hat a person knowingly exposes to the public ... is not a subject of Fourth Amendment protection."

cards.front: Katz majority: "The Fourth Amendment protects {{c1::people, not places}}."
cards.back: people, not places

cards.front: Government attached a listening/recording device to the outside of a public telephone booth, with no physical penetration of the booth. Which case, and was it a search?
cards.back: Katz v. United States (1967) — yes, it was a search even though there was no trespass; it overruled Olmstead's trespass-only view.

cards.front: Which earlier wiretap decision did Katz overrule, and on what point?
cards.back: Katz overruled Olmstead v. United States (1928) on its trespass-only view — a search can occur with no physical entry.

cards.front: What did Olmstead v. United States (1928) hold about wiretapping that involved no physical entry of the defendants' premises?
cards.back: That it was NOT a Fourth Amendment search — a pure property/trespass framing requiring a physical entry.

cards.front: Olmstead's no-search rationale: "There was no searching. There was no seizure. ... There was no {{c1::entry of the houses or offices of the defendants}}."
cards.back: entry of the houses or offices of the defendants

cards.front: Which Olmstead dissent foreshadowed the modern privacy theory, and with what phrase?
cards.back: Justice Brandeis's dissent, urging a broad "right to be let alone."
cards.cite: Olmstead v. United States, 277 U.S. 438, 478 (1928) (Brandeis, J., dissenting)

cards.front: Is Olmstead simply a dead letter today?
cards.back: No. It is overruled on the privacy point by Katz, but its property/physical-entry instinct was REVIVED by Jones as an independent route to finding a search.

cards.front: Officers pushed a "spike mike" into a party wall until it contacted the heating duct of the house. Which case, and was it a search?
cards.back: Silverman v. United States (1961) — yes, an unauthorized physical penetration into a constitutionally protected area, and thus a search.

cards.front: What did Silverman v. United States (1961) hold the test turned on — and what did it expressly decline to rest on?
cards.back: It turned on an unauthorized physical intrusion into a constitutionally protected area; the Court expressly declined to rest on local-law refinements of "technical trespass."

cards.front: Per Silverman, must a Fourth Amendment trespass track state property/tort law to count as a search?
cards.back: No. "Inherent Fourth Amendment rights are not inevitably measurable in terms of ancient niceties of tort or real property law" — the unauthorized physical penetration into the home was enough.

cards.front: Government installed a GPS tracker on a vehicle and monitored its movements. Which case, and on which theory was it a search?
cards.back: United States v. Jones (2012) — a search under the revived TRESPASS theory (physical intrusion on an "effect" to obtain information), without reaching Katz.

cards.front: Why does Jones matter — what is its significance in the search-definition framework?
cards.back: It is the controlling modern trespass-search case: it revived the property/trespass theory as an independent route to a "search," running in parallel with Katz.

cards.front: Jones described the trespass as: "The Government physically {{c1::occupied private property for the purpose of obtaining information}}."
cards.back: occupied private property for the purpose of obtaining information

cards.front: Did Jones REPLACE the Katz privacy test with the trespass test?
cards.back: No. Jones revived the property/trespass baseline that Katz had supplemented; it did not overrule Katz. The two theories now run in parallel.

cards.front: Jones on the relationship between the theories: "Katz did not {{c1::narrow the Fourth Amendment's scope}}."
cards.back: narrow the Fourth Amendment's scope

cards.front: Per Jones, do a defendant's Fourth Amendment rights depend solely on the Katz formulation?
cards.back: No — a defendant's "Fourth Amendment rights do not rise or fall with the Katz formulation." The trespass theory is an independent basis.

cards.front: How did Jones (quoting Soldal v. Cook County) describe what Katz did to property-based protection?
cards.back: Katz "established that property rights are not the sole measure of Fourth Amendment violations, but did not snuff out the previously recognized protection for property."
cards.cite: United States v. Jones, 565 U.S. 400, 407 (2012) (quoting Soldal v. Cook County, 506 U.S. 56, 64 (1992))

cards.front: Officers brought a drug-detection dog onto the front porch of a home to sniff for narcotics. Which case, and on which theory was it a search?
cards.back: Florida v. Jardines (2013) — a search under the Jones TRESPASS theory: entering the curtilage with the dog exceeded the implied license to approach and knock.

cards.front: What is Jardines's significance for the trespass theory of a search?
cards.back: It applied the Jones trespass theory to the home's curtilage — exceeding the implied license to approach is a trespassory search, and the Court did not need to reach Katz privacy.

cards.front: What did Carpenter v. United States (2018) hold about the government acquiring extended historical cell-site location information (CSLI)?
cards.back: It is a search — a person has a reasonable expectation of privacy in "the whole of their physical movements."

cards.front: Carpenter: individuals have a reasonable expectation of privacy in "the {{c1::whole of their physical movements}}."
cards.back: whole of their physical movements

cards.front: What is the third-party doctrine, and what did Carpenter do to it?
cards.back: The third-party doctrine is the rule that information voluntarily shared with a third party generally loses Fourth Amendment protection. Carpenter NARROWED it for pervasive digital location data; it did not abolish it.

cards.front: In the historical arc of the privacy theory, what does Carpenter represent?
cards.back: The Katz privacy theory recalibrating to new technology — applying reasonable-expectation-of-privacy analysis to pervasive digital-age location data.

cards.front: Common pitfall: an officer assumes "no physical trespass means no search." Why is that wrong?
cards.back: That was Olmstead, which Katz overruled. A bug on a phone booth, a thermal scan, or long-term CSLI can be a search with zero physical entry under the privacy theory.

cards.front: Common pitfall: an officer assumes "no privacy invasion means no search." Why is that wrong?
cards.back: Jones and Jardines find a search on TRESPASS grounds even where a pure Katz analysis is contested (e.g., a car's location on public roads). Trespass to gather information is independently a search.

cards.front: Common pitfall: attributing the two-prong reasonable-expectation test to the Katz majority. What is the correct attribution?
cards.back: It is Justice Harlan's concurrence — cite it as Katz, 389 U.S. at 361 (Harlan, J., concurring), not the majority.
cards.cite: Katz v. United States, 389 U.S. 347, 361 (1967) (Harlan, J., concurring)

cards.front: Name the historical arc of the TRESPASS theory of a search (three cases, in order).
cards.back: Olmstead (1928, property/entry framing) → Silverman (1961, physical penetration into the home) → Jones (2012, revived trespass theory) — extended to curtilage in Jardines (2013).

cards.front: Name the historical arc of the PRIVACY theory of a search (two anchor cases, in order).
cards.back: Katz (1967, reasonable expectation of privacy supplements property) → Carpenter (2018, privacy theory recalibrated for digital-age CSLI).

cards.front: Did Katz SUPPLANT the property baseline or merely SUPPLEMENT it?
cards.back: It supplemented it. Katz added the privacy theory; it did not eliminate property-based protection, which Jones later revived as an independent route.

cards.front: Under what constitutional standard is force used during an arrest, investigatory stop, or other seizure of a free person judged (Graham v. Connor)?
cards.back: The Fourth Amendment's objective-reasonableness standard — not substantive due process.

cards.front: Graham v. Connor: quote the holding on how excessive-force claims during a seizure must be analyzed.
cards.back: "[A]ll claims that law enforcement officers have used excessive force ... in the course of an arrest, investigatory stop, or other 'seizure' of a free citizen should be analyzed under the Fourth Amendment and its 'reasonableness' standard, rather than under a 'substantive due process' approach."

cards.front: Graham v. Connor: "The 'reasonableness' of a particular use of force must be judged from the perspective of {{c1::a reasonable officer on the scene}}, rather than with the {{c2::20/20 vision of hindsight}}."
cards.back: a reasonable officer on the scene / the 20/20 vision of hindsight

cards.front: Graham v. Connor: from whose perspective, and at what moment, is a use of force judged for reasonableness?
cards.back: From the perspective of a reasonable officer on the scene at the moment force is used — not with the 20/20 vision of hindsight.

cards.front: Graham v. Connor: quote verbatim the three named factors that guide the reasonableness inquiry.
cards.back: "[T]he severity of the crime at issue, whether the suspect poses an immediate threat to the safety of the officers or others, and whether he is actively resisting arrest or attempting to evade arrest by flight."

cards.front: Graham v. Connor: what is the FIRST named reasonableness factor?
cards.back: The severity of the crime at issue.

cards.front: Graham v. Connor: what is the SECOND named reasonableness factor?
cards.back: Whether the suspect poses an immediate threat to the safety of the officers or others.

cards.front: Graham v. Connor: what is the THIRD named reasonableness factor?
cards.back: Whether the suspect is actively resisting arrest or attempting to evade arrest by flight.

cards.front: Graham v. Connor: what word in the opinion shows the three named factors are non-exclusive rather than a closed checklist, and what follows from it?
cards.back: The word "including." Because the factors are non-exclusive, they are the starting point and force is judged on the totality of the circumstances.

cards.front: Common pitfall (Graham): what is the error in judging force only against severity, threat, and resistance?
cards.back: Treating the three factors as a closed test. Graham says "including" — they are illustrative; the standard is the totality of the circumstances, so you must account for the rest of the scene.

cards.front: Graham v. Connor: what role does the officer's underlying intent or motivation play in the reasonableness inquiry?
cards.back: None — the inquiry is purely objective: whether the actions are objectively reasonable in light of the facts confronting the officer, without regard to underlying intent or motivation.

cards.front: Graham v. Connor: quote the line on how an officer's intentions affect (or don't affect) the analysis.
cards.back: "An officer's evil intentions will not make a Fourth Amendment violation out of an objectively reasonable use of force; nor will an officer's good intentions make an objectively unreasonable use of force constitutional."

cards.front: Common pitfall (Graham): why does a "good faith" or "malicious/sadistic" framing miss the mark on an excessive-force claim?
cards.back: Because reasonableness is objective. Graham rejected the substantive-due-process good-faith test; an officer's subjective good or bad intent does not change whether the force was objectively reasonable.

cards.front: Graham v. Connor: what allowance must the "calculus of reasonableness" make for the conditions officers face?
cards.back: Allowance for the fact that officers are often forced to make split-second judgments in circumstances that are tense, uncertain, and rapidly evolving about the amount of force necessary.

cards.front: What doctrine does Graham v. Connor anchor — why does it matter for any use-of-force question?
cards.back: It establishes that using force during a seizure is itself a Fourth Amendment event, governing all such claims by a single objective-reasonableness standard rather than substantive due process.

cards.front: Graham v. Connor arose as a civil 42 U.S.C. § 1983 suit. For a suppression-focused analysis, what does it supply and what stays out of scope?
cards.back: It supplies the governing Fourth Amendment force standard (objective reasonableness). Whether an officer is personally liable — qualified immunity and § 1983 damages — is a separate civil question and does not change what the Fourth Amendment requires.

cards.front: Tennessee v. Garner: when may deadly force be used against a fleeing suspect?
cards.back: Only where it is necessary to prevent escape and the officer has probable cause to believe the suspect poses a significant threat of death or serious physical injury to the officer or others.

cards.front: Tennessee v. Garner: quote the rule limiting deadly force against a fleeing suspect.
cards.back: "[S]uch force may not be used unless it is necessary to prevent the escape and the officer has probable cause to believe that the suspect poses a significant threat of death or serious physical injury to the officer or others."

cards.front: Tennessee v. Garner: "[A]pprehension by the use of {{c1::deadly force}} is a {{c2::seizure}} subject to the reasonableness requirement of the Fourth Amendment."
cards.back: deadly force / seizure

cards.front: Which case held that shooting an apparently unarmed, non-dangerous fleeing suspect to stop the escape was an unreasonable seizure?
cards.back: Tennessee v. Garner, 471 U.S. 1 (1985).

cards.front: Scott v. Harris: how did the Court characterize Garner's relationship to Graham's reasonableness standard?
cards.back: Garner is not a rigid separate test; it is "simply an application" of Graham's Fourth Amendment reasonableness standard to a particular type of force.

cards.front: Scott v. Harris: quote the "on/off switch" passage on Garner.
cards.back: "Garner did not establish a magical on/off switch that triggers rigid preconditions whenever an officer's actions constitute 'deadly force.' Garner was simply an application of the Fourth Amendment's 'reasonableness' test ... to the use of a particular type of force."

cards.front: Scott v. Harris: how did the Court rule on the officer's decision to ram a fleeing motorist whose reckless high-speed flight endangered the public?
cards.back: It was reasonable — the Court had "little difficulty in concluding it was reasonable" for the officer to take the action he did.

cards.front: Common pitfall: what is wrong with reading Garner as a rigid two-part precondition for any use of deadly force?
cards.back: Scott v. Harris corrects it — there is no magical on/off switch. Garner is Graham reasonableness applied to a non-dangerous fleeing suspect; a dangerous fleeing suspect (e.g., a reckless high-speed driver) is a different totality, so reasonableness still turns on the totality of the circumstances.

cards.front: Common pitfall: how do the Fourth Amendment force standard and officer liability differ?
cards.back: Graham fixes the constitutional force standard (objective reasonableness). Qualified immunity and § 1983 damages are separate civil questions and do not change what the Fourth Amendment requires.

cards.front: What four elements must a valid Fourth Amendment warrant have?
cards.back: (1) Probable cause; (2) supported by oath or affirmation; (3) presented to a neutral and detached magistrate; and (4) particularly describing the place to be searched and the persons or things to be seized.

cards.front: A valid warrant needs probable cause, supported by {{c1::oath or affirmation}}, presented to a {{c2::neutral and detached magistrate}}, and {{c3::particularly describing}} the place and the persons/things to be seized.
cards.back: oath or affirmation; neutral and detached magistrate; particularly describing

cards.front: Johnson v. United States (1948) — who must draw the probable-cause inferences?
cards.back: A neutral and detached magistrate, not the officer engaged in the often competitive enterprise of ferreting out crime.

cards.front: Johnson v. United States (1948) — quote the rule on who decides probable cause.
cards.back: Its protection consists in requiring "that those inferences be drawn by a neutral and detached magistrate instead of being judged by the officer engaged in the often competitive enterprise of ferreting out crime."
cards.cite: Johnson v. United States, 333 U.S. at 13-14

cards.front: Why does the warrant requirement put the probable-cause inference in a magistrate's hands (the Johnson principle)?
cards.back: Because the officer hunting for crime is not detached; the Fourth Amendment's protection lies in having a neutral decisionmaker, not the investigator, draw the inference of probable cause.

cards.front: Illinois v. Gates (1983) — what standard does the magistrate use to assess probable cause from an informant's tip?
cards.back: The totality of the circumstances — a practical, common-sense judgment of whether there is a fair probability of crime.

cards.front: Under Illinois v. Gates (1983), what is the role of an informant's veracity, reliability, and basis of knowledge?
cards.back: They are relevant factors that inform the totality judgment — not rigid, independent, dispositive hurdles.

cards.front: Franks v. Delaware (1978) — when must a warrant be voided and the fruits excluded?
cards.back: When a knowing/intentional or reckless material falsehood, necessary to probable cause, is proven by a preponderance and the affidavit's remaining content is then insufficient to establish probable cause.

cards.front: Franks v. Delaware (1978) — what must a defendant show to get a hearing on a false affidavit?
cards.back: A substantial preliminary showing that a false statement was made knowingly/intentionally, or with reckless disregard for the truth, and that the statement is necessary to the finding of probable cause.

cards.front: Franks v. Delaware (1978) — quote what happens to a warrant once the falsity is proven and the remaining affidavit is insufficient.
cards.back: "[T]he search warrant must be voided and the fruits of the search excluded to the same extent as if probable cause was lacking on the face of the affidavit."

cards.front: How does Franks v. Delaware relate to Leon's good-faith exception?
cards.back: Franks is the first hole in Leon's good-faith shield: good faith does not apply where the magistrate was misled by an affidavit the affiant knew was false or recklessly disregarded the truth.

cards.front: Maryland v. Garrison (1987) — on what information is a warrant's validity judged?
cards.back: On the information reasonably available to officers when they sought the warrant; a reasonable, good-faith mistake about the premises does not invalidate it.

cards.front: Officers got a warrant believing one apartment occupied the whole third floor, but it actually held two units; the search continued until the mistake became apparent. Which case, and what result?
cards.back: Maryland v. Garrison (1987) — the reasonable, good-faith mistake about the premises did not void the warrant or the search conducted before the error became apparent.

cards.front: Groh v. Ramirez (2004) — is a warrant that fails to describe the things to be seized valid if the supporting affidavit is particular?
cards.back: No — such a warrant is facially invalid under the Particularity Clause, and a particular affidavit cannot rescue it.

cards.front: Groh v. Ramirez teaches that particularity must live on the {{c1::face of the warrant}} itself — the {{c2::affidavit}} cannot cure a blank warrant.
cards.back: face of the warrant; affidavit

cards.front: Andresen v. Maryland (1976) — does a particularized warrant to seize a person's business records and use them in evidence violate the Fifth Amendment?
cards.back: No — there is no Fifth Amendment compulsion problem because the accused is not compelled to do anything; nothing is extracted from him.

cards.front: Andresen v. Maryland (1976) — quote the holding on searching/seizing business records and the Fifth Amendment.
cards.back: "[T]he search of an individual's office for business records, their seizure, and subsequent introduction into evidence do not offend the Fifth Amendment's proscription that '[n]o person . . . shall be compelled in any criminal case to be a witness against himself.'"

cards.front: Wilson v. Arkansas (1995) — how does knock-and-announce fit the Fourth Amendment?
cards.back: The common-law knock-and-announce principle is part of the Fourth Amendment reasonableness inquiry, but it is not a rigid rule and yields to countervailing law-enforcement interests.

cards.front: Richards v. Wisconsin (1997) — is there a blanket no-knock exception for felony drug cases?
cards.back: No — there is no blanket exception by crime category; the no-knock decision is made case-by-case.

cards.front: Richards v. Wisconsin (1997) — what must officers have to justify a no-knock entry, and on what grounds?
cards.back: Reasonable suspicion that knocking would be dangerous, futile, or would allow the destruction of evidence.

cards.front: Distinguish what Wilson v. Arkansas established about knock-and-announce from what Richards v. Wisconsin added.
cards.back: Wilson made knock-and-announce part of the reasonableness inquiry (subject to law-enforcement exceptions); Richards held those exceptions cannot be applied as a blanket category — a no-knock entry needs case-specific reasonable suspicion.

cards.front: Hudson v. Michigan (2006) — does a knock-and-announce violation require suppression of the evidence found inside?
cards.back: No — the exclusionary rule does not apply to knock-and-announce violations; the interests it protects have nothing to do with the seizure of evidence.

cards.front: Common pitfall: instructors assume a knock-and-announce violation suppresses the evidence. What is the correct rule (Hudson)?
cards.back: It does not suppress evidence (Hudson v. Michigan). The entry may be unlawful for civil purposes while the seized evidence still comes in; the remedy is civil, not exclusionary.

cards.front: United States v. Grubbs (2006) — are anticipatory warrants (effective only on a future triggering condition) constitutional?
cards.back: Yes — anticipatory warrants are not categorically unconstitutional.

cards.front: United States v. Grubbs (2006) — what two things must a magistrate find it presently probable will be true for an anticipatory warrant?
cards.back: (1) The triggering condition will occur; and (2) once it does, contraband will be at the place to be searched.

cards.front: Lo-Ji Sales, Inc. v. New York (1979) — why was the search invalid when the issuing magistrate led it?
cards.back: An open-ended warrant executed by a magistrate who joined and led the search becomes a forbidden general warrant, and the magistrate loses his neutral-and-detached character.
cards.cite: Lo-Ji Sales, Inc. v. New York, 442 U.S. 319 (1979)

cards.front: Lo-Ji Sales, Inc. v. New York (1979) — quote how the Court described the issuing judge's loss of neutrality.
cards.back: "He allowed himself to become a member, if not the leader, of the search party which was essentially a police operation. ... he was not acting as a judicial officer but as an adjunct law enforcement officer."
cards.cite: Lo-Ji Sales, Inc. v. New York, 442 U.S. at 327

cards.front: Coolidge v. New Hampshire (1971) — why was a warrant issued by the State Attorney General invalid?
cards.back: Because as the chief investigator/prosecutor he was not neutral and detached; the issuing official must be detached from the investigation.

cards.front: Lo-Ji and Coolidge each show a magistrate failing the neutral-and-detached test — how do the two failures differ?
cards.back: In Lo-Ji, the issuing judge lost neutrality by joining and leading the search itself; in Coolidge, the issuing official (the State Attorney General) was never neutral because he was the chief investigator/prosecutor.

cards.front: United States v. Leon (1984) — can a facially deficient warrant still support admissible evidence?
cards.back: Yes — evidence obtained in objectively reasonable good-faith reliance on a warrant may be admissible even if the warrant is later found deficient, because suppression would not serve the exclusionary rule's deterrent purpose.

cards.front: United States v. Leon (1984) — name the warrant-defects in this page that defeat good-faith reliance.
cards.back: Good faith does not apply where the magistrate was misled by a knowing/reckless false affidavit (Franks), where the magistrate wholly abandoned the neutral role (Lo-Ji), or where the warrant is so facially deficient in particularity that officers cannot reasonably presume it valid (cf. Groh).
cards.cite: United States v. Leon, 468 U.S. at 923

cards.front: Particularity limits what officers may seize on sight under a warrant — how do items not described in the warrant come in, if at all?
cards.back: Only under the Plain View Doctrine; the warrant's particularity clause does not authorize seizing items it does not describe.

cards.front: Name the three ways a warrant fails when one of its elements collapses, with the anchoring case for each.
cards.back: A falsehood corrupts the probable-cause showing (Franks); the magistrate abandons neutrality (Lo-Ji / Coolidge); or the description is open-ended or absent (Groh).
